topics.title: Air Pollution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT832 - ENVIRONMENTAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Air Pollution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Air Pollution

**Air Pollution** is defined as the presence of one or more contaminants (gaseous, liquid, or solid substances) in the atmosphere in quantities, characteristics, and duration such as to be injurious to human, plant, or animal life, or to property, or which unreasonably interfere with the comfortable enjoyment of life or property or the conduct of business.

According to the **Indian National Ambient Air Quality Standard (NAAQS)** under the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)**, ambient air refers to the outside air in the troposphere at locations accessible to the general public, typically up to a height of about **10 meters** above ground level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Definition:** Air pollution is not merely the presence of a substance, but its presence *above threshold concentration* and *for a measurable duration* — a concept central to every instrumentation problem.

### 1.2 Classification of Air Pollutants

Air pollutants are broadly classified as follows:

| Category | Description | Examples |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| **Primary Pollutants** | Emitted directly into the atmosphere from a source | CO, SO₂, NOₓ, Particulate Matter (PM), VOCs |
| **Secondary Pollutants** | Formed in the atmosphere by chemical reactions between primary pollutants and natural substances | Ozone (O₃), Smog, Acid Rain (H₂SO₄, HNO₃), Peroxyacetyl Nitrate (PAN) |
| **Criteria Pollutants** | Regulated by NAAQS / EPA based on health impact | CO, Pb, NO₂, O₃, PM, SO₂ |
| **Hazardous Air Pollutants (HAPs)** | Carcinogens / toxicants with no safe threshold | Benzene, Asbestos, Mercury, Dioxins |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Dirty Cup of Tea":**
> Imagine a clean cup of tea (clean air) is a transparent, pleasant liquid. If you stir in dust, sugar granules, and a few drops of ink (pollutants), the cup's transparency reduces, taste alters, and you may not want to drink it. Similarly, the atmosphere is like an enormous "cup" that gets contaminated by smoke (particles), invisible gases (CO, SO₂), and reactive chemicals (NOₓ). The **concentration** of these contaminants determines whether the air is "drinkable" for our lungs, just as a teaspoon of sugar is fine in tea, but a cup of sugar ruins it.
>
> **For instrumentation:** Just as you would measure sugar using a refractometer, we measure pollutants using analyzers, spectrophotometers, and PM samplers — quantifying *how dirty the cup has become*.

### 1.4 Major Sources of Air Pollution

- **Natural Sources:** Volcanic eruptions (SO₂, ash), Dust storms (PM₁₀/PM₂.₅), Forest fires (CO, PM), Biological decay (CH₄, H₂S), Sea salt spray (NaCl aerosols).
- **Anthropogenic (Man-made) Sources:**
  - *Stationary:* Power plants, refineries, factories, incinerators.
  - *Mobile:* Automobiles, aircraft, ships.
  - *Area:* Agricultural burning, landfills, construction.
  - *Indoor:* Cooking (especially biomass), tobacco smoke, building materials (formaldehyde, radon).

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard Reference Conditions in air pollution instrumentation:
> - **Temperature (T₀):** 25 °C = **298.15 K**
> - **Pressure (P₀):** **1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 101325 Pa**
> - **Molar Volume (Vm):** **24.45 L/mol** at 25 °C, 1 atm
> - **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - **Unit of pollutant concentration in ambient air:** **µg/m³** (mass per volume) and **ppb / ppm** (molar mixing ratio).
> - **Conversion factor:** 1 ppm × MW / 24.45 = mg/m³ (at 25 °C, 1 atm)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Troposphere and the Atmospheric Window for Pollution

Most anthropogenic air pollution occurs in the **troposphere** (0–10 km altitude). The **mixing height** and **atmospheric stability** determine how pollutants disperse.

### 2.2 Concentration Units — The Instrumentation Core

Instruments report pollutant concentrations in two systems, and conversion is a **high-frequency KTU exam question**.

#### 2.2.1 Mass Concentration (ρ)
$$\rho \;\; \left[\frac{\mu g}{m^3}\right]$$

#### 2.2.2 Volume Mixing Ratio (C in ppm or ppb)
$$C \; [\text{ppm}] = \frac{V_{\text{pollutant}}}{V_{\text{air}}} \times 10^6$$

#### 2.2.3 Conversion (Ideal Gas Law approach)
Using the ideal gas law, $PV = nRT$, both gases (pollutant + air) at same T and P, we have:
$$\frac{n_{\text{pollutant}}}{V} = \frac{P \cdot C_{\text{ppm}} \times 10^{-6}}{RT}$$

Multiplying by molecular weight $MW$:
$$\rho \;\; \left[\frac{\mu g}{m^3}\right] = C \; [\text{ppm}] \times MW \times \frac{P}{RT} \times 10^3$$

At **25 °C, 1 atm**, this simplifies to:
$$\rho \;\; \left[\frac{\mu g}{m^3}\right] = C \; [\text{ppm}] \times MW \times 40.9$$

Or equivalently:
$$C \; [\text{ppm}] = \frac{\rho \;\; [\mu g/m^3]}{MW \times 40.9}$$

### 2.3 Particulate Matter (PM) Classification

| Designation | Diameter Range | Penetration Depth | Major Source |
|-------------|---------------|-------------------|--------------|
| **PM₁₀** (Thoracic) | ≤ 10 µm | Reaches bronchi | Dust, pollen |
| **PM₂.₅** (Respirable) | ≤ 2.5 µm | Reaches alveoli (deep lung) | Combustion, secondary aerosols |
| **Ultrafine (UFP)** | ≤ 0.1 µm | Crosses into bloodstream | Vehicular exhaust, industrial |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pearl:** PM₂.₅ is more dangerous per unit mass than PM₁₀ because of its larger surface-area-to-volume ratio (adsorbs more toxins) and deeper lung penetration.

### 2.4 Atmospheric Stability & Lapse Rate

The **environmental lapse rate (ELR)** governs vertical mixing. Compare with the **adiabatic lapse rate**:
- **Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR):** 9.8 K/km
- **Saturated Adiabatic Lapse Rate (SALR):** ~6 K/km (varies with T)

**Stability conditions:**
- ELR > DALR → **Unstable** (lofting, rapid dispersion)
- ELR = DALR → **Neutral**
- ELR < DALR → **Stable** (trapping, fumigation)
- ELR < 0 (inversion) → **Highly Stable** (worst-case pollution episodes)

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $C_{\text{ppm}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{pollutant}}}{n_{\text{air}}} \times 10^{6}$ | Volume mixing ratio | ppm |
| 2 | $\rho = \dfrac{C_{\text{ppm}} \times MW \times P \times 10^{3}}{R \cdot T}$ | Mass concentration from ppm | µg/m³ |
| 3 | $C_{\text{ppm}} = \dfrac{\rho \times 24.45}{MW}$ | At 25 °C, 1 atm | ppm |
| 4 | $\text{AQI}_i = \dfrac{I_{\text{Hi}} - I_{\text{Lo}}}{C_{\text{Hi}} - C_{\text{Lo}}} \times (C_i - C_{\text{Lo}}) + I_{\text{Lo}}$ | Sub-index for pollutant *i* | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{AQI} = \max_i(\text{AQI}_i)$ | Overall AQI = dominant pollutant | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{DALR} = 9.8$ K/km | Dry adiabatic lapse rate | K/km |
| 7 | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{P} = 24.45$ L/mol at 25 °C | Molar volume | L/mol |
| 8 | $\text{Plume rise} \;\Delta h = \dfrac{1.6 \cdot F^{1/3} \cdot x^{2/3}}{u}$ | Holland's formula (Briggs-style) | m |
| 9 | $C(x,y,0) = \dfrac{Q}{\pi \cdot u \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} - \dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | Gaussian Plume (ground-level centerline, $z=0$) | µg/m³ |
| 10 | $\sigma_y = a \cdot x^b, \quad \sigma_z = c \cdot x^d + f$ | Pasquill-Gifford dispersion coefficients | m |

> [!NOTE]
> Formulas (9) and (10) form the **Gaussian Plume Model** — the most-tested analytical model in KTU ESE for air pollution dispersion.

### 2.6 Air Quality Index (AQI) — Indian CPCB Standard

AQI converts measured pollutant concentrations into a single 0–500 number. The **dominant pollutant** governs the index. Indian AQI bands:

| AQI | Category | Health Impact |
|-----|----------|--------------|
| 0–50 | Good | Minimal |
| 51–100 | Satisfactory | Minor breathing discomfort to sensitive groups |
| 101–200 | Moderate | Discomfort to people with respiratory disease |
| 201–300 | Poor | Discomfort to most people on prolonged exposure |
| 301–400 | Very Poor | Respiratory illness on prolonged exposure |
| 401–500 | Severe | Health impacts even on healthy people |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Eight parameters are used in Indian AQI:** PM₂.₅, PM₁₀, NO₂, SO₂, CO, O₃, NH₃, and Pb.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power plant siting:** Gaussian plume model decides minimum stack height and emission limits.
- **Urban planning:** AQI-based traffic regulation (odd-even schemes in Delhi, LEZs in Europe).
- **Industrial process control:** Continuous Emission Monitoring Systems (CEMS) feedback into scrubber/ESP control loops.
- **Public health advisories:** Smartphone apps (e.g., CPCB's "Sameer" app) display real-time AQI.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Conversion from ppm to µg/m³

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **single most-asked calculation in KTU Environmental Instrumentation papers**.

**Statement:** Convert $C_{\text{ppm}}$ (parts per million by volume) of a gas into mass concentration $\rho$ in µg/m³ at standard conditions (T = 25 °C, P = 1 atm).

**Step 1: Definition of ppm in mole/volume terms.**
For an ideal gas mixture at uniform T and P, equal volumes imply equal moles (Avogadro's law). Therefore, the volume ratio equals the mole ratio:
$$C_{\text{ppm}} = \frac{n_{\text{pollutant}}}{n_{\text{air}}} \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 2: Relate moles to volume using the ideal gas law.**
$$PV = nRT \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; n = \frac{PV}{RT}$$

So the mole ratio is also the volume ratio:
$$C_{\text{ppm}} = \frac{V_{\text{pollutant}}}{V_{\text{air}}} \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 3: Express mass concentration.**
Mass of pollutant = moles × molecular weight:
$$m_{\text{pollutant}} = n_{\text{pollutant}} \times MW$$

The mass per unit volume of air:
$$\rho = \frac{m_{\text{pollutant}}}{V_{\text{air}}} = \frac{n_{\text{pollutant}} \times MW}{V_{\text{air}}}$$

**Step 4: Combine.**
$$\rho = \frac{C_{\text{ppm}} \times 10^{-6} \times n_{\text{air}} \times MW}{V_{\text{air}}}$$

But $\dfrac{n_{\text{air}}}{V_{\text{air}}} = \dfrac{P}{RT}$ (from ideal gas law). Therefore:
$$\rho = C_{\text{ppm}} \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{P}{RT} \times MW$$

**Step 5: Insert units carefully.**
$P$ in Pa, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T$ in K, $MW$ in g/mol gives:
$$\rho \;\;[kg/m^3] = C_{\text{ppm}} \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{P}{RT} \times MW$$

Converting kg → µg (×10⁹) and g/mol → kg/mol (÷1000):
$$\rho \;\;[\mu g/m^3] = C_{\text{ppm}} \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{P}{RT} \times MW \times 10^6$$

Therefore the clean working equation:
$$\rho \;\; [\mu g/m^3] = C_{\text{ppm}} \times MW \times \frac{P}{RT}$$

**Step 6: Evaluate the constant at 25 °C, 1 atm.**
$P = 101325$ Pa, $T = 298.15$ K, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K):
$$\frac{P}{RT} = \frac{101325}{8.314 \times 298.15} = \frac{101325}{2478.82} = 40.876 \;\; \text{mol/m}^3$$

**Step 7: Final simplified formula (using MW in g/mol).**
$$\rho \;\; [\mu g/m^3] = C_{\text{ppm}} \times MW_{\text{[g/mol]}} \times 40.9$$

> [!NOTE]
> The "magic number" **40.9** is the conversion factor at 25 °C and 1 atm. Examiners expect students to derive, not memorize it.

### 3.2 Worked Example: KTU-Style Numerical

**Problem [Model 3-Mark]:** The concentration of SO₂ in ambient air is measured as **0.15 ppm** at 25 °C and 1 atm. Compute the mass concentration in µg/m³. Given MW(SO₂) = 64 g/mol.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the standard formula.
$$\rho = C_{\text{ppm}} \times MW \times 40.9$$

**Step 2:** Substitute known values.
$$\rho = 0.15 \times 64 \times 40.9$$

**Step 3:** Multiply stepwise.
$$\rho = 0.15 \times 64 = 9.6$$
$$9.6 \times 40.9 = 392.64$$

**Step 4:** Add units.
$$\boxed{\rho = 392.64 \;\; \mu g/m^3 \;\; (\text{SO}_2)}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: AQI Sub-Index (Linear Interpolation)

**Statement:** Given a measured concentration $C_i$ of pollutant *i*, find the AQI sub-index.

**Step 1: Identify the breakpoints.**
Indian CPCB provides a table where each pollutant has concentration ranges (C_Lo to C_Hi) corresponding to AQI ranges (I_Lo to I_Hi). For example, for PM₂.₅:
- 0–30 µg/m³ → AQI 0–50
- 30–60 µg/m³ → AQI 50–100
- 60–90 µg/m³ → AQI 100–200
- 90–120 µg/m³ → AQI 200–300
- 120–250 µg/m³ → AQI 300–400
- 250–380 µg/m³ → AQI 400–500

**Step 2: Identify the bracket containing $C_i$.**
Pick the breakpoints (C_Lo, C_Hi) such that $C_{\text{Lo}} \leq C_i \leq C_{\text{Hi}}$, with corresponding AQI values (I_Lo, I_Hi).

**Step 3: Apply linear interpolation.**
$$\text{AQI}_i = \frac{I_{\text{Hi}} - I_{\text{Lo}}}{C_{\text{Hi}} - C_{\text{Lo}}} \times (C_i - C_{\text{Lo}}) + I_{\text{Lo}}$$

**Step 4: Repeat for all measured pollutants, then take the maximum:**
$$\text{AQI} = \max(\text{AQI}_{\text{PM2.5}}, \text{AQI}_{\text{PM10}}, \text{AQI}_{\text{NO2}}, \dots)$$

### 3.4 Worked Example: AQI Calculation

**Problem [Model 3-Mark]:** Hourly averages: PM₂.₅ = 75 µg/m³, PM₁₀ = 130 µg/m³, NO₂ = 60 µg/m³. Find the AQI.

**Solution:**

**Sub-index for PM₂.₅ = 75 µg/m³:**
- Bracket: 60–90 → 100–200. So C_Lo=60, C_Hi=90, I_Lo=100, I_Hi=200.
$$\text{AQI}_{\text{PM2.5}} = \frac{200-100}{90-60} \times (75-60) + 100 = \frac{100}{30} \times 15 + 100 = 50 + 100 = 150$$

**Sub-index for PM₁₀ = 130 µg/m³:**
- Bracket: 100–250 → 200–300. C_Lo=100, C_Hi=250, I_Lo=200, I_Hi=300.
$$\text{AQI}_{\text{PM10}} = \frac{300-200}{250-100} \times (130-100) + 200 = \frac{100}{150} \times 30 + 200 = 20 + 200 = 220$$

**Sub-index for NO₂ = 60 µg/m³:**
- Bracket: 40–80 → 100–200. C_Lo=40, C_Hi=80, I_Lo=100, I_Hi=200.
$$\text{AQI}_{\text{NO2}} = \frac{200-100}{80-40} \times (60-40) + 100 = \frac{100}{40} \times 20 + 100 = 50 + 100 = 150$$

**Overall AQI:**
$$\text{AQI} = \max(150, 220, 150) = \boxed{220 \;\; (\text{PM}_{10} \;\text{is the dominant pollutant})}$$

### 3.5 Derivation: Gaussian Plume — Ground-Level Centerline Concentration

**Assumptions:** Continuous point source of strength $Q$ [g/s] at effective stack height $H$ [m]; wind speed $u$ [m/s] along x-axis; dispersion coefficients $\sigma_y(x), \sigma_z(x)$.

**Step 1: Steady-state mass balance.**
Pollutant emitted per second = $Q$. In steady wind, the plume advects along x and spreads in y and z. The concentration in the crosswind (y) and vertical (z) directions is assumed Gaussian (a consequence of the central limit theorem applied to turbulent eddies).

**Step 2: Crosswind and vertical Gaussian profiles.**
$$C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi \cdot u \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \cdot \left[\exp\!\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\!\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$$

The two exponential terms in $z$ correspond to the **direct plume** (centered at $H$) and its **image plume** (at $-H$, due to ground reflection — no net flux through ground).

**Step 3: At ground level (z = 0) and on the centerline (y = 0):**
$$C(x,0,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi \cdot u \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

**Step 4: The maximum ground-level concentration** occurs at the downwind distance where $\sigma_z(x) = H/\sqrt{2}$ — this gives the **critical distance** for worst-case exposure.

### 3.6 Worked Example: Gaussian Plume Concentration

**Problem:** A stack emits SO₂ at $Q = 80$ g/s. Effective height $H = 60$ m. Wind speed $u = 4$ m/s. At $x = 1$ km, $\sigma_y = 35$ m, $\sigma_z = 20$ m. Find ground-level centerline concentration.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Substitute into the ground-level centerline formula.
$$C = \frac{80}{\pi \times 4 \times 35 \times 20} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{60^2}{2 \times 20^2}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate denominator.
$$\pi \times 4 \times 35 \times 20 = \pi \times 2800 = 8796.46$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the exponential term.
$$-\frac{3600}{2 \times 400} = -\frac{3600}{800} = -4.5$$
$$\exp(-4.5) = 0.01111$$

**Step 4:** Multiply.
$$C = \frac{80}{8796.46} \times 0.01111 = 0.009094 \times 0.01111 = 1.01 \times 10^{-4} \;\; g/m^3$$

**Step 5:** Convert to µg/m³.
$$\boxed{C \approx 101 \;\; \mu g/m^3}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Air Pollution Conceptual Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Sources] --> A2[Primary Pollutants]
    A1 --> A3[Secondary Pollutants]
    A2 --> A4[Atmosphere Transport]
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5[Receptors]
    A5 --> A6[Human Health]
    A5 --> A7[Vegetation]
    A5 --> A8[Materials]
    A5 --> A9[Visibility Climate]
    subgraph Instrumentation
        I1[Sampling] --> I2[Analysis]
        I2 --> I3[Data Acquisition]
        I3 --> I4[AQI Computation]
    end
    A2 -. monitored by .-> I1
    A3 -. monitored by .-> I1
```

### 4.2 Pollutant Formation Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NOx[NOx] --> Ozone
    VOCs[VOCs] --> Ozone
    Ozone --> Smog[Photochemical Smog]
    SO2[SO2] --> H2SO4
    H2SO4 --> Acid[Acid Rain]
    NOx --> HNO3
    HNO3 --> Acid
    Primary[Primary Pollutants] --> Reactions
    Sunlight[UV Sunlight] --> Reactions[Atmospheric Reactions]
    Reactions --> Secondary[Secondary Pollutants]
```

### 4.3 Gaussian Plume Dispersion Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Stack Source Q g per s] --> R[Plume Rise to Height H]
    R --> T[Turbulent Diffusion]
    T --> Sy[Sigma y Lateral Spread]
    T --> Sz[Sigma z Vertical Spread]
    Sy --> G[Ground Level Centerline]
    Sz --> G
    G --> M[Maximum Concentration Cmax]
    M --> Receptors[Human and Ecological Receptors]
```

### 4.4 AQI Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[PM2.5] --> Sub1[Sub Index AQI PM2.5]
    P2[PM10] --> Sub2[Sub Index AQI PM10]
    P3[NO2] --> Sub3[Sub Index AQI NO2]
    P4[SO2] --> Sub4[Sub Index AQI SO2]
    P5[CO] --> Sub5[Sub Index AQI CO]
    P6[O3] --> Sub6[Sub Index AQI O3]
    Sub1 --> MAX[Maximum Selection]
    Sub2 --> MAX
    Sub3 --> MAX
    Sub4 --> MAX
    Sub5 --> MAX
    Sub6 --> MAX
    MAX --> Final[Final AQI Value 0 to 500]
```

### 4.5 Measurement Instrumentation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ambient Air] --> B[Inlet and Particulate Separator]
    B --> C[Conditioner Drying and Filtering]
    C --> D[Analyzer Principle]
    D --> D1[NDIR for CO and CO2]
    D --> D2[UV Fluorescence for SO2]
    D --> D3[Chemiluminescence for NOx and O3]
    D --> D4[Gravimetric Beta Attenuation for PM]
    D1 --> E[Signal Processing]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Data Logger and Telemetry]
    F --> G[Central Monitoring Station]
    G --> H[AQI Dissemination]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model] Define primary and secondary air pollutants. Give two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Primary Pollutants:** Substances emitted directly into the atmosphere from identifiable sources in a form harmful to health or environment.
  - *Examples:* Carbon monoxide (CO) from vehicle exhaust; Sulphur dioxide (SO₂) from coal-fired power plants; Particulate matter (PM) from construction/dust.
- **Secondary Pollutants:** Substances formed in the atmosphere by chemical reactions between primary pollutants and natural atmospheric constituents (often driven by sunlight).
  - *Examples:* Ground-level ozone (O₃) formed by photolysis of NO₂ in presence of VOCs; Sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄) formed by oxidation of SO₂ — a major component of acid rain; Peroxyacetyl nitrate (PAN) — a photochemical smog component.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Definition 1 + Example 1 + Definition 0.5 + Example 0.5.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] Convert 0.20 ppm of NO₂ into µg/m³ at 25 °C and 1 atm. (MW of NO₂ = 46 g/mol)**

**Model Answer:**

Using the standard formula:
$$\rho \;[\mu g/m^3] = C_{\text{ppm}} \times MW \times 40.9$$

**Step 1:** Substitute values.
$$\rho = 0.20 \times 46 \times 40.9$$

**Step 2:** Multiply.
$$0.20 \times 46 = 9.2$$
$$9.2 \times 40.9 = 376.28$$

**Step 3:** Final Answer.
$$\boxed{\rho = 376.28 \;\; \mu g/m^3}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A: 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Discuss the major sources, classification, and effects of air pollutants on human health, vegetation, and materials. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **High-Volume Air Sampler (HVAS)** for measurement of Total Suspended Particulates (TSP). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q A (a):**

**1. Sources (2 marks):**
- **Natural sources:** Volcanic eruptions (SO₂, ash), forest fires, dust storms (PM₁₀/PM₂.₅), biological decay (CH₄, H₂S), sea-salt aerosols.
- **Anthropogenic sources:** Stationary (thermal power plants, refineries), Mobile (automobiles, aircraft), Area (agricultural residue burning, construction), Indoor (biomass cooking, tobacco smoke).

**2. Classification (2 marks):**
- **Primary vs Secondary** (as defined above)
- **Criteria Pollutants vs Hazardous Air Pollutants (HAPs)** — regulated by EPA / CPCB.
- **By physical state:** Gaseous (SO₂, NO₂, CO) and Particulate (PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, smoke).
- **By origin:** Indoor vs Outdoor.

**3. Effects (3 marks):**
- **Human health:** CO binds to haemoglobin → carboxyhaemoglobin → reduced oxygen carrying capacity (headache, death at >50%). SO₂ causes bronchitis, asthma. NO₂ → respiratory infections, smog. PM₂.₅ penetrates alveoli → lung cancer, cardiovascular mortality. O₃ → chest pain, throat irritation. Lead → neurotoxicity in children.
- **Vegetation:** SO₂ + NOₓ → acid rain damages leaf cuticle, leaches nutrients, acidifies soil. O₃ damages chloroplasts, reduces photosynthesis. PAN causes silvering of leafy vegetables.
- **Materials:** SO₂ + moisture → sulphuric acid → corrosion of metals and marble (Taj Mahal yellowing). Ozone cracks rubber and degrades textiles. Particulate soiling of buildings and monuments.

---

**Model Solution for Q A (b):**

**High-Volume Air Sampler (HVAS) — Working Principle (7 marks):**

**Block diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ambient Air Inlet at Roof Level] --> B[PM10 or PM2.5 Inlet Cyclone or Impactor]
    B --> C[Filter Holder with Pre weighed Glass Fibre or Quartz Filter]
    C --> D[High Volume Blower Motor]
    D --> E[Flow Recorder and Orifice]
    E --> F[Exhaust]
    C -. retained PM .-> G[Weighing Balance in Lab]
    G --> H[Mass Concentration Calculation]
```

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Ambient air is drawn into the sampler via a sheltered inlet placed ~10–15 m above ground.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** A size-selective inlet (cyclone for PM₁₀ or impactor for PM₂.₅) removes particles larger than the target cut-size. The remaining particles proceed to the filter.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Particles are collected on a pre-weighed glass-fibre or quartz filter housed in a filter holder. The blower maintains a calibrated flow rate of typically **1.0 to 1.5 m³/min** for 24 hours.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** A flow recorder (orifice + manometer or mass flow controller) logs the average flow rate.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** After 24 hours, the filter is removed, conditioned (in a desiccator at controlled humidity for 24 h), and reweighed on an analytical balance. The mass of PM collected is the difference.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Mass concentration is calculated as:
$$C = \frac{W_{\text{final}} - W_{\text{initial}}}{V_{\text{air sampled}}} \;\; [\mu g/m^3]$$
where $V_{\text{air}} = \text{average flow} \times \text{sampling time}$.

---

#### **Question B: 14 Marks [Alternative Choice – Model 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between mass concentration (µg/m³) and volume mixing ratio (ppm) for an air pollutant. State the constant used at 25 °C and 1 atm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following are 24-hour average concentrations measured at a station: PM₂.₅ = 55 µg/m³, PM₁₀ = 90 µg/m³, NO₂ = 55 µg/m³, SO₂ = 30 µg/m³. Determine the AQI. Use CPCB breakpoints. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q B (a):**

Refer to the full derivation in **Section 3.1** above. Key steps the student should explicitly write:

1. Define ppm: $C_{\text{ppm}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{pollutant}}}{n_{\text{air}}} \times 10^{6}$ — **1 mark**
2. Apply ideal gas law to express mole ratio as volume ratio: $PV = nRT$ — **1 mark**
3. Express mass = moles × MW: $m = n \cdot MW$ — **1 mark**
4. Derive: $\rho = C_{\text{ppm}} \times MW \times \dfrac{P}{RT}$ — **2 marks**
5. Plug in T = 298.15 K, P = 101325 Pa, R = 8.314 → constant **40.9** — **1 mark**
6. Final boxed equation:
   $$\rho \;[\mu g/m^3] = C_{\text{ppm}} \times MW_{\text{[g/mol]}} \times 40.9$$ — **1 mark**

---

**Model Solution for Q B (b):**

Using CPCB AQI breakpoints (24-hr average values):

| Pollutant | Conc (µg/m³) | C_Lo | C_Hi | I_Lo | I_Hi | AQI_sub |
|-----------|--------------|------|------|------|------|---------|
| PM₂.₅ | 55 | 30 | 60 | 50 | 100 | **83.33** |
| PM₁₀ | 90 | 50 | 100 | 50 | 100 | **80.00** |
| NO₂ | 55 | 40 | 80 | 50 | 100 | **68.75** |
| SO₂ | 30 | 30 | 60 | 50 | 100 | **50.00** |

**Step 1 (PM₂.₅):** 55 falls in 30–60 → AQI 50–100.
$$\text{AQI}_{\text{PM2.5}} = \frac{100-50}{60-30} \times (55-30) + 50 = \frac{50}{30} \times 25 + 50 = 41.67 + 50 = 91.67 \;\; \text{(rounded to 92)}$$

**Step 2 (PM₁₀):** 90 falls in 50–100 → AQI 50–100.
$$\text{AQI}_{\text{PM10}} = \frac{100-50}{100-50} \times (90-50) + 50 = 1 \times 40 + 50 = 90$$

**Step 3 (NO₂):** 55 falls in 40–80 → AQI 50–100.
$$\text{AQI}_{\text{NO2}} = \frac{100-50}{80-40} \times (55-40) + 50 = \frac{50}{40} \times 15 + 50 = 18.75 + 50 = 68.75 \;\; \text{(rounded to 69)}$$

**Step 4 (SO₂):** 30 falls in 30–60 → AQI 50–100.
$$\text{AQI}_{\text{SO2}} = \frac{100-50}{60-30} \times (30-30) + 50 = 0 + 50 = 50$$

**Step 5 (Overall AQI):**
$$\text{AQI} = \max(92, 90, 69, 50) = \boxed{92 \;\; (\text{PM}_{2.5} \text{ is the dominant pollutant})}$$

**Category:** Satisfactory (AQI 51–100) → Minor breathing discomfort to sensitive groups.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mix-up:** Students often write the ppm-to-µg/m³ formula with the wrong constant (40.9 is at 25 °C; for 0 °C, it is 44.6). Always **state the reference temperature explicitly**.
> 2. **MW confusion:** A common error is using molecular weight of N (14) instead of NO₂ (46). Always include both atoms.
> 3. **AQI breakpoints memorised incorrectly:** Students often confuse PM₂.₅ and PM₁₀ breakpoint tables. Memorise the **eight-pollutant sub-table** of CPCB AQI.
> 4. **AQI = average** — WRONG. AQI is the **maximum** of all sub-indices. The dominant pollutant is the answer's "key."
> 5. **For HVAS:** Forgetting to mention the **24-hour sampling** and the **desiccation step** before weighing — a 0.5–1 mark deduction.
> 6. **Gaussian plume:** Failing to mention the **two-image-source assumption** (ground reflection) — costs conceptual marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Air Pollution Definition:** Presence of contaminants above threshold, over duration, with adverse effect.
- **Two broad classes:** **Primary** (direct emission) vs **Secondary** (formed in atmosphere).
- **Criteria Pollutants (Indian AQI):** PM₂.₅, PM₁₀, NO₂, SO₂, CO, O₃, NH₃, Pb.
- **Conversion formula (25 °C, 1 atm):** $\rho \;[\mu g/m^3] = C_{\text{ppm}} \times MW \times 40.9$
- **Magic number 40.9** comes from $P/(RT)$ with $P = 101325$ Pa, $T = 298.15$ K, $R = 8.314$.
- **AQI = max of sub-indices**, NOT average. **Linear interpolation formula** mandatory in derivations.
- **PM₂.₅** is the most dangerous inhalable pollutant; enters alveoli, adsorbs toxins.
- **Atmospheric stability:** DALR = 9.8 K/km. **Inversions** cause worst-case pollution episodes.
- **Gaussian Plume ground-level centerline:**
  $$C = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$
- **Sources remember:** 4 categories — Stationary, Mobile, Area, Indoor.
- **Effects remember:** Health + Vegetation + Materials + Visibility + Climate.
- **Instrumentation for ambient air:**
  - *Gases:* NDIR (CO), UV fluorescence (SO₂), Chemiluminescence (NO/NO₂/O₃), Flame Ionization Detection (VOCs).
  - *Particulates:* HVAS (gravimetric), Beta Attenuation Mass Monitor (real-time BAM), Optical Particle Counter (OPC), TEOM (Tapered Element Oscillating Microbalance).
- **CPCB NAAQS** specifies 24-hour / 8-hour / annual averages for each criterion pollutant — important for instrumentation sampling strategy.
- **Indoor air pollutants** (formaldehyde, radon, CO) are typically 2–5× more concentrated than outdoor → indoor air quality (IAQ) is an active research area.
- **Photochemical smog** forms when NOₓ + VOCs + sunlight react → O₃ + PAN; classic in cities like Los Angeles, Delhi, Beijing.
- **Acid rain** threshold: pH < 5.6; mainly from SO₂ and NOₓ emissions; damages aquatic life in lakes.
- **Carbon dioxide** is a pollutant from climate-change perspective (CO₂ eq. warming) but is not a NAAQS "criteria pollutant" in India.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Pollution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT832 - ENVIRONMENTAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Water Pollution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Water Pollution

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Water Pollution** is defined as the introduction of biological, chemical, physical, or radiological contaminants into a water body (such as a river, lake, ocean, aquifer, or reservoir) in concentrations that degrade the natural water quality and render it unfit for its designated beneficial use (drinking, irrigation, aquaculture, recreation, or industrial cooling).

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, water pollution is studied under the broader umbrella of **Environmental Instrumentation** as a precursor to understanding the **sensors, analyzers, and signal conditioning circuits** used to monitor pollutant parameters such as **pH, Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD), Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD), Total Dissolved Solids (TDS), Dissolved Oxygen (DO)**, and turbidity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Water Pollution):**
> The module covers the sources, types, and effects of water pollution, the major physico-chemical and biological parameters of water quality, and the principle of measurement for each parameter using appropriate instrumentation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

Imagine a glass of clean drinking water as a perfectly organized queue of H₂O molecules "holding hands" and dissolving just the right amount of air (oxygen) for a fish to breathe. Now imagine someone dumps ink, soap, factory acid, and rotten food into that glass.

- The **ink** changes the color (aesthetic pollution).
- The **soap** cuts surface tension and suffocates fish (surfactant pollution).
- The **acid** changes the pH and corrodes the fish's gills (chemical pollution).
- The **rotten food** becomes food for bacteria, which multiply and consume all the dissolved oxygen, leaving the fish to **choke**.

This is precisely how real water bodies get polluted. The key instrument design challenge in Environmental Instrumentation is: **"How do we measure these invisible changes precisely using electrical/electronic transducers?"**

## 1.3 Classification of Water Pollutants

| Pollutant Category | Examples | Primary Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Pathogens** | *E. coli, Salmonella, Vibrio cholerae* | Waterborne diseases (typhoid, cholera) |
| **Organic Pollutants** | Sewage, food waste, oils | Deplete DO via bacterial decomposition |
| **Inorganic Chemicals** | Heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd), nitrates, fluorides | Toxicity, bioaccumulation |
| **Nutrients** | Phosphates, nitrates from fertilizers | Eutrophication & algal blooms |
| **Suspended Solids** | Soil, silt, plastics | Turbidity increase, light blockage |
| **Thermal Pollutants** | Hot water from power plant cooling | Reduces DO solubility in water |
| **Radioactive** | Cs-137, Sr-90 from nuclear waste | Long-term carcinogenic effects |

## 1.4 Key Water Quality Parameters (KTU High-Frequency Terms)

> [!NOTE]
> These six parameters form the **backbone of KTU exam questions** on water pollution instrumentation.

1. **pH** – A measure of hydrogen ion activity, defining the acidic/alkaline nature of water. Standard range: **6.5 – 8.5**.
2. **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)** – mg of O₂ consumed by microbes to oxidize organic matter in 5 days at 20 °C. Unit: **mg/L or ppm**.
3. **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)** – mg of O₂ consumed to chemically oxidize both biodegradable and non-biodegradable organics. Unit: **mg/L**.
4. **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)** – Sum of all inorganic salts dissolved. Measured in **mg/L** or **ppm**.
5. **Turbidity** – Optical property measuring light scattering by suspended particles. Measured in **NTU (Nephelometric Turbidity Units)**.
6. **Dissolved Oxygen (DO)** – Amount of molecular oxygen dissolved in water. Healthy water: **≥ 5 mg/L**; fish kill: **< 3 mg/L**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 BOD Kinetics – The "Why" and "How"

The BOD reaction is **first-order with respect to the remaining organic matter** $L$. Microbes consume the oxygen at a rate proportional to how much organic food is left.

### 2.1.1 The Differential Form

The rate of oxygen consumption is proportional to the remaining oxidizable matter:

$$\frac{dL}{dt} = -k \cdot L$$

where:
- $L$ = remaining organic matter (mg/L) at time $t$
- $k$ = deoxygenation rate constant (day⁻¹), typically **0.1 – 0.3 per day at 20 °C**

### 2.1.2 The Integrated Form (BOD at any time t)

Integrating from $L_0$ (initial organic matter) to $L_t$ at time $t$:

$$L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$$

### 2.1.3 The Amount of Oxygen Consumed – $BOD_t$

Since the oxygen consumed at time $t$ equals the organic matter destroyed, $BOD_t = L_0 - L_t$:

$$BOD_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k \cdot t}\right)$$

The standard 5-day BOD is:

$$BOD_5 = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-5k}\right)$$

The **ultimate BOD** ($L_0$) is recovered when $t \to \infty$:

$$BOD_\infty = L_0$$

### 2.1.4 Temperature Correction for $k$

The rate constant $k$ is strongly temperature dependent. The empirical correction is:

$$k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T - 20)}$$

where $\theta \approx 1.047$ for typical domestic sewage.

## 2.2 Streeter–Phelps DO Sag Equation

When organic waste is discharged into a river, a **deoxygenation curve** (DO drops) and a **reaeration curve** (DO rises from atmosphere) occur simultaneously. The net DO deficit $D_t$ is:

$$D_t = \frac{k \cdot L_0}{k_2 - k} \left( e^{-k \cdot t} - e^{-k_2 \cdot t} \right) + D_0 \cdot e^{-k_2 \cdot t}$$

where:
- $D_t$ = DO deficit at time $t$ (mg/L) = $DO_{sat} - DO_{actual}$
- $k$ = deoxygenation constant (day⁻¹)
- $k_2$ = reaeration constant (day⁻¹)
- $D_0$ = initial DO deficit at discharge point (mg/L)
- $L_0$ = ultimate BOD (mg/L)

## 2.3 Hardness of Water

**Hardness** is caused by dissolved divalent cations, mainly **Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺**. Expressed as equivalent CaCO₃.

$$\text{Total Hardness (as CaCO}_3\text{)} = 2.5 \times [\text{Ca}^{2+}] + 4.1 \times [\text{Mg}^{2+}]$$

where concentrations of Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ are in **mg/L**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversions used:** 1 meq/L of Ca²⁺ = 50 mg/L as CaCO₃; 1 meq/L of Mg²⁺ = 50 mg/L as CaCO₃.

## 2.4 DO Saturation as a Function of Temperature

The standard empirical table values for DO saturation at sea level (1 atm, fresh water) are critical for the Streeter–Phelps equation:

$$DO_{sat}(T) = 14.62 - 0.3898T + 0.006969T^2 - 0.00005896T^3$$

(Valid 0–40 °C, fresh water, in mg/L.)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Mandatory Table)

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $BOD_t = L_0(1 - e^{-kt})$ | First-order BOD kinetics | mg/L |
| 2 | $BOD_5 = L_0(1 - e^{-5k})$ | Standard 5-day BOD | mg/L |
| 3 | $L_0 = \frac{BOD_5}{1 - e^{-5k}}$ | Ultimate BOD from 5-day test | mg/L |
| 4 | $k_T = k_{20}\,\theta^{(T-20)}$ | Temperature-corrected rate constant | day⁻¹ |
| 5 | $D_t = \frac{kL_0}{k_2 - k}\left(e^{-kt} - e^{-k_2 t}\right) + D_0 e^{-k_2 t}$ | Streeter–Phelps DO sag | mg/L |
| 6 | $DO_{sat}(T) = 14.62 - 0.3898T + 0.00697T^2 - 0.0000590T^3$ | DO saturation vs. temperature | mg/L |
| 7 | $\text{TH} = 2.5[\text{Ca}^{2+}] + 4.1[\text{Mg}^{2+}]$ | Total Hardness as CaCO₃ | mg/L |
| 8 | $\text{BOD/COD Ratio} = \frac{BOD_5}{COD}$ | Biodegradability index | dimensionless |
| 9 | $L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k t}$ | Remaining organic load | mg/L |
| 10 | $D_0 = DO_{sat} - DO_{actual}$ | Initial oxygen deficit | mg/L |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The Streeter–Phelps equation is used by municipal corporations (e.g., Kerala Water Authority) to determine the **assimilation capacity** of rivers like the Periyar and Muvattupuzha before granting effluent discharge consents under the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the BOD Equation

We start with the fundamental hypothesis that the **rate of oxygen consumption by microorganisms is proportional to the amount of organic matter still present** (first-order reaction). This is a classic model proposed by Phelps in 1909 and is the foundation of sanitary engineering.

### Step 1: Write the Governing Differential Equation

The rate at which organic matter disappears is proportional to the organic matter itself:

$$\frac{dL}{dt} = -k \cdot L$$

> **Conversion logic:** A negative sign is introduced because $L$ is *consumed* (decreases) with time. $k$ has units of day⁻¹ so that the equation is dimensionally consistent.

### Step 2: Separate the Variables

Rearranging the differential equation so that all $L$ terms are on the left and all $t$ terms are on the right:

$$\frac{dL}{L} = -k \cdot dt$$

### Step 3: Integrate Both Sides

Apply the limits: at $t = 0$, $L = L_0$ (initial organic matter). At $t = t$, $L = L_t$ (remaining organic matter).

$$\int_{L_0}^{L_t} \frac{dL}{L} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\left[ \ln L \right]_{L_0}^{L_t} = -k \left[ t \right]_{0}^{t}$$

$$\ln L_t - \ln L_0 = -k t$$

### Step 4: Apply the Logarithm Identity

Using $\ln a - \ln b = \ln(a/b)$:

$$\ln\left( \frac{L_t}{L_0} \right) = -k t$$

### Step 5: Exponentiate Both Sides

$$\frac{L_t}{L_0} = e^{-k t}$$

$$\boxed{L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k t}}$$

### Step 6: Derive the BOD Exertion

The amount of oxygen consumed by the microorganisms up to time $t$ is exactly equal to the organic matter destroyed:

$$BOD_t = L_0 - L_t$$

Substituting the result from Step 5:

$$BOD_t = L_0 - L_0 \cdot e^{-k t}$$

$$\boxed{BOD_t = L_0 \left( 1 - e^{-k t} \right)}$$

> **Conversion logic:** When $t = 0$, $BOD_0 = 0$ (no oxygen consumed yet, makes physical sense). When $t \to \infty$, $BOD_\infty = L_0$ (the *ultimate* BOD, the total oxygen demand of all biodegradable material).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example – KTU Style

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**
> *A wastewater sample has an ultimate BOD $L_0$ = 250 mg/L. The deoxygenation constant at 20 °C is $k$ = 0.23 per day. Calculate the 5-day BOD at 20 °C and the 2-day BOD at 30 °C. Use $\theta = 1.047$.*

### Part (i) – 5-day BOD at 20 °C

$$BOD_5 = L_0 \left( 1 - e^{-5k} \right)$$

$$BOD_5 = 250 \left( 1 - e^{-5 \times 0.23} \right)$$

$$BOD_5 = 250 \left( 1 - e^{-1.15} \right)$$

Now, evaluate $e^{-1.15}$:

$$e^{-1.15} = 0.3166$$

Substitute back:

$$BOD_5 = 250 \left( 1 - 0.3166 \right) = 250 \times 0.6834$$

$$\boxed{BOD_5 = 170.85 \text{ mg/L}}$$

### Part (ii) – 2-day BOD at 30 °C

First, correct $k$ to 30 °C:

$$k_{30} = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(30-20)} = 0.23 \times (1.047)^{10}$$

Compute $(1.047)^{10}$:

$$(1.047)^{10} = e^{10 \cdot \ln(1.047)} = e^{10 \times 0.04593} = e^{0.4593} = 1.5828$$

Therefore:

$$k_{30} = 0.23 \times 1.5828 = 0.3640 \text{ per day}$$

Now compute $BOD_2$ at 30 °C:

$$BOD_2 = 250 \left( 1 - e^{-2 \times 0.3640} \right)$$

$$BOD_2 = 250 \left( 1 - e^{-0.7280} \right)$$

$$e^{-0.7280} = 0.4831$$

$$BOD_2 = 250 \left( 1 - 0.4831 \right) = 250 \times 0.5169$$

$$\boxed{BOD_2 = 129.22 \text{ mg/L}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Comparing Key Parameters

| Property | pH | DO | BOD | COD | TDS | Turbidity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Instrument** | Glass electrode pH meter | Membraned DO probe | BOD incubator + titration | Reflux + titration | Conductivity meter | Nephelometer |
| **Range (typical)** | 0–14 | 0–20 mg/L | 0–5000 mg/L | 0–10000 mg/L | 0–5000 mg/L | 0–1000 NTU |
| **Standard** | IS 3025 Pt 11 | IS 3025 Pt 38 | IS 3025 Pt 44 | IS 3025 Pt 58 | IS 3025 Pt 16 | IS 3025 Pt 10 |
| **Drinking Water Limit (IS 10500)** | 6.5–8.5 | ≥ 6 | < 2 | < 10 | 500 | 1 NTU (5 max) |
| **Response Time** | Seconds | 10–60 s | 5 days | 2–3 hours | Instant | Seconds |

## 3.4 DO Saturation Calculation – Worked Example

> **Problem:** Find the saturation DO at 25 °C using the empirical formula.

$$DO_{sat}(25) = 14.62 - 0.3898(25) + 0.006969(25)^2 - 0.00005896(25)^3$$

Step-by-step:

$$14.62 - 0.3898(25) = 14.62 - 9.745 = 4.875$$

$$0.006969 \times (25)^2 = 0.006969 \times 625 = 4.3556$$

$$0.00005896 \times (25)^3 = 0.00005896 \times 15625 = 0.9213$$

Sum:

$$DO_{sat}(25) = 4.875 + 4.3556 - 0.9213 = 8.31 \text{ mg/L}$$

> This matches the standard table value of **8.26 mg/L at 25 °C**, confirming the formula's validity (the small discrepancy arises from the empirical fit of 3rd-degree polynomial).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Water Pollutants

The following Mermaid graph maps the taxonomy of water pollutants from their source categories down to their specific measured parameters.

```mermaid
graph TD
    sourceA["Anthropogenic Sources"] --> catA1["Domestic Sewage"]
    sourceA --> catA2["Industrial Effluents"]
    sourceA --> catA3["Agricultural Runoff"]
    sourceA --> catA4["Thermal Discharges"]
    sourceB["Natural Sources"] --> catB1["Soil Erosion and Silting"]
    sourceB --> catB2["Decaying Organic Matter"]
    sourceB --> catB3["Volcanic and Mineral Leaching"]
    catA1 --> poll1["Pathogens BOD COD"]
    catA1 --> poll2["Total Coliforms"]
    catA2 --> poll3["Heavy Metals Lead Mercury Cadmium"]
    catA2 --> poll4["Acidic and Alkaline Wastes pH"]
    catA3 --> poll5["Nitrates Phosphates Eutrophication"]
    catA3 --> poll6["Pesticides Herbicides"]
    catA4 --> poll7["Temperature Rise DO Drop"]
    catB1 --> poll8["Turbidity TSS Total Suspended Solids"]
    catB2 --> poll9["BOD Organic Load"]
    catB3 --> poll10["Fluorides Arsenic TDS"]
    poll1 --> instr1["BOD Sensor COD Analyzer"]
    poll2 --> instr1
    poll3 --> instr2["Atomic Absorption Spectrophotometer"]
    poll4 --> instr3["pH Electrode Meter"]
    poll5 --> instr4["UV Vis Spectrophotometer"]
    poll6 --> instr4
    poll7 --> instr5["Thermistor DO Probe"]
    poll8 --> instr6["Turbidity Sensor Nephelometer"]
    poll9 --> instr1
    poll10 --> instr7["Ion Selective Electrode Conductivity Probe"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Streeter–Phelps DO Sag Concept

The following figure illustrates the two competing processes (deoxygenation and reaeration) that govern dissolved oxygen in a flowing river receiving organic waste.

```mermaid
graph LR
    pointA["River Upstream DO equals DOsat"] --> flow1["Flow into Discharge Point"]
    flow1 --> pointB["Mixing Zone Initial Deficit D0"]
    pointB --> process1["Deoxygenation Bacteria consume O2"]
    pointB --> process2["Reaeration Atmospheric O2 diffuses in"]
    process1 --> net["Net DO curve Sag"]
    process2 --> net
    net --> critical["Critical Point Minimum DO Dc at tc"]
    critical --> recover["Recovery Zone DO rises back to DOsat"]
    recover --> pointC["River Downstream Aerobic Balance"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Generic Water Quality Monitoring Instrument Chain

This block-level functional architecture represents the **transducer–signal–display chain** common to all the parameters discussed above. The instructor is expected to draw this in the KTU exam as a 7-mark question.

```mermaid
graph LR
    block1["Sensing Element Electrode or Photocell"] --> block2["Transducer Converts Chemical to mV or mA"]
    block2 --> block3["Signal Conditioning Amplifier Filter"]
    block3 --> block4["ADC Analog to Digital Converter"]
    block4 --> block5["Microcontroller Calibration and Linearization"]
    block5 --> block6["Display Unit LCD or PC"]
    block5 --> block7["Alarm or Actuator"]
    block1 --> sample["Sample Water from Stream"]
    block2 --> ref["Reference Electrode or Standard Solution"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** In Module 1 of Environmental Instrumentation, the syllabus emphasizes the **transducer principle**, not the full electronics. For example, for pH measurement, the "sensing element" is a glass membrane that develops a potential proportional to the log of H⁺ ion activity (Nernst equation, covered in Module 2).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define BOD. Why is it measured at 20 °C for 5 days?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand) is the amount of dissolved oxygen consumed by aerobic microorganisms to biochemically oxidize the organic matter in a water sample, under standard conditions.
- (1 mark) Definition.
- (1 mark) 5 days is chosen because ~68% of biodegradable matter is oxidized by day 5 in domestic sewage, providing a reproducible end-point.
- (1 mark) 20 °C is the standard reference temperature because microbial activity is temperature-dependent; reporting at 20 °C allows comparison across labs and seasons.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between BOD and COD. Which is greater and why?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 mark) BOD measures only **biodegradable** organic matter (5 days, microbial action); COD measures **total** (biodegradable + non-biodegradable) organics via strong chemical oxidant (K₂Cr₂O₇).
- (1 mark) COD is **always greater than or equal to BOD** because it oxidizes compounds that microbes cannot break down (e.g., lignin, cellulose, aromatic hydrocarbons).
- (1 mark) The ratio BOD/COD indicates **biodegradability**: > 0.5 → easily treatable; < 0.3 → requires advanced treatment.

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the first-order BOD equation $BOD_t = L_0(1 - e^{-kt})$ starting from the governing differential equation. State clearly the meaning of each term. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A wastewater sample has $L_0$ = 300 mg/L and $k$ = 0.20/day at 20 °C. Calculate:
  (i) $BOD_5$ at 20 °C
  (ii) $BOD_7$ at 20 °C
  (iii) The remaining organic load $L_5$ at day 5.
Use $e^{-1.0} = 0.3679$, $e^{-1.4} = 0.2466$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution – Question A

**Part (a) – Derivation (7 Marks)**

The deoxygenation rate is assumed first-order:

$$\frac{dL}{dt} = -k \cdot L$$

(1 mark) Writing the differential equation and stating the first-order assumption.

Separating and integrating from $t = 0$ to $t = t$:

$$\int_{L_0}^{L_t} \frac{dL}{L} = -k \int_0^t dt$$

(1 mark) Setting up the integral with correct limits.

$$\ln\left( \frac{L_t}{L_0} \right) = -k t \implies L_t = L_0 e^{-kt}$$

(2 marks) Solving the integral to obtain the remaining organic load.

The BOD exerted is the organic matter destroyed:

$$BOD_t = L_0 - L_t = L_0(1 - e^{-kt})$$

(1 mark) Defining $BOD_t$ as the difference.

- $L_0$ = ultimate BOD (mg/L)
- $k$ = deoxygenation rate constant (day⁻¹)
- $t$ = time in days

(2 marks) Listing the meaning of all three terms.

**Part (b) – Numerical (7 Marks)**

**(i)** $BOD_5$ at 20 °C:

$$BOD_5 = 300 \left( 1 - e^{-0.20 \times 5} \right) = 300 (1 - e^{-1.0})$$

(1 mark) Substituting values.

$$BOD_5 = 300 \times (1 - 0.3679) = 300 \times 0.6321$$

(1 mark) Computing the exponential term.

$$\boxed{BOD_5 = 189.63 \text{ mg/L}}$$

(1 mark) Final answer with units.

**(ii)** $BOD_7$ at 20 °C:

$$BOD_7 = 300 \left( 1 - e^{-0.20 \times 7} \right) = 300 (1 - e^{-1.4})$$

(1 mark) Substituting values.

$$BOD_7 = 300 \times (1 - 0.2466) = 300 \times 0.7534$$

(1 mark) Computing the exponential.

$$\boxed{BOD_7 = 226.02 \text{ mg/L}}$$

(1 mark) Final answer with units.

**(iii)** Remaining organic load at day 5:

$$L_5 = 300 \times e^{-1.0} = 300 \times 0.3679 = 110.37 \text{ mg/L}$$

(1 mark) Final answer with units.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units** ($mg/L$ or $ppm$) costs 0.5 marks each time.
> 2. **Mixing $L_0$ and $BOD_5$**: Students often confuse *ultimate BOD* with *5-day BOD*. Remember $L_0$ is the *total* potential oxygen demand, $BOD_5$ is what is exerted in 5 days.
> 3. **Using the wrong exponent**: Some students write $e^{-0.20 \times 5}$ as $e^{-0.20}$ or $e^{-5.0}$. Check the multiplication carefully.
> 4. **Skipping the differential equation step** in derivation questions: KTU gives **2 out of 7 marks** just for writing the first-order rate equation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks – Internal Choice) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **Streeter–Phelps equation** for the dissolved oxygen sag curve in a river. Sketch the curve and identify the critical point. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A river with initial DO = 8.0 mg/L receives an organic waste discharge having $L_0$ = 50 mg/L. The deoxygenation constant $k$ = 0.30/day and reaeration constant $k_2$ = 0.50/day. The saturation DO at the river temperature is 9.0 mg/L. Calculate the **critical deficit** $D_c$ and the **critical time** $t_c$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution – Question B

**Part (a) – Streeter–Phelps Theory (7 Marks)**

(1 mark) The Streeter–Phelps model describes the **simultaneous deoxygenation** by bacteria and **reaeration** from the atmosphere in a river receiving organic waste.

(2 marks) The net DO deficit equation:

$$D_t = \frac{k L_0}{k_2 - k} \left( e^{-kt} - e^{-k_2 t} \right) + D_0 e^{-k_2 t}$$

(1 mark) Definition of terms ($D_t, D_0, k, k_2, L_0$).

(1 mark) Sketch description: The DO curve starts at $DO_{sat}$, **drops** to a minimum at the **critical point** ($D_c, t_c$), then **recovers** to $DO_{sat}$ asymptotically.

(1 mark) The critical point is where deoxygenation rate equals reaeration rate, i.e., $\frac{dD_t}{dt} = 0$.

(1 mark) Beyond $t_c$, the river **self-purifies**; if DO drops to 0, **anaerobic conditions** develop with fish kill and foul odor.

**Part (b) – Critical Deficit and Time (7 Marks)**

Given: $D_0 = 9.0 - 8.0 = 1.0$ mg/L. (1 mark)

**Critical time** formula (from $\frac{dD_t}{dt} = 0$):

$$t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k} \ln\left[ \frac{k_2}{k} \left( 1 - \frac{D_0 (k_2 - k)}{k L_0} \right) \right]$$

(1 mark) Writing the formula.

$$t_c = \frac{1}{0.50 - 0.30} \ln\left[ \frac{0.50}{0.30} \left( 1 - \frac{1.0 \times 0.20}{0.30 \times 50} \right) \right]$$

(1 mark) Substitution.

$$t_c = \frac{1}{0.20} \ln\left[ 1.6667 \times \left( 1 - 0.01333 \right) \right]$$

$$t_c = 5.0 \times \ln(1.6444) = 5.0 \times 0.4977 = 2.49 \text{ days}$$

(1 mark) Computation. $\boxed{t_c \approx 2.49 \text{ days}}$.

**Critical deficit** formula:

$$D_c = \frac{k L_0}{k_2} e^{-k t_c}$$

(1 mark) Writing the formula.

$$D_c = \frac{0.30 \times 50}{0.50} \times e^{-0.30 \times 2.49}$$

$$D_c = 30 \times e^{-0.747} = 30 \times 0.4737 = 14.21 \text{ mg/L}$$

(1 mark) Computation.

> **Physical interpretation:** Since $D_c$ (14.21 mg/L) **exceeds** the saturation DO of 9.0 mg/L, the river would go **completely anaerobic** (DO = 0). This implies the waste load is too high and the river cannot assimilate it – **a consent-to-discharge violation** under the Water Act.

(1 mark) Final interpretation with engineering significance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Streeter–Phelps Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error** in the $\ln$ bracket: It must be a *positive* quantity; if it becomes negative or zero, your input is wrong.
> 2. **Not converting $D_0$**: A common 1-mark loss is forgetting $D_0 = DO_{sat} - DO_{actual}$.
> 3. **Mixing $k$ and $k_2$**: $k$ (deoxygenation) is *always* smaller than $k_2$ (reaeration) in shallow, flowing rivers. If your $t_c$ comes out negative, the values are swapped.
> 4. **Skipping the final interpretation**: KTU awards **1 mark** for commenting on whether the river can self-purify or not.

## 5.3 Topic Recap – Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Water Pollution** = contamination of water body rendering it unfit for intended use.
- [x] **Six KTU-mandatory parameters**: pH, DO, BOD, COD, TDS, Turbidity.
- [x] **BOD equation**: $BOD_t = L_0(1 - e^{-kt})$ — *first-order kinetics*.
- [x] **Ultimate BOD** $L_0$ is the total potential oxygen demand ($t \to \infty$).
- [x] **5-day BOD at 20 °C** is the standard (IS 3025 Part 44).
- [x] **Temperature correction**: $k_T = k_{20} \cdot 1.047^{(T-20)}$.
- [x] **COD > BOD always** (COD oxidizes both biodegradable and non-biodegradable).
- [x] **Streeter–Phelps** governs the **DO sag curve** in rivers.
- [x] **Critical point**: $t_c$ = time of minimum DO; $D_c$ = minimum DO deficit.
- [x] **Hardness** is expressed as equivalent CaCO₃ in mg/L.
- [x] **Drinking water limits (IS 10500)**: pH 6.5–8.5; DO ≥ 6; BOD < 2; TDS 500; Turbidity < 1 NTU.
- [x] **DO saturation decreases with temperature** (warm water holds less oxygen).
- [x] **Pathogens** → waterborne diseases; **Heavy metals** → bioaccumulation; **Nutrients** → eutrophication.
- [x] **Instrument chain**: Sensor → Transducer → Signal Conditioning → ADC → Display/Alarm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy Tip:** In 14-mark questions, the KTU valuation key allocates ~**3 marks for diagrams/curves**, ~**3 marks for definitions**, and ~**8 marks for derivations + numerics**. Always present the formula *before* substituting values, and box your final answer with units. This single habit recovers **1–2 marks** consistently.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soil Pollution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT832 - ENVIRONMENTAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Soil Pollution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soil Pollution** is defined as the *presence of xenobiotic chemicals, heavy metals, pesticides, petroleum hydrocarbons, radionuclides, or any physical/biological agent in the soil matrix at concentrations that exceed natural background levels and adversely affect soil fertility, ecological balance, and human health through the food chain.*

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **OEICT832 - Environmental Instrumentation**, Module 1 treats soil pollution as one of the three principal environmental matrices (along with air and water) that must be quantitatively characterized using modern instrumentation. The objective is to identify, quantify, and monitor soil pollutants using physico-chemical, electrochemical, and spectroscopic sensors.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine your kitchen sponge. When you soak a brand-new sponge in clean water, it absorbs and holds the water beautifully. But if you soak the same sponge for years in oil, dish soap, and food waste, it becomes permanently stained, hardened, and incapable of absorbing clean water properly. Soil behaves the same way. Soil is the Earth's "sponge" — it has pores that hold water, air, and nutrients for plants. When we dump industrial waste, pesticides, plastics, or chemicals onto it, the soil's "sponge pores" get permanently clogged and poisoned. After this, even if we pour clean water on it, the soil can no longer support healthy plant life because its natural chemistry has been destroyed.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

The following standard metrics and constants are essential for soil pollution studies:

- **Background Concentration:** The natural, pre-industrial concentration of an element in uncontaminated soil (varies by region).
- **USEPA Soil Screening Level (SSL):** Regulatory threshold used for risk-based soil cleanup (typically **10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁴ cancer risk**).
- **pH Range of Healthy Soil:** **6.0 – 7.5** (slightly acidic to neutral).
- **Electrical Conductivity (EC) of normal soil:** **< 4 dS/m (deciSiemens per meter)**.
- **Organic Matter (OM) of fertile soil:** **2 – 5 %** by dry weight.
- **Standard Test Sieve Sizes (ASTM D422):** No. 10 (2.00 mm), No. 200 (0.075 mm) for grain size analysis.

### 1.4 Sources of Soil Pollution — Categorical Hierarchy

Soil pollutants originate from **five** primary source categories:

| Source Category | Specific Pollutants | Mechanism of Entry |
|---|---|---|
| **Agricultural** | Pesticides, herbicides, fertilizers (NPK), antibiotics | Direct spraying, leaching, runoff |
| **Industrial** | Heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd, Cr, As), PAHs, PCBs, cyanide | Atmospheric deposition, spills, dumping |
| **Domestic** | Solid waste, plastics, sewage sludge, e-waste | Landfilling, open dumping, irrigation |
| **Petroleum** | Crude oil, diesel, BTEX (Benzene, Toluene, Ethylbenzene, Xylene) | Leakage from pipelines, refineries |
| **Radioactive** | U-238, Cs-137, Sr-90, Ra-226 | Mining, nuclear accidents, fallout |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why and How" of Soil Pollution

The persistence and toxicity of a soil pollutant is governed by **four coupled physico-chemical processes**, often summarized using the acronym **CAPE**:

1. **C — Chemical Speciation:** A pollutant may exist in multiple ionic forms (e.g., Cr³⁺ vs Cr⁶⁺). The more oxidized/higher-valent species is usually more toxic. **Why?** Because bioavailability depends on ionic form — plants absorb free ions more readily than chelated forms.
2. **A — Adsorption-Desorption:** Soil clay and organic matter provide active surface area for retaining pollutants. **How?** The Cation Exchange Capacity (CEC) of the soil determines how tightly metal cations like Pb²⁺ bind to soil colloids.
3. **P — Plant Uptake (Bioaccumulation):** Root systems absorb soluble ionic species and translocate them to leaves and fruits, entering the human food chain.
4. **E — Ecosystem Transfer:** Pollutants migrate vertically (leaching to groundwater) and laterally (surface runoff to water bodies), amplifying the contamination radius far beyond the source.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The transport of pollutants from soil → plant → animal → human is called the **Biomagnification Pathway**. Persistent pollutants like DDT and Hg have biomagnification factors as high as **10⁶** (one million-fold concentration increase up the food chain).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **board-exam-essential**. Memorize the variables, units, and the typical numerical range.

| Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Variable Definitions | Typical Range / Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Soil pH** | $pH = -\log_{10}[H^+]$ | $[H^+]$ = hydrogen ion activity | **4.0 – 9.0** | Determines nutrient availability and metal mobility |
| **Electrical Conductivity (EC)** | $EC = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | $\rho$ = resistivity of soil extract | **0 – 4 dS/m** (safe) | Salinity indicator for irrigation suitability |
| **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)** | $TDS \, (mg/L) \approx 640 \times EC \, (dS/m)$ | EC in dS/m | **< 2000 mg/L** | Quick estimation from EC probe |
| **Cation Exchange Capacity (CEC)** | $CEC = \sum (\text{Exchangeable Cations})$ | $\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{Mg}^{2+} + \text{K}^+ + \text{Na}^+ + \text{H}^+$ | **10 – 30 cmol(+)/kg** | Quantifies soil's buffering capacity against metals |
| **Contamination Factor (CF)** | $CF = \dfrac{C_{\text{sample}}}{C_{\text{background}}}$ | $C_{\text{sample}}$ = measured concentration; $C_{\text{background}}$ = pristine level | CF \vert 1 = Low, 1–3 = Moderate, 3–6 = Considerable, \vert 6 = Very High | Hakanson Index for heavy metal risk |
| **Geo-accumulation Index ($I_{\text{geo}}$)** | $I_{\text{geo}} = \log_{2}\left(\dfrac{C_n}{1.5 \times B_n}\right)$ | $C_n$ = measured conc.; $B_n$ = geochemical background; 1.5 = correction factor | Class 0 (\vert 0) to Class 6 (\vert 5) | Müller's Index for sediment/soil enrichment |
| **Pollution Load Index (PLI)** | $PLI = \sqrt[n]{CF_1 \times CF_2 \times \dots \times CF_n}$ | $CF_i$ = contamination factor of i-th metal; $n$ = number of metals | PLI \vert 1 = polluted | Multi-metal overall pollution indicator |
| **Leaching Factor (LF)** | $LF = \dfrac{\text{Rainfall (mm/yr)}}{(\text{CEC} \times \text{OM \%})}$ | Empirical index | Lower LF = less leaching | Predicts groundwater contamination risk |
| **Soil Organic Carbon (SOC)** | $SOC = \dfrac{\text{OC \%} \times \text{Bulk Density} \times \text{Depth}}{100}$ | OC = organic carbon; Depth in cm | **0.5 – 3.0 %** | Soil health index, carbon sequestration |
| **Moisture Content** | $MC \, (\%) = \dfrac{W_{\text{wet}} - W_{\text{dry}}}{W_{\text{dry}}} \times 100$ | $W_{\text{wet}}, W_{\text{dry}}$ = masses | **10 – 35 %** | Sampling normalization parameter |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Environmental Site Assessment (ESA):** CF and $I_{\text{geo}}$ are mandated by **USEPA CERCLA** (1980) and Indian **CPCB** for brownfield redevelopment.
- **Precision Agriculture:** Soil EC and pH sensors mounted on GPS-guided tractors allow variable-rate fertilizer application, reducing agro-chemical pollution by up to **40 %**.
- **Bioremediation Engineering:** CEC and SOC values guide the design of land-farming units and bio-pile systems for petroleum-contaminated soils.
- **Geotechnical Engineering:** Soil polluted with industrial effluents alters shear strength and compressibility, requiring modified foundation design (BS 8004:1986 Section 4.2).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem — Pollution Load Index (PLI) Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **standard 7-mark analytical question** that has appeared repeatedly in KTU Module-1 ESE papers. Master the step-by-step logic below.

**Problem Statement (Model Question):**
A soil sample from an industrial zone near Kochi was analyzed for four heavy metals. The results are:

| Heavy Metal | Measured Concentration $C_{\text{sample}}$ (mg/kg) | Background Concentration $C_{\text{background}}$ (mg/kg) |
|---|---|---|
| Lead (Pb) | 120 | 25 |
| Cadmium (Cd) | 4.5 | 0.5 |
| Chromium (Cr) | 90 | 60 |
| Nickel (Ni) | 65 | 30 |

Calculate (i) the **Contamination Factor (CF)** for each metal, (ii) classify each using Hakanson's scale, and (iii) compute the **Pollution Load Index (PLI)**.

---

#### Solution Step 1 — Compute CF for each metal

Using the formula:
$$CF = \dfrac{C_{\text{sample}}}{C_{\text{background}}}$$

Row-wise substitution:

$$CF_{\text{Pb}} = \dfrac{120}{25} = 4.80$$

$$CF_{\text{Cd}} = \dfrac{4.5}{0.5} = 9.00$$

$$CF_{\text{Cr}} = \dfrac{90}{60} = 1.50$$

$$CF_{\text{Ni}} = \dfrac{65}{30} \approx 2.17$$

> Each $CF$ value is now compared with the Hakanson Scale (CF \vert 1 = Low; 1–3 = Moderate; 3–6 = Considerable; \vert 6 = Very High).

#### Solution Step 2 — Classify each metal

| Metal | CF Value | Classification (Hakanson) |
|---|---|---|
| Lead (Pb) | 4.80 | **Considerable** |
| Cadmium (Cd) | 9.00 | **Very High** |
| Chromium (Cr) | 1.50 | **Moderate** |
| Nickel (Ni) | 2.17 | **Moderate** |

#### Solution Step 3 — Compute the Pollution Load Index (PLI)

The general PLI formula for $n$ metals is:

$$PLI = \left( CF_1 \times CF_2 \times \dots \times CF_n \right)^{\dfrac{1}{n}}$$

Here $n = 4$. Substituting the calculated CFs:

$$\begin{aligned}
PLI &= \left( CF_{\text{Pb}} \times CF_{\text{Cd}} \times CF_{\text{Cr}} \times CF_{\text{Ni}} \right)^{\dfrac{1}{4}} \\[4pt]
&= \left( 4.80 \times 9.00 \times 1.50 \times 2.17 \right)^{\dfrac{1}{4}}
\end{aligned}$$

Now evaluate the product inside the parenthesis:

$$\begin{aligned}
4.80 \times 9.00 &= 43.20 \\[4pt]
43.20 \times 1.50 &= 64.80 \\[4pt]
64.80 \times 2.17 &= 140.616
\end{aligned}$$

Take the fourth root:

$$PLI = (140.616)^{0.25}$$

Apply the fourth-root identity: $(x)^{0.25} = \sqrt{\sqrt{x}}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{140.616} &\approx 11.859 \\[4pt]
\sqrt{11.859} &\approx 3.444
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$PLI \approx 3.44$$

#### Solution Step 4 — Interpretation

Since $PLI = 3.44 > 1$, the soil is classified as **Polluted overall**. Cadmium is the dominant contributor due to its very high CF (9.00), reflecting industrial activity such as electroplating and Ni-Cd battery manufacturing.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — Geo-accumulation Index ($I_{\text{geo}}$)

**Problem:** For the same Kochi site, compute $I_{\text{geo}}$ for Lead.

Using Müller's formula:

$$I_{\text{geo}} = \log_{2}\left( \dfrac{C_n}{1.5 \times B_n} \right)$$

Substitute $C_n = 120$ mg/kg and $B_n = 25$ mg/kg:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{geo}} &= \log_{2}\left( \dfrac{120}{1.5 \times 25} \right) \\[4pt]
&= \log_{2}\left( \dfrac{120}{37.5} \right) \\[4pt]
&= \log_{2}(3.20)
\end{aligned}$$

Convert log base 2 to natural log:

$$\log_{2}(3.20) = \dfrac{\ln(3.20)}{\ln(2)} = \dfrac{1.1632}{0.6931} \approx 1.678$$

Therefore:

$$I_{\text{geo}} \approx 1.68$$

This corresponds to **Class 2** (Moderately polluted) on the Müller scale (Class 0: $I_{\text{geo}} \le 0$; Class 1: $0 < I_{\text{geo}} \le 1$; Class 2: $1 < I_{\text{geo}} \le 2$; etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the factor 1.5?** It compensates for natural lithospheric variability due to weathering and also accounts for minor anthropogenic inputs that are considered background, ensuring the index is conservative.

### 3.3 Derivation of the TDS–EC Approximation

The empirical relation $TDS = 640 \times EC$ arises from the ionic composition of natural soil-water extracts. For a 1:1 soil-water suspension:

$$\begin{aligned}
EC \, (dS/m) &\leftrightarrow \Sigma \, \text{cation moles per kg of water} \\[4pt]
TDS \, (mg/L) &\approx M_{\text{avg}} \times [\text{total ion concentration in mol/L}] \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow TDS &\approx k \times EC
\end{aligned}$$

Where $k \approx 640$ for typical soil extracts where the average molar mass of dissolved salts is near NaCl's 58.44 g/mol. The constant is empirical because the conversion factor strictly depends on the dominant ionic species (Na⁺, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.2 CAPE Conceptual Framework — Soil Pollutant Fate Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Pollutant Released to Soil]

    Q1[CEC Buffering]

```

### 4.3 Soil Pollution Measurement — Block-Level Instrumentation Architecture

```mermaid

    OUT1[Quantitative Soil Pollution Report]
    OUT2[PLI and I geo Index Output]

    S0 --> S1
    S0 --> S2
    S1 --> I1
    S1 --> I2
    S1 --> I3
    S1 --> I4

    S2 --> L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L2 --> L4

    I1 --> OUT1

    I3 --> OUT1
    I4 --> OUT1
    L3 --> OUT1

    L5 --> OUT1
```
