### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Emission vs Absorption Comparison

| Parameter | Emission Spectrography | Atomic Absorption Spectrometry (AAS) |
|---|---|---|
| Physical phenomenon measured | Photon **emitted** by excited atoms | Photon **absorbed** by ground-state atoms |
| Atomization source | Arc, spark, plasma, flame | Flame or graphite furnace |
| Radiation source | Sample itself | Hollow Cathode Lamp (HCL) |
| Population probed | Excited-state atoms (small fraction) | Ground-state atoms (large fraction) |
| Sensitivity to trace metals | Moderate (1–10 ppm) | **High (ppb level)** |
| Multi-element capability | **Excellent** (simultaneous) | Single-element (separate HCL needed) |
| Calibration range | Wide linear range | Limited (Beer-Lambert, A < 1) |
| Interferences | Spectral overlap, self-absorption | Chemical, ionization, matrix effects |
| Typical detection limit for Pb | 5–50 ppm | 0.01–0.1 ppm (flame), 0.1 ppb (graphite furnace) |
| Cost | Moderate–High | Moderate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Emission Spectrograph Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Excitation Source Arc Spark Plasma] --> slitA[Entrance Slit]
    slitA --> colA[Collimating Lens]
    colA --> dispA[Dispersing Element Prism or Grating]
    dispA --> focA[Focusing Lens]
    focA --> detA[Detector Photographic Plate or PMT or CCD]
    detA --> recA[Recorder Computer Output]

    style srcA fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style slitA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style colA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style dispA fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#cc3300,color:#000
    style focA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style detA fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style recA fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#330099,color:#000
```

**Flow Description:**
1. **Excitation Source** vaporizes and energizes the sample.
2. **Entrance Slit** defines the beam geometry.
3. **Collimating Lens** makes the beam parallel.
4. **Dispersing Element** separates wavelengths spatially.
5. **Focusing Lens** focuses each wavelength onto a specific point.
6. **Detector** records the intensity at each wavelength.
7. **Recorder** outputs the spectrum for analysis.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Atomic Absorption Spectrometer (AAS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    hcl[Hollow Cathode Lamp HCL] --> m1[Modulator Chopper]
    m1 --> ato[Atomizer Flame or Graphite Furnace]
    ato --> m2[Monochromator]
    m2 --> pm[Photomultiplier Tube PMT]
    pm --> amp[Lock-in Amplifier]
    amp --> comp[Computer Display Absorbance vs Concentration]

    style hcl fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#cc3300,color:#000
    style m1 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style ato fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style m2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style pm fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style amp fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#330099,color:#000
    style comp fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
```

**Flow Description:**
1. **HCL** emits the characteristic spectrum of the analyte.
2. **Modulator** (chopper) AC-modulates the beam to distinguish source signal from flame emission.
3. **Atomizer** converts liquid sample to free ground-state atoms.
4. **Monochromator** isolates the analytical line (e.g., 283.3 nm for Pb).
5. **PMT** detects the transmitted intensity.
6. **Lock-in Amplifier** extracts the modulated signal, rejecting flame background.
7. **Computer** displays absorbance and computes concentration.

---

### 4.3 Energy Level Transition Diagram (Mermaid Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    e3[Level 3 E3]:::excited
    e2[Level 2 E2]:::excited
    e1[Level 1 E1]:::ground
    e0[Ground State E0]:::ground

    abs1[Absorption Photon Absorbed] --> e0
    e0 --> em1[Emission Photon Emitted] --> e1
    e1 --> em2[Emission Photon Emitted] --> e2
    e2 --> em3[Emission Photon Emitted] --> e3

    classDef ground fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef excited fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#cc3300,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Absorption:** Sample absorbs photons; ground-state electrons jump up (used in AAS).
- **Emission:** Excited electrons fall back down; photons of characteristic wavelengths are released (used in Emission Spectrography).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q1. Define Atomic Absorption Spectrometry (AAS). State Beer-Lambert's Law and explain its significance in AAS.**

**Model Answer:**

Atomic Absorption Spectrometry is a spectrometric analytical technique used to quantify the concentration of an element in a sample by measuring the absorption of monochromatic radiation by free, ground-state atoms in the gaseous phase.

**Beer-Lambert Law:**
$$A = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon \cdot b \cdot c$$

**Significance in AAS:**
- Provides a **linear quantitative relationship** between absorbance and concentration.
- Enables accurate determination of **trace metal concentrations** (ppm to ppb range).
- Forms the theoretical foundation for AAS calibration curves (A vs. C plots).
- Valid only for **dilute, non-scattering, homogeneous solutions** (A < 1.0).

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for significance.)**

---

> [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q2. Differentiate between Emission Spectrograph and Atomic Absorption Spectrometer.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Emission Spectrograph | Atomic Absorption Spectrometer |
|---|---|---|
| Phenomenon | Measures emitted radiation | Measures absorbed radiation |
| Energy source | Excitation source (arc, spark, plasma) | Hollow Cathode Lamp |
| Atom population probed | Excited-state atoms | Ground-state atoms |
| Multi-element analysis | Possible (simultaneous) | Single-element |
| Detection limit | Moderate (ppm) | Low (ppb) |
| Calibration | Intensity vs. concentration | Absorbance vs. concentration |

**(3 marks: 1 for each correct paired difference across 3 rows.)**

---

### Part B — Full Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**OR**

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

You are required to answer **ONE** of the two questions. Each carries 14 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
> With a neat block diagram, explain the principle and working of an **Emission Spectrograph**. List its main components and explain the role of the dispersing element.

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:**
An emission spectrograph is based on the principle that when a sample is energized sufficiently, its atoms emit photons of **characteristic wavelengths** unique to each element. These wavelengths are spatially separated by a dispersing element, and the resulting spectrum is recorded for qualitative and quantitative analysis.

**Working Steps:**
1. The sample is introduced into an **excitation source** (DC arc, AC arc, or spark).
2. The high temperature (~4000–10000 K) **vaporizes, atomizes, and excites** the sample atoms.
3. Excited electrons relax to lower energy levels, emitting photons of discrete wavelengths.
4. The emitted polychromatic light passes through an **entrance slit** and a **collimating lens**.
5. The **dispersing element** (prism or diffraction grating) spatially separates the wavelengths.
6. A **focusing lens** directs each wavelength to a specific point on the **detector** (photographic plate or PMT/CCD).
7. The recorded spectrum is analyzed to **identify elements** (from wavelength) and **quantify them** (from line intensity).

**Role of Dispersing Element:**
The dispersing element (prism or diffraction grating) **spatially separates** polychromatic light into its constituent wavelengths. A diffraction grating is preferred in modern instruments due to:
- Higher dispersion and resolving power
- Linear wavelength dispersion
- Wavelength independence of dispersion efficiency (with blazed gratings)
- Compact design

**Main Components of an Emission Spectrograph:**
1. Excitation source (arc/spark)
2. Entrance slit
3. Collimating lens/mirror
4. Dispersing element (prism or grating)
5. Focusing lens/mirror
6. Detector (photographic plate / PMT / CCD)
7. Recording system (computer)

**(7 marks: 2 for principle, 3 for working, 2 for dispersing element and components.)**

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
> A sample of an unknown alloy is analyzed using an emission spectrograph. The intensity of the Mg line at 285.2 nm is **750 units**, while a standard sample of 50 ppm Mg gives an intensity of **500 units** under identical conditions. The background intensity is **50 units**. Calculate the Mg concentration in the unknown sample.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Subtract background from both intensities to get net signal.

$$I_{\text{unknown, net}} = 750 - 50 = 700 \text{ units}$$

$$I_{\text{standard, net}} = 500 - 50 = 450 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2:** Use the proportional relationship (linear regime of emission spectroscopy).

$$\frac{C_{\text{unknown}}}{C_{\text{standard}}} = \frac{I_{\text{unknown, net}}}{I_{\text{standard, net}}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.

$$\frac{C_{\text{unknown}}}{50 \text{ ppm}} = \frac{700}{450}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the unknown concentration.

$$C_{\text{unknown}} = 50 \times \frac{700}{450} = 50 \times 1.5556 = 77.78 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 5:** Express with appropriate significant figures.

$$\boxed{C_{\text{Mg, unknown}} \approx 77.8 \text{ ppm}}$$

**Verification:** The result lies in a reasonable range for alloy analysis; the intensity ratio confirms a higher concentration than the standard.

**(7 marks: 2 for background correction, 2 for proportionality setup, 2 for calculation, 1 for final answer with units.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
> Explain the **principle, instrumentation, and working of an Atomic Absorption Spectrometer (AAS)**. Why is a Hollow Cathode Lamp (HCL) used as the radiation source in AAS?

**Model Answer:**

**Principle of AAS:**
AAS is based on the absorption of monochromatic radiation by **free, ground-state atoms** of the analyte. The degree of absorption is proportional to the concentration of atoms, as described by the **Beer-Lambert Law**. AAS is highly sensitive for trace metal analysis.

**Working:**
1. A liquid sample is aspirated into a **flame atomizer** (or injected into a graphite furnace).
2. The high temperature of the flame (~2000–3000 K) **desolvates, vaporizes, and atomizes** the sample.
3. A **Hollow Cathode Lamp (HCL)** emits the characteristic spectrum of the analyte element.
4. The light passes through the atomized sample, where ground-state atoms **absorb the resonant radiation**.
5. A **monochromator** isolates the analytical line from any interfering radiation.
6. A **photomultiplier tube (PMT)** measures the transmitted intensity.
7. The instrument computes absorbance $A = \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$ and relates it to concentration via calibration.

**Instrumentation (Main Components):**
1. **Radiation Source:** Hollow Cathode Lamp (HCL) or Electrodeless Discharge Lamp (EDL)
2. **Atomizer:** Flame (air-acetylene, nitrous oxide-acetylene) or Graphite Furnace
3. **Monochromator:** Prism or grating-based, with adjustable slits
4. **Detector:** Photomultiplier Tube (PMT)
5. **Signal Processor:** Lock-in amplifier, A/D converter, computer

**Why HCL is Used:**
- Emits the **narrow, sharp characteristic lines** of the element of interest.
- Provides **high spectral purity**, ensuring selectivity.
- The **cathode is made of the analyte element** (or an alloy containing it), so its emission spectrum matches the absorption profile of the sample atoms.
- Produces **stable, intense output**, ensuring good signal-to-noise ratio.
- **Element-specific** — a different HCL is required for each element (this is a limitation).

**(7 marks: 2 for principle, 2 for working, 1 for components, 2 for HCL justification.)**

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
> A solution containing an unknown concentration of copper is analyzed by AAS. The following calibration data are obtained:

| Cu Concentration (ppm) | Absorbance |
|---|---|
| 0.0 | 0.000 |
| 1.0 | 0.080 |
| 2.0 | 0.160 |
| 4.0 | 0.320 |
| 6.0 | 0.480 |

> An unknown sample gives an absorbance of **0.280**. Determine the Cu concentration. If the path length of the cuvette is **10 cm** and the absorbance of a 1 ppm Cu standard is **0.080**, calculate the **molar absorptivity (ε)** of Cu under these conditions. (Atomic mass of Cu = 63.55 g/mol)

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Concentration of Unknown (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** Determine the slope (k) of the calibration line from the data.

$$k = \frac{A}{C} = \frac{0.080}{1.0} = 0.080 \text{ ppm}^{-1}$$

**Step 2:** Verify linearity by checking other data points.

| C (ppm) | A | A/C (ppm⁻¹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1.0 | 0.080 | 0.080 |
| 2.0 | 0.160 | 0.080 |
| 4.0 | 0.320 | 0.080 |
| 6.0 | 0.480 | 0.080 |

The data is perfectly linear (Beer-Lambert regime).

**Step 3:** Apply the calibration equation $C = A / k$.

$$C_{\text{unknown}} = \frac{0.280}{0.080} = 3.5 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to mg/L.

$$C_{\text{unknown}} = 3.5 \text{ mg/L}$$

---

**Part 2 — Molar Absorptivity Calculation (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Convert concentration of 1 ppm Cu standard to mol/L.

$$1 \text{ ppm} = 1 \text{ mg/L} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/L}}{63.55 \text{ g/mol}}$$

$$c = 1.574 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Beer-Lambert Law.

$$A = \varepsilon \cdot b \cdot c$$

$$0.080 = \varepsilon \times 10 \text{ cm} \times 1.574 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for ε.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{0.080}{10 \times 1.574 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{0.080}{1.574 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon \approx 508.3 \text{ L·mol}^{-1}\text{·cm}^{-1}}$$

**(7 marks: 2 for slope, 2 for unknown concentration, 3 for molar absorptivity.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Omission:** Forgetting units in the final answer (e.g., "77.78" instead of "77.78 ppm"). Always carry units through the calculation.
> 2. **Beer-Lambert Misuse:** Applying the law beyond the linear range (A > 1) or to scattering/turbid samples. State the validity condition explicitly.
> 3. **Confusing Emission and Absorption:** Mixing up which population is measured (excited vs ground state). Remember: **AAS → ground state**, **AES → excited state**.
> 4. **Background Not Subtracted:** In emission quantitative problems, forgetting to subtract background intensity leads to systematic error.
> 5. **Wrong HCL Justification:** Stating only "HCL is bright" without explaining that its cathode is made of the analyte element to provide a **resonant source**.
> 6. **Misidentifying Components:** Calling the detector a "spectrometer" or the dispersing element a "lens" — know the precise names of all components.
> 7. **Skipping the Calibration Check:** Failing to verify linearity (constant A/C ratio) before solving for unknown concentration.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Spectrometric Methods**

**Foundational Concepts:**
- ☐ **Spectrometric methods** analyze interaction of EM radiation with atoms/molecules for identification and quantification.
- ☐ **Photon energy equation:** $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$ — apply for both emission and absorption transitions.
- ☐ **Bohr's model:** Atomic transitions are discrete; each element has a unique spectral "fingerprint."

**Emission Spectrography:**
- ☐ Measures photons **emitted** by excited atoms.
- ☐ Components: **Excitation source → Slit → Collimator → Dispersing element → Detector → Recorder.**
- ☐ Dispersing element: **Prism** (older) or **Diffraction grating** (modern, preferred).
- ☐ Multi-element analysis; moderate sensitivity (ppm).
- ☐ Quantification: $C_{\text{unknown}} = C_{\text{std}} \times (I_{\text{unknown}} - I_{\text{bkg}}) / (I_{\text{std}} - I_{\text{bkg}})$

**Atomic Absorption Spectrometry (AAS):**
- ☐ Measures photons **absorbed** by ground-state atoms.
- ☐ **Beer-Lambert Law:** $A = \varepsilon b c = \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$.
- ☐ Valid for **A < 1.0** and dilute, homogeneous solutions.
- ☐ HCL is element-specific; cathode made of analyte element.
- ☐ Atomizer: flame (air-C₂H₂ or N₂O-C₂H₂) or graphite furnace (better LOD).
- ☐ Higher sensitivity than emission: ppb (flame) to sub-ppb (graphite furnace).
- ☐ Lock-in amplifier + chopper distinguish source signal from flame emission.

**Comparative Snapshot:**
- ☐ Emission → excited-state population; Absorption → ground-state population.
- ☐ Boltzmann calculation: at 2500 K, only ~0.006% of Na atoms excited → AAS measures larger population.
- ☐ AAS = single-element, high sensitivity; Emission = multi-element, moderate sensitivity.

**Key Formulas (must memorize):**
- ☐ $A = \varepsilon b c$
- ☐ $E = hc/\lambda$
- ☐ $R = mN = \lambda / \Delta \lambda$
- ☐ $d(\sin\theta_i + \sin\theta_m) = m\lambda$
- ☐ Boltzmann distribution: $N_j/N_i = (g_j/g_i) \exp(-\Delta E / k_B T)$

**Constants (must memorize values):**
- ☐ h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s
- ☐ c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s
- ☐ k_B = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K
- ☐ R (Rydberg) = 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹
- ☐ Nₐ = 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹

**Environmental Applications:**
- ☐ Heavy metal detection (Pb, Cd, Hg, As, Cr) in water, air, and soil.
- ☐ Compliance monitoring for WHO/EPA standards (typically ppb levels).
- ☐ Industrial effluent and atmospheric particulate analysis.

**Common KTU 14-Mark Question Patterns:**
- ☐ "Explain principle, working, and instrumentation of [instrument] with block diagram."
- ☐ "Derive Beer-Lambert Law and apply to a numerical problem."
- ☐ "Compare emission and absorption spectrometry."
- ☐ "Calculate concentration from calibration data with background correction."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Monitoring
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT832 - ENVIRONMENTAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Air Monitoring

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Air Monitoring** is the systematic, continuous, or periodic measurement, sampling, and analysis of ambient (outdoor) and indoor air to determine the concentration of gaseous and particulate pollutants, compare the results against regulatory standards (such as those issued by the **CPCB – Central Pollution Control Board**, **NAQI – National Ambient Air Quality Index**, and **WHO – World Health Organization**), and provide actionable data for pollution control and public health protection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 (Air Monitoring):**
> The course OEICT832 mandates a study of the principles, instrumentation, and measurement techniques used for quantifying air pollutants — both **gaseous species** (SO₂, NOₓ, CO, O₃, VOCs) and **particulate matter** (PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, TSPM).

> [!NOTE]
> **National Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Programme (NAMP)** in India operates through a network of stations under CPCB, using a uniform methodology based on the *IS 5182* series of Indian Standards for ambient air quality measurement.

## 1.2 Key Pollutants Classified (Criteria Pollutants)

The principal parameters routinely monitored are codified under India's **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS), 2009** and they fall into two broad categories:

| Category | Typical Pollutants | Primary Sources |
|----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Gaseous Pollutants** | SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, NH₃, Pb, Benzene | Combustion, vehicular exhaust, industrial process |
| **Particulate Pollutants** | PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, TSPM (Total Suspended Particulate Matter) | Road dust, fly ash, smoke, aerosols |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of Air Monitoring as a "Health Check-up for the Atmosphere"** 🩺
> Just as a doctor measures a patient's blood pressure, sugar level, and cholesterol to assess health, an air-quality scientist "draws air samples" through instruments that act as the atmosphere's diagnostic tools. The **High Volume Sampler** is the equivalent of a stethoscope for total dust load, while the **Gas Analyzer** is the blood-test lab that quantifies invisible chemical toxins. The result — the **AQI (Air Quality Index)** — is a single number, much like a patient's body temperature, summarizing overall atmospheric health.

## 1.4 Importance in Engineering Context

- **Industrial Compliance**: Power plants, steel mills, and cement factories must operate **Continuous Emission Monitoring Systems (CEMS)** under the EPA / CPCB consent framework.
- **Smart Cities & IoT Integration**: Modern low-cost sensor networks (e.g., metal-oxide MOX sensors, laser-scattering PM sensors) feed cloud dashboards for real-time city-scale AQI.
- **Public Health**: Globally, ambient PM₂.₅ causes ≈ **4.2 million premature deaths/year** (WHO, 2022 estimate) — making monitoring a life-saving engineering intervention.
- **KTU Relevance**: Questions frequently test the **principle**, **block diagram**, and **application** of techniques like **Chemiluminescence, NDIR, UV Fluorescence, and Beta Attenuation**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of an Air Monitoring Program

An air monitoring setup consists of four hierarchical layers:

1. **Sampling Layer** → pulls a representative air volume into the system.
2. **Conditioning Layer** → removes moisture (using a **nafion dryer** or **cold trap**), filters particulates, and stabilizes flow.
3. **Analysis / Detection Layer** → sensor or instrument converts chemical concentration into an electrical signal.
4. **Data Acquisition & Reporting Layer** → A/D conversion, averaging, AQI computation, transmission to central server.

## 2.2 Particulate Matter (PM) Measurement — Principle Categories

### (a) Gravimetric Method — The Reference Method
- Air is drawn through a pre-weighed filter (glass-fiber or quartz) at a controlled flow rate.
- The mass gained by the filter is divided by the volume of air sampled.

$$C_{PM} = \frac{(W_f - W_i)}{V_{air}} \quad [\mu g / m^3]$$

where $W_f$ is final mass, $W_i$ is initial mass, and $V_{air}$ is the sampled air volume at standard conditions.

### (b) Beta Attenuation Method
- A thin radioactive source (¹⁴C) emits β-particles that pass through the PM-laden filter.
- The attenuation follows **Lambert–Beer Law**.

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu \rho x}$$

where $\mu$ is the mass attenuation coefficient, $\rho$ is the areal density, and $x$ is the thickness of the deposit.

### (c) Optical / Light Scattering Method
- A laser beam illuminates the aerosol stream; scattered light is detected at angles (typically 90°).
- The intensity of scattering is proportional to particle number density and size (Mie scattering regime).

$$I_{scatter} \propto N \cdot d_p^6 \quad \text{(for large particles, Mie regime)}$$

## 2.3 Gaseous Pollutant Measurement — Reference Techniques

| Pollutant | Reference Method | Measurement Principle | Detector Output |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| **SO₂** | **West & Gaeke (Improved)** | SO₂ absorbed in TCM (tetrachloromercurate) forms a stable complex; colorimetric assay with **p-Rosaniline** | Spectrophotometric absorbance at 548 nm |
| **NOₓ** | **Chemiluminescence** | NO + O₃ → NO₂* + O₂; excited NO₂* emits photons at 600–875 nm | Photomultiplier Tube (PMT) counts |
| **CO** | **Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR)** | CO absorbs IR at **4.67 μm** (fundamental C–O stretch) | Differential IR detector output |
| **O₃** | **UV Photometric** | O₃ absorbs UV at **253.7 nm** (Hartley band) | Beer–Lambert absorbance |
| **VOCs** | **PID (Photo-Ionization Detector)** | UV lamp (10.6 eV) ionizes VOC molecules | Ion-current measured |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All equations, constants, and units required for KTU 2024 ESE on Air Monitoring:**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Constants | Application |
|---|------------------|-----------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Particulate concentration | $C = \dfrac{W_f - W_i}{V}$ | $\mu g/m^3$ | Gravimetric PM |
| 2 | Beta attenuation (Beer–Lambert) | $I = I_0 e^{-\mu_m \rho_a x}$ | $\mu_m \approx 0.25\ cm^2/g$ (C-14 source) | PM mass loading |
| 3 | Sampling flow conversion (STP) | $V_{STP} = V_{actual} \times \dfrac{P_{act}}{P_{std}} \times \dfrac{T_{std}}{T_{act}}$ | $P_{std} = 101.325\ kPa,\ T_{std} = 298.15\ K$ | Correct flow to standard state |
| 4 | Chemiluminescence intensity | $I = K \cdot [NO] \cdot [O_3]$ | $K$ = reaction constant | NO/NOₓ analyzer |
| 5 | NDIR output (Lambert–Beer) | $A = \varepsilon \cdot l \cdot C$ | $\varepsilon$ = molar absorptivity | CO, CO₂, HC |
| 6 | AQI computation (sub-index) | $I_p = \dfrac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}}(C_p - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$ | $C_p$ = measured pollutant concentration | Indian AQI |
| 7 | Sampling efficiency (isokinetic) | $\eta_{iso} = 1 - \left( \dfrac{v_s - v_a}{v_a} \right)^2$ | $v_s$ = stream velocity, $v_a$ = probe velocity | Stack sampling |
| 8 | PM cut-point (PM₁₀) | $D_{aero,50} = 10\ \mu m$ aerodynamic | — | Impactor design |
| 9 | Optical scattering (Mie, large dₚ) | $I_s \propto d_p^6$ | — | Laser PM sensor |
| 10 | Henry's law (wet scrubber / impinger) | $C_{aq} = K_H \cdot P_{gas}$ | $K_H$ = Henry constant | Gas absorption in sampling train |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical constant to remember:** Standard reference for Indian NAAQS: **25 °C (298.15 K)** and **1 atm (101.325 kPa)**. Any volumetric flow value not corrected to STP is considered *invalid* for KTU numerical problems.

## 2.5 Why Each Technique Is Used — Engineering Rationale

- **Gravimetric** is the *gold standard* for PM because it is direct, calibration-free, and legally defensible in court — but it is slow (24 h sample).
- **Beta Attenuation** allows *near-real-time* PM mass measurement (hourly resolution), ideal for CEMS.
- **NDIR** is robust, non-consumable, and selective for IR-active species → preferred for **CO** in ambient and in-vehicle monitoring.
- **Chemiluminescence** has detection limits down to **parts-per-billion (ppb)** → mandated for NOₓ compliance.
- **UV Photometry** has sub-ppb sensitivity for O₃ → forms the basis of the **Dasibi O₃ Analyzer**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Standardization of Sampled Air Volume (STP Correction)

The High Volume Sampler (HVS) draws air at ambient temperature $T_{act}$ and ambient pressure $P_{act}$. However, pollutant concentrations must be reported at **Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP)** so that results from different geographies and seasons are comparable.

**Step 1 — Write the ideal gas equation for actual and standard conditions:**

$$P_{act} V_{act} = n R T_{act}$$
$$P_{std} V_{std} = n R T_{std}$$

**Step 2 — Divide the two equations (same moles of gas sampled):**

$$\dfrac{V_{std}}{V_{act}} = \dfrac{P_{act}}{P_{std}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{std}}{T_{act}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the standardized volume:**

$$V_{std} = V_{act} \cdot \dfrac{P_{act}}{P_{std}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{std}}{T_{act}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values for a typical CPCB run (Delhi winter, $T_{act} = 290\ K$, $P_{act} = 100\ kPa$):**

$$V_{std} = V_{act} \cdot \dfrac{100}{101.325} \cdot \dfrac{298.15}{290}$$
$$V_{std} = V_{act} \cdot 0.9870 \cdot 1.0281 \approx V_{act} \cdot 1.0146$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** A measured $V_{act} = 24\ m^3$ at winter conditions becomes $V_{std} = 24.35\ m^3$ — a **+1.46%** correction. Ignoring this correction introduces systematic bias in computed PM concentration.

> [!TIP]
> This is a *favorite 3-mark KTU question*: *"A Hi-Vol sampler runs for 24 h at 1.13 m³/min. Ambient T = 35 °C, P = 0.985 atm. Compute the standardized volume."*

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Beta Attenuation Mass Loading Derivation

The relationship between the number of β-particles reaching the detector and the mass of PM deposited on the filter must be derived from exponential attenuation.

**Step 1 — Differential form of attenuation** (each infinitesimally thin layer absorbs a fraction proportional to its mass):

$$dI = -\mu_m \cdot (dm) \cdot I$$

where $dm$ is the incremental mass per unit area (g/cm²) and $\mu_m$ is the **mass absorption coefficient** (cm²/g).

**Step 2 — Integrate from 0 to total mass loading $\rho_a$:**

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \dfrac{dI}{I} = -\mu_m \int_0^{\rho_a} dm$$
$$\ln\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\mu_m \rho_a$$
$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu_m \rho_a}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for mass loading $\rho_a$:**

$$\rho_a = -\dfrac{1}{\mu_m} \ln\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right) \quad \left[\dfrac{g}{cm^2}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Convert mass loading to mass concentration** using known sampled volume $V$ and deposit area $A$:

$$C_{PM} = \dfrac{\rho_a \cdot A}{V} \quad \left[\dfrac{g}{cm^3}\right] = \dfrac{\mu g}{m^3}\ \text{(with unit conversion)}$$

**Step 5 — Linear approximation** for small $\rho_a$ (typical of 1-hr samples):

$$e^{-\mu_m \rho_a} \approx 1 - \mu_m \rho_a$$
$$\rho_a \approx \dfrac{1}{\mu_m}\left(1 - \dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$$

This linearization simplifies the electronic readout — the detector current is *directly* proportional to the mass deposited on the filter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical values:** For a ¹⁴C source ($\beta$ endpoint energy ≈ 156 keV) interacting with carbonaceous aerosol, $\mu_m \approx 0.22\ cm^2/g$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — NDIR Signal Generation

The NDIR analyzer operates on the dual-beam, single-wavelength principle.

**Step 1 — Beer–Lambert law for the sample cell:**

$$I = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\varepsilon l C}$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the molar absorptivity of the target gas (CO at 4.67 μm: $\varepsilon \approx 0.18\ m^2/mg$), $l$ is the optical path length, and $C$ is concentration.

**Step 2 — Take the absorbance:**

$$A = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon l C$$

**Step 3 — Dual-beam compensation** (reference cell filled with N₂ — non-absorbing):

The optical bench has a chopper alternating the IR beam between the *sample cell* (containing ambient air) and the *reference cell* (containing pure N₂). The detector sees an AC signal:

$$V_{AC} \propto I_0 \left(1 - 10^{-\varepsilon l C}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Linearize for low $C$** (typical ambient CO is a few ppm → $\varepsilon l C \ll 1$):

$$V_{AC} \approx I_0 \cdot \varepsilon l C \cdot \ln(10) = 2.303\ I_0 \varepsilon l C$$

The signal voltage is thus *linearly* proportional to the CO concentration.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Four Major Analyzers

| Property | Chemiluminescent (NOₓ) | NDIR (CO) | UV Photometric (O₃) | Beta Attenuation (PM) |
|----------|----------------------|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Principle** | Chemiluminescence reaction | IR absorption | UV absorption | β-particle attenuation |
| **Detection Limit** | 1 ppb | 100 ppb | 1 ppb | 5 μg/m³ (1-hr) |
| **Response Time** | 30 s | 60 s | 20 s | 60 min |
| **Interferences** | Ethylene, H₂S | H₂O, CO₂ | Aerosols, SO₂ | Radioactive decay |
| **Calibration Gas** | NO in N₂ (NIST-traceable) | CO in N₂ | O₃ generator (UV lamp) | Reference gravimetric |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Answer-Structuring Tip:** Always start a question like *"Explain the working of an NOₓ analyzer"* with **(1) Principle, (2) Block Diagram, (3) Chemical Reaction, (4) Detector, (5) Application**. Marks are awarded per point — a single 14-mark question expects all five.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Q:** An air sample is drawn through a Hi-Vol sampler at a flow rate of 1.10 m³/min for 8 hours. The temperature is 38 °C and the pressure is 0.97 atm. The filter gained 412 mg of particulate matter. Calculate (i) the STP-corrected volume, and (ii) the PM concentration in μg/m³.

**Solution:**

**Step (i) — Find $V_{act}$:**
$$V_{act} = 1.10\ m^3/min \times 480\ min = 528\ m^3$$

**Step (ii) — Standardize the volume:**

$$V_{std} = 528 \times \dfrac{0.97}{1} \times \dfrac{298.15}{311.15}$$
$$V_{std} = 528 \times 0.97 \times 0.9582 \approx 490.6\ m^3$$

**Step (iii) — Compute PM concentration:**

$$C_{PM} = \dfrac{412 \times 10^3\ \mu g}{490.6\ m^3} \approx 840\ \mu g/m^3$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (KTU Examiners' Note):** Students forget to convert milligrams to micrograms OR they divide by $V_{act}$ instead of $V_{std}$. The second error overstates the concentration by ≈ **7.6%** in this example.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Station

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Atmospheric Air Inlet] --> A2[PM10 Cyclone Separator]
    A2 --> A3[Fine PM Filter and Nafion Dryer]
    A3 --> A4[Multi-Gas Manifold]
    A4 --> A5[SO2 Analyzer UVF]
    A4 --> A6[NOx Analyzer Chemiluminescence]
    A4 --> A7[CO Analyzer NDIR]
    A4 --> A8[O3 Analyzer UV Photometric]
    A9[Hi-Vol PM2.5 Sampler] --> A10[Gravimetric Weighing Module]
    A10 --> A11[Concentration Calculator]
    A5 --> A12[Data Acquisition and DSP]
    A6 --> A12
    A7 --> A12
    A8 --> A12
    A11 --> A12
    A12 --> A13[AQI Computation Engine]
    A13 --> A14[Cloud Server and Display]
    A14 --> A15[Public Dashboard and Regulatory Reporting]
```

**Description of flow:**
- The ambient air first enters a **cyclone separator** that removes particles larger than 10 μm by centrifugal action.
- The dried, PM-filtered air stream is split by a **multi-port solenoid manifold** to feed four parallel gas analyzers in sequence.
- A separate **Hi-Vol PM₂.₅ sampler** runs gravimetric in parallel for 24 hours as the reference method.
- All analog outputs are digitized, time-stamped, and pushed to a **central AQI engine** that computes the composite index per the Indian CPCB formula.
- Data is mirrored to a **cloud server** and rendered to a public dashboard (e.g., the CPCB "Sameer" app).

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — NDIR Gas Analyzer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[IR Source Heated Filament] --> B2[Optical Chopper Modulation]
    B2 --> B3[Sample Cell Flowing Air]
    B3 --> B4[Reference Cell Filled N2]
    B4 --> B5[Optical Bandpass Filter at 4.67 um]
    B5 --> B6[Pyroelectric or Thermopile Detector]
    B6 --> B7[Lock-In Amplifier Synchronous to Chopper]
    B7 --> B8[Microcontroller Linearization]
    B8 --> B9[Digital Display and 4-20 mA Output]
```

**Working logic:**
- The **optical chopper** modulates the IR beam at ≈ 80 Hz, allowing AC coupling and elimination of DC drift.
- The sample cell contains ambient air with CO; the reference cell contains pure N₂.
- The detector receives less IR in the sample half of the cycle → produces a sine-wave output whose amplitude is proportional to **CO concentration**.
- A **lock-in amplifier** demodulates this signal with extreme noise rejection.

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Chemiluminescent NOₓ Analyzer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Ambient Air Sample] --> C2[NO Channel Reaction Chamber with O3]
    C2 --> C3[Photomultiplier Tube PMT]
    C1 --> C4[NO2 to NO Converter Molybdenum Heated]
    C4 --> C5[NOx Reaction Chamber with O3]
    C5 --> C3
    C3 --> C6[Photon Counting Electronics]
    C6 --> C7[Microprocessor Subtracts NO from NOx to Yield NO2]
    C7 --> C8[Display and Output]
```

**Working logic:**
- The first chamber measures **NO** directly (chemiluminescent reaction).
- The second channel passes the air through a **molybdenum converter** at ≈ 325 °C that reduces NO₂ → NO.
- Subtracting the NO signal from the NOₓ signal yields **NO₂** concentration.
- The PMT operates in photon-counting mode for sub-ppb detection.

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — High-Vol PM Sampler

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Ambient Air] --> D2[Shelter and Inlet Head 10 cut PM10]
    D2 --> D3[Brushless Blower 1.0 to 1.5 m3 per min]
    D3 --> D4[8x10 inch Glass Fiber Filter]
    D4 --> D5[Flow Orifice and Manometer]
    D5 --> D6[Elapsed Time Meter 24 h]
    D6 --> D7[Post Sampling Weighing on Microbalance]
    D7 --> D8[Concentration Calculator mass per volume]
```

**Working logic:**
- The blower draws ambient air through a pre-weighed **glass-fiber filter** for 24 hours.
- The manometer monitors flow to ensure the cut-point at PM₁₀ is maintained.
- After sampling, the filter is conditioned (24 h, 25 °C, 50% RH) and re-weighed.
- The PM mass per standard cubic meter is reported.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"List any three methods of measurement of SO₂ in ambient air and state the principle of the West–Gaeke method."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three methods for ambient SO₂ measurement are:
1. **West–Gaeke Method (Reference)** — SO₂ is absorbed in a 0.04 M **sodium tetrachloromercurate (TCM)** solution, forming a stable dichlorosulfitomercurate complex. The complex reacts with **p-rosaniline dye** and **formaldehyde** to form a red-violet **pararosaniline methylsulfonic acid** whose absorbance is measured spectrophotometrically at **548 nm**. (1 mark for absorption chemistry, 1 mark for reaction, 1 mark for measurement at 548 nm.)
2. **UV Fluorescence Method** — SO₂ molecules excited at 190–230 nm re-emit fluorescence at 320–400 nm; intensity is proportional to SO₂ concentration.
3. **Pulsed Fluorescence Analyzer** — pulsed UV excitation eliminates background, achieving sub-ppb detection limits.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**"Define AQI. List any four pollutants considered in the Indian National AQI computation."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Air Quality Index (AQI)** is a single composite number that summarizes the cumulative impact of multiple ambient pollutants on human health. (1 mark)
The **eight pollutants** considered in the Indian National AQI (CPCB, 2014) are: (2 marks)
1. Particulate Matter — **PM₂.₅**
2. Particulate Matter — **PM₁₀**
3. **NO₂** (Nitrogen Dioxide)
4. **SO₂** (Sulphur Dioxide)
5. **CO** (Carbon Monoxide)
6. **O₃** (Ozone)
7. **NH₃** (Ammonia)
8. **Pb** (Lead)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **Chemiluminescent NOₓ analyzer**. Discuss its detection limit, interferences, and the role of the molybdenum converter. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Describe the **High-Volume Air Sampler (Hi-Vol)** method for measurement of **PM₁₀**. State the equation for computing PM concentration, and the standardization correction for volume. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Q.A(a) Chemiluminescent NOₓ Analyzer (7 Marks)

**(i) Principle (1 mark):**
NO reacts with O₃ to form NO₂* in an excited electronic state, which relaxes to the ground state by emitting a photon in the **600–875 nm** red-IR range. The photon yield is proportional to the **NO concentration** in the sample.

$$\text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{NO}_2^{*} + \text{O}_2$$
$$\text{NO}_2^{*} \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + h\nu \quad (\lambda \approx 880\ nm)$$

**(ii) Block Diagram Explanation (3 marks):**
- **Reaction Chamber**: ambient air is mixed with generated O₃ (from an internal ozonator) at low pressure (≈ 25–100 torr) to enhance collision rate.
- **Optical Filter**: passes the emission band while blocking stray light.
- **PMT (Photomultiplier Tube)**: detects single photons with high gain; cooled to reduce dark current.
- **Photon-Counting Electronics**: counts pulses; count rate ∝ [NO].
- **Microprocessor**: subtracts NO channel from NOₓ channel to yield NO₂.

**(iii) Molybdenum Converter (2 marks):**
A separate air stream is passed over a **heated molybdenum catalyst** at ≈ 325 °C, which quantitatively reduces **NO₂ → NO** without affecting other N-species (HONO, HNO₃ partially). The NOₓ channel therefore measures **NO + converted NO₂**; subtraction gives [NO₂].

**(iv) Detection Limit and Interferences (1 mark):**
- **Detection limit** ≈ **0.05 ppb** (sub-ppb range) — best among reference methods.
- **Interferences**: **Ethylene, H₂S, and other hydrocarbons** also react with O₃ producing chemiluminescence → corrected by a **scrubber** (e.g., **Purafil** or **stainless-steel converter at 0 °C**) in the NO channel.

---

#### Model Answer — Q.A(b) Hi-Vol PM₁₀ Sampler (7 Marks)

**(i) Sampler Components (2 marks):**
A high-volume sampler consists of: an **inlet head** (with a size-selective impactor that removes particles > 10 μm by inertial impaction), a **brushless blower** (1.0–1.5 m³/min), a **filter holder** (8″ × 10″ glass-fiber or quartz filter), a **flow-measuring orifice with manometer**, and an **elapsed-time meter**.

**(ii) Operating Procedure (2 marks):**
1. The filter is conditioned in a controlled environment (25 °C, 50% RH) for 24 h and weighed on an **analytical balance** (sensitivity 1 μg).
2. The sampler is operated for **24 hours** at constant flow.
3. After sampling, the filter is conditioned again and re-weighed.

**(iii) PM₁₀ Concentration Equation (2 marks):**

$$C_{PM_{10}} = \dfrac{(W_f - W_i) \times 10^6}{V_{std}}\ \left[\mu g/m^3\right]$$

with

$$V_{std} = V_{act} \cdot \dfrac{P_{act}}{P_{std}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{std}}{T_{act}}$$

**(iv) Quality Assurance (1 mark):**
Field blanks, flow audits, and duplicate sampling (10% of stations) are mandated under **NAMP** to ensure data representativeness.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR)** technique for the measurement of **CO** in ambient air. State Beer–Lambert's law, draw the block diagram, and discuss the function of the optical chopper. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Describe the **UV Photometric method** for measuring **ozone (O₃)**. Give the principle, equation, and the **Hartley band** reference. Compare its sensitivity with chemiluminescence. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Q.B(a) NDIR CO Analyzer (7 Marks)

**(i) Principle (2 marks):**
CO has a strong fundamental **C–O stretching absorption** at **4.67 μm** in the mid-IR. The NDIR analyzer passes IR radiation through a sample cell containing ambient air; the fraction of IR absorbed is exponentially related to CO concentration (Beer–Lambert law).

$$A = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon l C$$

**(ii) Block Diagram (3 marks):**
(Refer to NDIR diagram in Section 4.2.) Sequence:
- **IR source** (nichrome wire) → emits broadband IR.
- **Optical chopper** (motor + slotted wheel) → modulates beam at ≈ 80 Hz.
- **Sample cell** (with gas inlet/outlet) and **reference cell** (N₂-filled, sealed).
- **Optical bandpass filter** at **4.67 μm** (interference filter) → blocks other wavelengths.
- **Pyroelectric / thermopile detector** → generates AC voltage proportional to (I_ref − I_sample).
- **Lock-in amplifier** → recovers AC signal with high SNR.
- **Microcontroller** → linearization (since absorbance is logarithmic) → 4–20 mA output.

**(iii) Optical Chopper Function (2 marks):**
The chopper produces an **alternating IR beam** between sample and reference paths. This enables:
1. **AC coupling** — eliminates 1/f noise and thermal drift of the source.
2. **Dual-beam cancellation** — compensates for source intensity fluctuation, detector dark current, and dust on optics.
3. The detector output is a sine wave at the chopper frequency; amplitude = differential absorption ∝ CO concentration.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1 — NDIR vs FTIR:** Students frequently confuse the two. NDIR uses a *discrete optical filter* at one specific wavelength; FTIR uses an *interferometer* to scan a wide spectrum. They are NOT the same.

---

#### Model Answer — Q.B(b) UV Photometric Ozone Analyzer (7 Marks)

**(i) Principle (2 marks):**
Ozone exhibits a strong UV absorption band called the **Hartley band**, peaking at **253.7 nm** (extinction coefficient $\varepsilon \approx 1.32 \times 10^5\ L\cdot mol^{-1}\cdot cm^{-1}$). The UV photometric analyzer measures the differential absorption between a sample and a reference (ozone-scrubbed) cell.

**(ii) Equation (2 marks):**
Using Beer–Lambert:

$$A = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_{ref}}{I_{sample}}\right) = \varepsilon l [O_3]$$

Solving for concentration:

$$[O_3] = \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon l} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_{ref}}{I_{sample}}\right) \quad [\text{ppb or } \mu g/m^3]$$

**(iii) Block Diagram (2 marks):**
- **UV lamp** (low-pressure mercury, emits 253.7 nm).
- **Beam splitter** → sample cell and reference cell.
- **Reference cell** contains a **ozone scrubber** (charcoal or KI) that removes all O₃.
- **UV photodiodes** at each cell output.
- **Log-ratio amplifier** computes absorbance.
- **Microcontroller** linearizes and outputs concentration.

**(iv) Sensitivity Comparison (1 mark):**
- UV Photometry: detection limit ≈ **1 ppb**; range 1–1000 ppb; **non-destructive** to O₃.
- Chemiluminescence: detection limit ≈ **0.05 ppb**; more sensitive but **O₃ is consumed** in the reaction.
- UV photometry is the **reference method** for ambient O₃ under US EPA (EQOA-0992-087) and IS 5182 (Part IX).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2 — O₃ Interferences:** Aromatic VOCs (benzene, toluene) and certain Hg vapors absorb at 253.7 nm. The reference cell must be *chemically identical* to the sample cell except for the absence of O₃ — the analyzer assumes *only O₃* differs between the two paths. This assumption fails in polluted urban air and requires correction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AIR MONITORING — Quick-Fire KTU Revision List:**

- ✅ Air monitoring = measurement of gaseous and particulate pollutants to compare with **NAAQS / WHO** limits.
- ✅ Two pollution categories: **Gaseous (SO₂, NOₓ, CO, O₃, VOCs, NH₃)** and **Particulate (PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, TSPM)**.
- ✅ **Reference method** = legally defensible, used for calibration. **Equivalent method** = faster, automated, EPA-approved.
- ✅ **Hi-Vol Sampler** = gravimetric PM₁₀/TSPM; flow 1.0–1.5 m³/min; 24-h sample; filter weighed before/after.
- ✅ **PM concentration formula**: $C = (W_f - W_i)/V_{std}$, with $V_{std}$ corrected to **25 °C, 1 atm**.
- ✅ **SO₂ reference method** = **West–Gaeke**, colorimetric, **p-rosaniline** at **548 nm**.
- ✅ **NOₓ reference method** = **Chemiluminescence**, NO + O₃ → NO₂* (λ ≈ 880 nm), PMT detector.
- ✅ **CO reference method** = **NDIR**, λ = **4.67 μm**, Beer–Lambert absorbance.
- ✅ **O₃ reference method** = **UV Photometric**, λ = **253.7 nm** (Hartley band).
- ✅ **Beta Attenuation** for real-time PM: $I = I_0 e^{-\mu_m \rho_a}$ — linear in mass loading for small deposits.
- ✅ **CEMS** = Continuous Emission Monitoring System; mandatory for stacks > certain capacity.
- ✅ **Indian AQI** considers **8 pollutants**; sub-index formula: $I_p = \dfrac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}}(C_p - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$.
- ✅ **Isokinetic sampling** in stacks requires probe velocity = stream velocity; deviations introduce bias for larger particles.
- ✅ **Standard reference conditions (STP)** for India: **298.15 K, 101.325 kPa** — must be in every numerical.
- ✅ **Common interferences**: H₂S, ethylene (chemiluminescent NOₓ); H₂O, CO₂ (NDIR); aromatic VOCs (UV O₃).
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark questions, always include: **Principle → Block Diagram → Equation → Detector → Application → Limitations**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Examiner's Advice:** A typical 14-mark question expects at least **2 diagrams** (block diagram + waveform or layout) and at least **1 formula derivation**. Mere textual explanation without visuals will *cap your score at 8–9 marks* regardless of content quality. Practice drawing the four block diagrams in Section 4 *by hand* before every test.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Ambient Air Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT832 - ENVIRONMENTAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Ambient Air Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Ambient Air Quality (AAQ)?

**Ambient Air** is the outdoor air surrounding us—the atmosphere in which humans, animals, and plants live. The **Ambient Air Quality** refers to the concentration of pollutants present in this outdoor air, measured against defined standards to determine its purity and safety.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Ambient Air Quality is the quantitative assessment of the levels of specific gaseous and particulate pollutants present in the surrounding outdoor atmosphere, expressed in micrograms per cubic meter (µg/m³) or milligrams per cubic meter (mg/m³), and evaluated against National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) prescribed by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB), Government of India, under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986.*

### Conceptual Analogy – The "Air Health Report Card"
Think of ambient air quality measurement as a **doctor's health check-up for the atmosphere**—just as a doctor measures your blood pressure, sugar, and cholesterol to grade your health, environmental engineers measure pollutants to grade the air. If the readings cross safe limits, the air gets a "poor health" rating, and a "pollution alert" is issued.

> [!NOTE]
> The term **Ambient** literally means "surrounding on all sides" (from Latin *ambire*, to go around). Hence, ambient air is the envelope of air immediately surrounding an observation point.

## 1.2 Air Quality Index (AQI)

The **Air Quality Index (AQI)** is a single, composite number that summarizes the overall pollution level by converting the measured concentrations of multiple individual pollutants into a unified scale from 0 to 500 (in the Indian CPCB system) or 0–500 (US EPA system).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** *The Air Quality Index is a piecewise linear transformation of measured pollutant concentrations into a dimensionless number on a scale of 0–500, where higher values indicate greater health risk, derived using the breakpoint sub-index algorithm defined by the CPCB (India) and US EPA.*

## 1.3 Criteria Pollutants (The "Usual Suspects")

The CPCB officially monitors **8 criteria pollutants**, and 1 additional pollutant group (VOCs in some cases):

| # | Pollutant | Symbol | Primary Health Concern |
|---|-----------|--------|------------------------|
| 1 | Particulate Matter (≤ 2.5 µm) | **PM2.5** | Deep lung penetration, cardiovascular |
| 2 | Particulate Matter (≤ 10 µm) | **PM10** | Respiratory irritation |
| 3 | Sulphur Dioxide | **SO₂** | Acid rain, bronchitis |
| 4 | Nitrogen Dioxide | **NO₂** | Smog, respiratory disease |
| 5 | Carbon Monoxide | **CO** | Oxygen transport blockage |
| 6 | Ozone | **O₃** | Photochemical smog |
| 7 | Ammonia | **NH₃** | Mucous membrane damage |
| 8 | Lead | **Pb** | Neurotoxic, especially children |
| 9 | Carbon Dioxide (now NAAQS 2024) | **CO₂** | Greenhouse, climate change |

> [!NOTE]
> Out of the 9, only **PM2.5, PM10, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, NH₃, and Pb** are the legally notified **NAAQS criteria pollutants** under the CPCB NAAQS notification, 2009 (revised 2024 draft).

## 1.4 National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)

The **NAAQS** are legally binding maximum permissible concentrations of pollutants in ambient air, prescribed by the CPCB. They are listed in **Table 1** of the NAAQS Notification 2009 (and 2024 revision).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students must memorize the NAAQS values for **PM2.5, PM10, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃** at both **24-hour** and **annual** averaging periods. KTU questions often test these values directly.

## 1.5 AQI Categories (CPCB Scale, India)

The CPCB AQI scale (introduced **2014**, revised **2018**) divides the 0–500 range into **8 health concern bands**:

| AQI Range | Category | Health Implication |
|-----------|----------|---------------------|
| 0 – 50 | **Good** | Minimal impact |
| 51 – 100 | **Satisfactory** | Minor breathing discomfort for sensitive people |
| 101 – 200 | **Moderate** | Breathing discomfort for people with lung/heart disease |
| 201 – 300 | **Poor** | Breathing discomfort on prolonged exposure |
| 301 – 400 | **Very Poor** | Respiratory illness on prolonged exposure |
| 401 – 500 | **Severe** | Health impacts even on healthy people |
| > 500 | **Hazardous / Emergency** | Serious health effects |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of an Air Quality Monitoring System

A complete ambient air quality measurement system consists of **three functional blocks**:

1. **Sampling Block** – Draws ambient air to the sensor at controlled flow rate.
2. **Analysis Block** – Detects pollutant-specific physical/chemical change.
3. **Data Acquisition & Reporting Block** – Converts signal to concentration, computes AQI, transmits to central server.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flow Rate Standard:** All NAAQS gaseous measurements are referenced to **standard conditions: 25 °C and 1 atm (101.325 kPa)**. The corrected concentration formula is shown in the formula sheet.

## 2.2 The Master Equation: AQI Sub-Index Calculation

The AQI is computed using the **CPCB/US EPA Piecewise Linear Breakpoint Method**. For each pollutant *p*, a sub-index $I_p$ is calculated, and the **overall AQI is the maximum** of all sub-indices (the dominant pollutant governs).

**General Form:**

$$I_p = \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} \cdot (C_p - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$$

Where:
- $I_p$ = Sub-AQI value for pollutant $p$ (dimensionless)
- $C_p$ = **Truncated** measured concentration of pollutant $p$ (µg/m³ or mg/m³)
- $BP_{Hi}$ = Concentration breakpoint just **greater than or equal to** $C_p$
- $BP_{Lo}$ = Concentration breakpoint just **less than or equal to** $C_p$
- $I_{Hi}$ = AQI value corresponding to $BP_{Hi}$
- $I_{Lo}$ = AQI value corresponding to $BP_{Lo}$

**Overall AQI:**

$$AQI = \max(I_{PM2.5}, I_{PM10}, I_{SO_2}, I_{NO_2}, I_{CO}, I_{O_3}, I_{NH_3}, I_{Pb})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Truncation Rule:** Concentrations are truncated to the number of decimal places given in the breakpoint table. For PM2.5 and PM10: truncate to integer (e.g., 87.9 → 87). For O₃ (8-hr): truncate to 3 decimals.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Expression | Units | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AQI Sub-Index (Piecewise Linear) | $I_p = \dfrac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} (C_p - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$ | dimensionless | AQI computation |
| 2 | Overall AQI | $AQI = \max(I_p)$ over all pollutants | dimensionless | Single health indicator |
| 3 | Concentration Correction (STP) | $C_{STP} = C_{measured} \times \dfrac{P_{measured}}{P_{STP}} \times \dfrac{T_{STP}}{T_{measured}}$ | µg/m³ | Reference to standard conditions |
| 4 | Beer-Lambert Law (Gas Absorption) | $A = \varepsilon \cdot b \cdot c = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | dimensionless | UV/IR gas analyzers |
| 5 | Beta Attenuation (PM) | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu \cdot m}$ | count rate | PM mass measurement |
| 6 | Mass collected (gravimetric) | $m = (W_f - W_i)$ where W = filter weight | µg | Reference PM method |
| 7 | Sampling Volume (Standard) | $V_{std} = Q \cdot t \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_{std}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{std}}{T}$ | m³ | Active sampler flow |
| 8 | Conversion (ppm ↔ µg/m³) | $C(\mu g/m^3) = C(ppm) \times \dfrac{MW}{24.45}$ | µg/m³ at 25 °C | Gas unit conversion |
| 9 | TEOM Frequency Shift (PM) | $\Delta f \propto m$ (mass on oscillating element) | Hz | Real-time PM mass |
| 10 | Chemiluminescence Intensity (NOx) | $I \propto [NO_2^*] \propto [NO_x]$ | photons/s | NO, NO₂ detection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Factor 24.45** in Equation 8 comes from the **molar volume of air at 25 °C and 1 atm = 24.45 L/mol**. This is a frequently tested value in KTU problems.

## 2.4 Pollutant-Specific Measurement Principles

### 2.4.1 Particulate Matter (PM2.5 and PM10)
- **Reference Method:** Gravimetric (filter-based)
- **Continuous Methods:** Beta Attenuation Mass Monitor (BAM), TEOM, Optical Light Scattering

### 2.4.2 Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)
- **Reference Method:** West-Gaeke (pararosaniline colorimetric)
- **Continuous Method:** UV Fluorescence

### 2.4.3 Nitrogen Dioxide (NO₂)
- **Reference Method:** Saltzman (wet chemical) / Griess-Saltzman
- **Continuous Method:** Chemiluminescence

### 2.4.4 Carbon Monoxide (CO)
- **Reference Method:** Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR) Spectrophotometry
- **Continuous:** NDIR / Gas Filter Correlation

### 2.4.5 Ozone (O₃)
- **Reference Method:** UV Photometry at **254 nm**
- **Continuous:** Same UV photometric principle

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 254 nm for Ozone?** This wavelength corresponds to a strong absorption maximum of O₃ (Hartley band), with very low interference from other atmospheric gases—making it the "fingerprint" wavelength for O₃ detection.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Smart Cities Mission** uses AQI as a daily public health bulletin.
- **Industrial Compliance** (e.g., refineries, power plants) requires continuous emission monitoring tied to AAQ data.
- **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** studies depend on baseline AAQ data.
- **International Reporting** (e.g., to WHO, UNFCCC) requires standardized measurement protocols.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Concentration Correction to STP

Ambient air monitoring instruments measure concentration at local temperature $T$ and pressure $P$. To compare against the NAAQS (which are defined at STP: 25 °C, 1 atm), we must normalize.

**Step 1 – Ideal Gas Law Statement**

The molar concentration (moles per unit volume) of an ideal gas varies inversely with absolute temperature and directly with pressure.

$$\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$$

**Step 2 – Setup**

For the same number of moles of pollutant in a given volume, when temperature and pressure change, the volume changes. The pollutant mass remains the same. So:

$$C_{STP} = \frac{m}{V_{STP}} = \frac{m}{V_{measured}} \cdot \frac{V_{measured}}{V_{STP}}$$

**Step 3 – Apply Ideal Gas Law**

$$\frac{V_{measured}}{V_{STP}} = \frac{P_{STP}}{P_{measured}} \cdot \frac{T_{measured}}{T_{STP}}$$

**Step 4 – Substitute**

$$C_{STP} = C_{measured} \times \frac{P_{STP}}{P_{measured}} \cdot \frac{T_{measured}}{T_{STP}}$$

This is the **final correction equation** for converting ambient concentration to standard reference conditions. Temperatures **must be in Kelvin**.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Example: AQI Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
The following 24-hour average concentrations were measured at a CPCB monitoring station in Kochi:

| Pollutant | Measured Concentration |
|-----------|------------------------|
| PM2.5 | 78 µg/m³ |
| PM10 | 142 µg/m³ |
| SO₂ | 38 µg/m³ |
| NO₂ | 55 µg/m³ |
| CO | 1.8 mg/m³ |
| O₃ (8-hr) | 89 µg/m³ |

**Step 1 – Apply Truncation Rules**
- PM2.5: 78.0 → 78 (integer)
- PM10: 142.0 → 142 (integer)
- SO₂: 38.0 → 38 (integer)
- NO₂: 55.0 → 55 (integer)
- CO: 1.80 (2 decimal places)
- O₃: 89.000 (3 decimal places)

**Step 2 – Look Up Breakpoints (CPCB 2014 / 2018 Table)**

| Pollutant | C_p | BP_Lo | BP_Hi | I_Lo | I_Hi |
|-----------|-----|-------|-------|------|------|
| PM2.5 | 78 | 61 | 90 | 101 | 200 |
| PM10 | 142 | 101 | 250 | 201 | 300 |
| SO₂ | 38 | 31 | 60 | 101 | 200 |
| NO₂ | 40 (Note: 55 falls in 41–80 range → BP_Lo=41, BP_Hi=80) | 41 | 80 | 101 | 200 |
| CO | 1.8 | 1.1 | 2.0 | 101 | 200 |
| O₃ (8-hr) | 89 | 50 | 100 | 101 | 200 |

> [!NOTE]
> For NO₂: 55 lies between 41 and 80, hence BP_Lo = 41, BP_Hi = 80, I_Lo = 101, I_Hi = 200. The earlier value of 40 is shown for column clarity—replaced correctly in the calculation.

**Step 3 – Compute Sub-Index for PM2.5**

$$I_{PM2.5} = \frac{200 - 101}{90 - 61} (78 - 61) + 101$$

$$I_{PM2.5} = \frac{99}{29} \times 17 + 101$$

$$I_{PM2.5} = 3.4138 \times 17 + 101$$

$$I_{PM2.5} = 58.03 + 101 = 159.03 \approx 159$$

**Step 4 – Compute Sub-Index for PM10**

$$I_{PM10} = \frac{300 - 201}{250 - 101} (142 - 101) + 201$$

$$I_{PM10} = \frac{99}{149} \times 41 + 201$$

$$I_{PM10} = 0.6644 \times 41 + 201$$

$$I_{PM10} = 27.24 + 201 = 228.24 \approx 228$$

**Step 5 – Compute Sub-Index for SO₂**

$$I_{SO_2} = \frac{200 - 101}{60 - 31} (38 - 31) + 101$$

$$I_{SO_2} = \frac{99}{29} \times 7 + 101 = 3.4138 \times 7 + 101$$

$$I_{SO_2} = 23.90 + 101 = 124.90 \approx 125$$

**Step 6 – Compute Sub-Index for NO₂**

$$I_{NO_2} = \frac{200 - 101}{80 - 41} (55 - 41) + 101$$

$$I_{NO_2} = \frac{99}{39} \times 14 + 101 = 2.5385 \times 14 + 101$$

$$I_{NO_2} = 35.54 + 101 = 136.54 \approx 137$$

**Step 7 – Compute Sub-Index for CO**

$$I_{CO} = \frac{200 - 101}{2.0 - 1.1} (1.8 - 1.1) + 101$$

$$I_{CO} = \frac{99}{0.9} \times 0.7 + 101 = 110 \times 0.7 + 101$$

$$I_{CO} = 77 + 101 = 178$$

**Step 8 – Compute Sub-Index for O₃**

$$I_{O_3} = \frac{200 - 101}{100 - 50} (89 - 50) + 101$$

$$I_{O_3} = \frac{99}{50} \times 39 + 101 = 1.98 \times 39 + 101$$

$$I_{O_3} = 77.22 + 101 = 178.22 \approx 178$$

**Step 9 – Determine Overall AQI**

$$AQI = \max(159, 228, 125, 137, 178, 178)$$

$$AQI = 228$$

**Step 10 – Identify Category and Dominant Pollutant**

- AQI = 228 → **"Poor"** category (201–300).
- Dominant pollutant = **PM10** (sub-index = 228 = overall AQI).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** PM10 is the "rate-limiting" pollutant. Mitigation actions (e.g., construction dust control, road sweeping) will have the greatest health impact.

## 3.3 Derivation: ppm to µg/m³ Conversion

This conversion is essential in KTU problems where pollutants are given in different units.

**Step 1 – Definition of ppm (volume mixing ratio)**

$$C_{ppm} = \frac{V_{pollutant}}{V_{air}} \times 10^6$$

**Step 2 – Convert volume to moles (using ideal gas law)**

At standard conditions (25 °C, 1 atm), 1 mole of any gas occupies **24.45 L**. Therefore:

$$n_{pollutant} = \frac{V_{pollutant}}{24.45 \times 10^{-3}} \text{ mol} = \frac{C_{ppm} \cdot V_{air}}{10^6 \cdot 24.45 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 3 – Mass of pollutant**

$$m = n_{pollutant} \times MW$$

$$m = \frac{C_{ppm} \cdot V_{air} \cdot MW}{24.45}$$

**Step 4 – Concentration in µg/m³**

$$C_{\mu g/m^3} = \frac{m}{V_{air} \text{ (in m}^3\text{)}} \times 10^6 \mu g / g$$

For $V_{air}$ in m³, after unit cancellations:

$$\boxed{C_{\mu g/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \frac{MW}{24.45}}$$

**Numerical Example:** SO₂ (MW = 64) at 0.05 ppm:

$$C = 0.05 \times \frac{64}{24.45} = 0.05 \times 2.618 = 0.1309 \text{ mg/m}^3 = 130.9 \text{ µg/m}^3$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Pollutant → Principle → Sensor

| Pollutant | Reference Method | Continuous Method | Physical Principle | Wavelength / Key |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PM2.5 / PM10** | Gravimetric (24-hr) | BAM, TEOM, Optical | Mass on filter; beta absorption | Beta source (¹⁴C, ⁸⁵Kr) |
| **SO₂** | West-Gaeke | UV Fluorescence | SO₂ + hν → SO₂* → SO₂ + hν' | Excitation 190–230 nm, Emission ~330 nm |
| **NO₂** | Griess-Saltzman | Chemiluminescence | NO + O₃ → NO₂* + O₂; NO₂* → NO₂ + hν | Chemiluminescent at 600–875 nm |
| **CO** | NDIR | NDIR / GFC | CO absorbs IR at 4.67 µm | 4.67 µm fundamental band |
| **O₃** | UV Photometric | UV Photometric | O₃ + hν → O₂ + O (Beer's law) | 254 nm |
| **NH₃** | Indophenol blue | Chemiluminescence | NH₃ + O₃ → chemiluminescent product | 600–900 nm |
| **Pb** | AAS / ICP-MS | XRF (particulate) | Atomic absorption at 283.3 nm | 283.3 nm |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Air Quality Monitoring Station

This functional architecture shows the data and signal flow inside a Continuous Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Station (CAAQMS) used by CPCB.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ambAir[Ambient Air Inlet] --> inletFilter[Inlet Filter and Rain Cap]
    inletFilter --> manifold[Gas Manifold Splitter]
    manifold --> pmChamber[PM Sampling Chamber]
    manifold --> so2Analyzer[SO2 UV Fluorescence Analyzer]
    manifold --> noxAnalyzer[NOx Chemiluminescence Analyzer]
    manifold --> coAnalyzer[CO NDIR Analyzer]
    manifold --> o3Analyzer[O3 UV Photometric Analyzer]
    manifold --> nh3Analyzer[NH3 Chemiluminescence Analyzer]
    pmChamber --> betaSensor[Beta Attenuation Sensor]
    betaSensor --> pmCPU[PM Mass Processor]
    so2Analyzer --> signalCPU1[Signal Conditioner 1]
    noxAnalyzer --> signalCPU2[Signal Conditioner 2]
    coAnalyzer --> signalCPU3[Signal Conditioner 3]
    o3Analyzer --> signalCPU4[Signal Conditioner 4]
    nh3Analyzer --> signalCPU5[Signal Conditioner 5]
    signalCPU1 --> daq[Data Acquisition Unit]
    signalCPU2 --> daq
    signalCPU3 --> daq
    signalCPU4 --> daq
    signalCPU5 --> daq
    pmCPU --> daq
    daq --> aqiCalc[AQI Computation Module]
    aqiCalc --> display[Local Display and Logger]
    aqiCalc --> gsmModem[GSM or GPRS Modem]
    gsmModem --> cloud[(CPCB Cloud Server)]
    cloud --> publicDashboard[Public Web Dashboard]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Air enters through a single inlet, is split to multiple parallel analyzers (multi-channel design ensures simultaneous measurement). The AQI Computation Module performs the piecewise linear breakpoint calculation (Section 2.2) and the maximum selection (Step 9 of Section 3.2).

## 4.2 Block Diagram: UV Fluorescence SO₂ Analyzer

The most commonly tested instrument in KTU for SO₂ measurement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sampleIn[Sample Gas In] --> refCell[Reference Cell]
    sampleIn --> measCell[Measurement Cell]
    uvLamp[UV Lamp 190 to 230 nm] --> modChopper[Chopper Modulator]
    modChopper --> refCell
    modChopper --> measCell
    refCell --> refDet[Reference Photodetector]
    measCell --> measDet[Measurement Photodetector]
    refDet --> logAmp[Logarithmic Amplifier]
    measDet --> logAmp
    logAmp --> proc[Microprocessor]
    proc --> concOut[Concentration Display ppm or ug per m3]
    proc --> analogOut[Analog Output 4 to 20 mA]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating Principle:** SO₂ absorbs UV light at 190–230 nm, gets excited to SO₂*, which decays by emitting longer-wavelength fluorescence (~330 nm). The reference cell compensates for lamp intensity drift, ensuring long-term stability.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Beta Attenuation PM Mass Monitor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ambAir[Ambient Air] --> sizeSelect[Size Selective Inlet PM2.5 or PM10]
    sizeSelect --> filterTape[Moving Filter Tape]
    betaSrc[C 14 or Kr 85 Beta Source] --> filterTape
    filterTape --> betaDet[Beta Particle Detector]
    betaDet --> countMeter[Count Rate Meter]
    countMeter --> mathUnit[Attenuation to Mass Calculator]
    mathUnit --> display[PM Mass Display ug per m3]
    flowCtrl[Volumetric Flow Controller 1 m3 per hr] --> sizeSelect
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Beer-Lambert for Beta Attenuation:** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu m}$, where $I_0$ is the count rate on clean tape, $I$ is count rate after PM deposit, $\mu$ is the mass absorption coefficient, and $m$ is the areal mass density (µg/cm²). The mass $m$ is recovered from the ratio $I/I_0$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Air Quality Index (AQI). List any four criteria pollutants monitored by CPCB under the National Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Programme (NAMP).**

**Model Answer (12 lines):**
Air Quality Index (AQI) is a numerical scale (0–500) that transforms measured ambient pollutant concentrations into a single health-based number using a piecewise linear breakpoint method. The dominant pollutant (highest sub-index) decides the overall AQI. Higher AQI values indicate greater health risk to the exposed population. The CPCB monitors eight criteria pollutants, of which four are:
1. **PM2.5** – fine particulate matter ≤ 2.5 µm aerodynamic diameter.
2. **PM10** – coarse particulate matter ≤ 10 µm.
3. **SO₂** – Sulphur dioxide from fossil fuel combustion.
4. **NO₂** – Nitrogen dioxide from vehicular and industrial emissions.
The AQI categories are Good, Satisfactory, Moderate, Poor, Very Poor, and Severe.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State Beer-Lambert Law. How is it applied in the measurement of ozone using UV photometry?**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**
Beer-Lambert Law states that the absorbance of light by a gas is proportional to the path length and concentration:

$$A = \varepsilon \cdot b \cdot c = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

In **UV photometry** for ozone, a 254 nm UV beam is passed through a sample cell containing ambient air. O₃ absorbs strongly at this wavelength (Hartley band). The intensity reduction is measured, and concentration is calculated from the absorbance. Reference and measurement cells (with and without O₃ scrubber) are used alternately via a chopper to compensate for lamp drift. This is the official **reference method** prescribed by CPCB for O₃ measurement.

---

## 5.2 KTU Part B: Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working of a **UV Fluorescence Analyzer** for the measurement of SO₂ in ambient air. State the chemical reaction involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following 24-hr average concentrations were observed at a station: PM2.5 = 65 µg/m³, PM10 = 132 µg/m³, SO₂ = 42 µg/m³, NO₂ = 38 µg/m³. Calculate the AQI and identify the dominant pollutant. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution – Part (a):**

The UV Fluorescence Analyzer is the most widely used continuous method for SO₂ measurement under NAMP. **Working Principle:** When SO₂ molecules absorb UV photons in the wavelength range 190–230 nm, they are excited to a higher energy state (SO₂*). These excited molecules relax back to the ground state by emitting fluorescence radiation at a longer wavelength (~330 nm). The intensity of this fluorescence is directly proportional to the SO₂ concentration in the cell.

**Chemical Reaction:**

$$SO_2 + h\nu \text{ (UV, 190–230 nm)} \rightarrow SO_2^* \text{ (excited)}$$

$$SO_2^* \rightarrow SO_2 + h\nu' \text{ (fluorescence, ~330 nm)}$$

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes.)

**Functional Description:**
1. **UV Lamp:** Produces 190–230 nm radiation; usually a zinc or cadmium lamp.
2. **Chopper:** Alternately directs the beam through the reference and measurement cells at low frequency (~100 Hz) to provide auto-zero compensation.
3. **Reference Cell:** Contains non-absorbing gas (e.g., N₂) for lamp intensity reference.
4. **Measurement Cell:** Contains the sample gas (ambient air); SO₂ absorbs UV.
5. **Photodetector:** Measures fluorescence emission at ~330 nm via a bandpass filter.
6. **Logarithmic Amplifier + Microprocessor:** Converts detector signal to concentration in µg/m³ or ppb.

**Typical Detection Range:** 0–500 ppb; Lower Detectable Limit (LDL) ≈ 1 ppb.

**Model Solution – Part (b):**

**Step 1 – Truncate Concentrations** (CPCB truncation rules applied):
- PM2.5: 65 (integer)
- PM10: 132 (integer)
- SO₂: 42 (integer)
- NO₂: 38 (integer)

**Step 2 – Look Up Breakpoints (CPCB AQI Table):**

| Pollutant | C_p | BP_Lo | BP_Hi | I_Lo | I_Hi |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| PM2.5 | 65 | 61 | 90 | 101 | 200 |
| PM10 | 132 | 101 | 250 | 201 | 300 |
| SO₂ | 42 | 31 | 60 | 101 | 200 |
| NO₂ | 38 | 31 | 40 | 101 | 200 |

**Step 3 – Sub-Index for PM2.5:**

$$I_{PM2.5} = \frac{200 - 101}{90 - 61}(65 - 61) + 101 = \frac{99}{29} \times 4 + 101 = 13.66 + 101 = 114.66 \approx 115$$

**Step 4 – Sub-Index for PM10:**

$$I_{PM10} = \frac{300 - 201}{250 - 101}(132 - 101) + 201 = \frac{99}{149} \times 31 + 201 = 20.60 + 201 = 221.60 \approx 222$$

**Step 5 – Sub-Index for SO₂:**

$$I_{SO_2} = \frac{200 - 101}{60 - 31}(42 - 31) + 101 = \frac{99}{29} \times 11 + 101 = 37.55 + 101 = 138.55 \approx 139$$

**Step 6 – Sub-Index for NO₂:**

$$I_{NO_2} = \frac{200 - 101}{40 - 31}(38 - 31) + 101 = \frac{99}{9} \times 7 + 101 = 77 + 101 = 178$$

**Step 7 – Overall AQI:**

$$AQI = \max(115, 222, 139, 178) = 222$$

**Category:** "Poor" (201–300)

**Dominant Pollutant:** PM10 (sub-index 222 = overall AQI)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to **truncate** the concentration to the correct number of decimal places before breakpoint lookup. For PM2.5 and PM10, the integer truncation rule applies; using 65.7 instead of 65 leads to **wrong breakpoint selection** and incorrect AQI. KTU examiners explicitly check this step.

---

### Question B (14 Marks – Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the principle and operation of a **Beta Attenuation Mass Monitor (BAM)** for continuous measurement of PM10 and PM2.5 in ambient air. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relation for converting pollutant concentration from **ppm to µg/m³** at 25 °C and 1 atm. Given: SO₂ concentration = 0.075 ppm at 30 °C and 0.98 atm. Convert to µg/m³ at STP. (Molecular weight of SO₂ = 64 g/mol.) **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution – Part (a):**

The **Beta Attenuation Mass Monitor (BAM)** is the most widely deployed continuous PM monitor by CPCB in India under CAAQMS networks. **Principle:** When a beam of beta particles (electrons) from a radioactive source passes through a deposit of particulate matter on a filter tape, the intensity of the transmitted beam decreases exponentially with the areal mass density of the deposit. This follows the **Beer-Lambert Law** analogy for beta radiation.

**Governing Equation:**

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu \cdot m}$$

Where:
- $I$ = transmitted beta count rate (counts per second) after PM deposit
- $I_0$ = transmitted beta count rate on clean filter (baseline)
- $\mu$ = mass absorption coefficient of beta particles in the deposit (cm²/µg)
- $m$ = areal mass density of PM deposit (µg/cm²)

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.3 of these notes.)

**Functional Description:**
1. **Size-Selective Inlet:** PM10 (100% cutoff at 10 µm) or PM2.5 inlet separates the desired fraction.
2. **Volumetric Flow Controller:** Maintains 1 m³/hr (16.67 L/min) for 24-hr equivalence.
3. **Moving Filter Tape:** Collects PM continuously; advances every hour.
4. **Beta Source:** ¹⁴C (carbon-14) or ⁸⁵Kr; emits low-energy beta particles.
5. **Beta Detector:** Measures transmitted count rate; gives both $I_0$ (clean) and $I$ (loaded).
6. **Microprocessor:** Computes $m$ from $I/I_0$, multiplies by filter area to get total mass, and divides by sampled volume to yield concentration in µg/m³.

**Advantages:** Real-time output, automated, low maintenance, EPA and CPCB equivalent method.
**Disadvantage:** Uses radioactive source (requires regulatory licensing).

**Model Solution – Part (b):**

**Step 1 – ppm to µg/m³ Conversion Formula:**

The volume mixing ratio in ppm is defined as:

$$C_{ppm} = \frac{V_{pollutant}}{V_{air}} \times 10^6$$

At **STP (25 °C, 1 atm)**, one mole of an ideal gas occupies **24.45 L**. Therefore, the number of moles in the pollutant volume is:

$$n = \frac{V_{pollutant}}{24.45 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}}$$

The mass of pollutant is:

$$m = n \times MW = \frac{C_{ppm} \cdot V_{air} \cdot MW}{10^6 \times 24.45 \times 10^{-3}}$$

Dividing by $V_{air}$ in m³ and multiplying by $10^6$ µg/g:

$$C_{\mu g/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \frac{MW}{24.45}$$

**Step 2 – Convert SO₂ at 30 °C, 0.98 atm to STP:**

Measured ppm (independent of T, P for volume ratio) = 0.075 ppm.
First, convert to µg/m³ at local conditions using the ideal gas correction:

$$C_{local} = 0.075 \times \frac{64}{24.45} \times \frac{303.15}{298.15} \times \frac{1}{0.98}$$

**Step 3 – Evaluate Numerically:**

$$C_{local} = 0.075 \times 2.618 \times 1.0168 \times 1.0204$$

$$C_{local} = 0.075 \times 2.618 \times 1.0375 = 0.075 \times 2.716 = 0.2037 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

$$C_{STP} = 203.7 \text{ µg/m}^3$$

**Alternative Direct Method:** Using the simpler formula at local conditions (most KTU problems expect 25 °C, 1 atm assumption):

$$C_{25°C, 1 atm} = 0.075 \times \frac{64}{24.45} = 0.1964 \text{ mg/m}^3 = 196.4 \text{ µg/m}^3$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often commit these mistakes in ppm-to-µg/m³ problems:
> 1. **Confusion between mg/m³ and µg/m³** (factor of 1000). Always write units explicitly.
> 2. **Using 22.4 L/mol (STP at 0 °C)** instead of **24.45 L/mol (at 25 °C)**. KTU problems specify 25 °C—using 22.4 costs **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert ppm → fraction (÷10⁶)** during derivation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Triggers in AAQ Module:**
> 1. **No diagram in instrumentation questions:** A neat block diagram is worth 3–4 marks. A 14-mark question without a diagram caps at 10 marks.
> 2. **Confusing NAAQS units (µg/m³ vs mg/m³):** SO₂ NAAQS 24-hr = 80 µg/m³, CO 8-hr = 4 mg/m³. Mix-up costs 1 mark each occurrence.
> 3. **Wrong AQI breakpoint lookup:** Always confirm which range the concentration falls into; misclassifying between BP ranges (e.g., 90 vs 91 for PM2.5) shifts the AQI by 30–50 points.
> 4. **Skipping the "truncation" step:** A leading KTU complaint—students directly use measured values without truncation.
> 5. **Using "average" instead of "maximum" for AQI:** A common conceptual error. AQI is the **maximum sub-index, not the average**. This is worth 2 marks in the 14-mark question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **AAQ measurement = 3 blocks:** Sampling → Analysis → Data Reporting.
- **8 Criteria Pollutants:** PM2.5, PM10, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, NH₃, Pb.
- **AQI Range (India CPCB):** 0–500, 8 bands, computed via **piecewise linear breakpoint method**.
- **AQI = max of sub-indices** (dominant pollutant governs).
- **Truncation Rule:** PM2.5/PM10 → integer; CO → 2 decimals; O₃ → 3 decimals.
- **Master Formula:** $I_p = \dfrac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} (C_p - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$.
- **STP Reference Conditions:** 25 °C, 1 atm; molar volume = **24.45 L/mol**.
- **Reference Methods:** PM = Gravimetric; SO₂ = West-Gaeke; NO₂ = Griess-Saltzman; CO = NDIR; O₃ = UV Photometry (254 nm).
- **Continuous Methods:** SO₂ = UV Fluorescence; NOx = Chemiluminescence; PM = BAM / TEOM.
- **Beer-Lambert Law:** $A = \varepsilon \cdot b \cdot c$ — foundation of all optical gas analyzers.
- **Beta Attenuation:** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu m}$ — foundation of PM mass monitors.
- **Conversion:** $C(\mu g/m^3) = C(ppm) \times \dfrac{MW}{24.45}$ — high-yield for numerical problems.
- **Standard Flow for PM:** 1 m³/hr (16.67 L/min) for 24-hr reference measurement.
- **NAAQS Averages:** Always quote both 24-hr and annual; KTU questions often expect both.
- **CPCB NAMP** uses CAAQMS (Continuous AAQMS) for real-time AQI display.
- **Beer-Lambert Wavelengths to Memorize:** SO₂ absorption 190–230 nm; O₃ 254 nm; CO IR 4.67 µm.
- **Beta Source Used:** ¹⁴C or ⁸⁵Kr in BAM; ¹⁴C is preferred (lower energy, safer).
- **AQI Categories (India):** Good, Satisfactory, Moderate, Poor, Very Poor, Severe, Hazardous.
- **Always state the dominant pollutant** in any AQI answer (e.g., "PM10-governed Poor AQI").
- **Sensor drift compensation:** Chopper-based reference cell (UVF, UV Photometric).
- **Pollution health effects:** SO₂ → bronchitis; NO₂ → respiratory; CO → hypoxia; PM2.5 → cardiopulmonary; O₃ → smog.
