# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | 3 Marks | CO4 | Bloom Level: Remember/Understand]**

**Q1. Define the term "Electrostatic Precipitator" and mention any two industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**

An **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)** is an air pollution control device that removes fine particulate matter from a gas stream by giving the particles a negative electrical charge using a high-voltage electrode (corona discharge) and then collecting them on positively charged collecting plates.

**Two Industrial Applications:**
1. **Thermal Power Plants** – to control fly ash emissions from coal-fired boilers.
2. **Cement Industry** – to capture kiln dust from clinker production.

**Marking Scheme (3 marks):** Definition (2 marks) + Applications (1 mark).

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | 3 Marks | CO4 | Bloom Level: Understand]**

**Q2. What is the significance of Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD) in industrial wastewater treatment?**

**Model Answer:**

**BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand)** is the amount of dissolved oxygen (in mg/L) required by aerobic microorganisms to biologically oxidize the organic matter present in wastewater over a standard period of **5 days at 20°C**.

**Significance:**
1. **Indicator of Organic Pollution:** Higher BOD indicates higher organic load in wastewater.
2. **Design Parameter:** Used to design aeration tanks, biological reactors, and to size the ETP.
3. **Regulatory Compliance:** Statutory limit for industrial effluent discharge into water bodies is typically **BOD < 30 mg/L** (CPCB norm).
4. **Treatment Efficiency Check:** Comparing inlet and outlet BOD gives efficiency of secondary treatment.

**Marking Scheme (3 marks):** Definition (1.5 marks) + 2 significance points (0.75 mark each).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 | Module 4 Internal Choice | CO4 | Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze]**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)** used for particulate matter control in a thermal power plant. List the key instrumentation used for monitoring its performance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 (ESP is a key sub-block of the air pollution control architecture).

**Working Principle — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1: Gas Inlet:** Hot, dust-laden flue gas from the boiler enters the ESP inlet, typically at 130–180°C.

**Step 2: Charging Section:** The gas passes between negatively charged **discharge electrodes** (tungsten wires) and grounded **collecting plates** (steel). A high DC voltage of **30–100 kV** is applied, generating a **corona discharge** that ionizes gas molecules.

**Step 3: Particle Charging:** The negative ions (mostly O₂⁻ and OH⁻) attach to the dust particles, giving them a net negative charge.

**Step 4: Collection:** The negatively charged particles migrate under the influence of the electric field toward the positively charged collecting plates, where they deposit.

**Step 5: Rapping and Removal:** Periodically, the plates are struck by **rappers** (mechanical hammer systems) which dislodge the dust layer, causing it to fall into the **hoppers** below.

**Step 6: Discharge:** The cleaned gas exits the ESP outlet and proceeds to further treatment (FGD, SCR).

**Key Instrumentation for Performance Monitoring:**
1. **High Voltage Rectifier** – supplies and monitors DC voltage.
2. **Current Density Sensor** – measures corona current (anomalies indicate sparking or plate failure).
3. **Differential Pressure Transmitter** – measures pressure drop across ESP (indicates plate clogging).
4. **Opacity Monitor** – located at stack outlet, measures % opacity to ensure emission compliance.
5. **Temperature Sensors (RTD/TC)** – monitors gas temperature at inlet/outlet.
6. **Vibration Sensors on Rappers** – ensures rapping mechanism is functioning.
7. **Hopper Level Sensors** – prevents dust overflow.

**Marking Scheme (7 marks):** Working principle (4 marks) + Diagram (1.5 marks) + Instrumentation (1.5 marks).

---

**(b)** A thermal power plant emits SO₂ at a rate of **400 kg/hr**. Calculate the minimum stack height as per CPCB norms. If the actual concentration of SO₂ at ground level is measured as **40 µg/m³**, comment on compliance with the NAAQS standard of **80 µg/m³**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Given data**
$$Q = 400 \text{ kg/hr}$$
$$\text{Standard } C_{\text{limit}} = 80 \text{ µg/m}^3$$

**Step 2: Apply CPCB stack height formula**
$$H = 14\sqrt{Q}$$

**Step 3: Substitute**
$$H = 14 \times \sqrt{400} = 14 \times 20 = 280 \text{ m}$$

> **Answer:** Minimum required stack height = **280 m**.

**Step 4: Compliance check**
$$C_{\text{actual}} = 40 \text{ µg/m}^3$$
$$C_{\text{limit}} = 80 \text{ µg/m}^3$$

Since $C_{\text{actual}} < C_{\text{limit}}$ (40 < 80), the plant is **compliant** with the NAAQS standard.

**Step 5: Margin of safety**
$$\text{Margin} = 80 - 40 = 40 \text{ µg/m}^3 \text{ (which is } 50\% \text{ below the standard)}$$

> **Conclusion:** The plant meets the regulatory requirement with a healthy safety margin. However, continuous monitoring via CEMS is recommended to ensure sustained compliance.

**Marking Scheme (7 marks):** Formula (2 marks) + Calculation (2 marks) + Compliance analysis (3 marks).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)**

**(a)** Describe the **three stages of industrial Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP)** with a neat flow diagram. Explain the role of **instrumentation** at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Flow Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2 for the comprehensive ETP flow.

**Stage 1: Primary Treatment (Physical/Chemical)**

**Step 1:** Industrial effluent enters a collection sump and passes through **bar screens** to remove large floating debris.

**Step 2:** Flow is normalized in an **equalization tank** to handle fluctuations in flow and pH.

**Step 3:** **pH adjustment** is done using acid/alkali dosing systems.

**Step 4:** **Coagulants** (alum, ferric chloride, polyelectrolytes) are added in flocculation tanks to aggregate fine suspended particles.

**Step 5:** In the **primary clarifier**, heavy solids settle as sludge; floating oils are skimmed off.

**Instrumentation at Stage 1:**
- pH sensor (online) with PID-controlled dosing pumps
- Flow meters (electromagnetic / ultrasonic) at inlet and outlet
- Level sensors in tanks
- Turbidity sensor at clarifier outlet
- Sludge density meter

**Stage 2: Secondary Treatment (Biological)**

**Step 6:** Effluent enters the **aeration tank** where aerobic bacteria oxidize dissolved organic matter (reducing BOD).

**Step 7:** Mixed liquor flows to **secondary clarifier**; biomass settles as activated sludge, partly returned to aeration tank (RAS) and partly wasted (WAS).

**Instrumentation at Stage 2:**
- **Dissolved Oxygen (DO) sensor** – critical for maintaining 1.5–2.5 mg/L DO
- **MLSS (Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids) sensor** – for biomass control
- **BOD/COD online analyzers**
- **Respiration rate meters** (OUR – Oxygen Uptake Rate)
- Air flow meters to aeration blowers

**Stage 3: Tertiary Treatment (Advanced)**

**Step 8:** Polishing step using **Activated Carbon Filters, RO, Ion Exchange** to remove residual organics, TDS, and heavy metals.

**Step 9:** **Disinfection** using UV lamps or chlorination to kill pathogens.

**Step 10:** Treated water is either **recycled** to process or **discharged** to environment.

**Instrumentation at Stage 3:**
- **Conductivity / TDS sensor**
- **TOC (Total Organic Carbon) analyzer**
- **UV transmittance sensor**
- **Residual chlorine analyzer**
- **Color and turbidity sensors**

**Marking Scheme (7 marks):** Three stages explained (4 marks) + Flow diagram (1.5 marks) + Instrumentation at each stage (1.5 marks).

---

**(b)** A textile dyeing industry discharges **500 m³/day** of wastewater with a BOD of **1200 mg/L**. After biological treatment in the ETP, the BOD reduces to **30 mg/L**. Calculate:
   - (i) Mass of BOD removed per day
   - (ii) Removal efficiency of the secondary treatment
   - (iii) Compliance status if the regulatory limit is **BOD < 100 mg/L** for discharge to inland surface water. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Given data**
$$Q = 500 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} = 500{,}000 \text{ L/day}$$
$$C_{\text{in}} = 1200 \text{ mg/L (BOD)}$$
$$C_{\text{out}} = 30 \text{ mg/L (BOD)}$$
$$C_{\text{limit}} = 100 \text{ mg/L}$$

**(i) Mass of BOD removed per day:**

**Step 2: Calculate load difference**
$$\Delta C = C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}} = 1200 - 30 = 1170 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 3: Multiply by flow**
$$\text{Mass removed} = 1170 \text{ mg/L} \times 500{,}000 \text{ L/day}$$

$$\text{Mass removed} = 585{,}000{,}000 \text{ mg/day}$$

**Step 4: Convert to kg (1 kg = 10⁶ mg)**
$$\text{Mass removed} = \dfrac{585{,}000{,}000}{10^6} = 585 \text{ kg/day}$$

> **Answer (i): 585 kg of BOD is removed per day.**

**(ii) Removal efficiency:**

**Step 5: Apply efficiency formula**
$$\eta = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{1200 - 30}{1200} \times 100 = \dfrac{1170}{1200} \times 100$$

$$\eta = 97.5\%$$

> **Answer (ii): Removal efficiency = 97.5%.**

**(iii) Compliance check:**

**Step 6: Compare outlet BOD with regulatory limit**
$$C_{\text{out}} = 30 \text{ mg/L} < 100 \text{ mg/L} = C_{\text{limit}}$$

> **Answer (iii): The treated effluent is COMPLIANT with the regulatory discharge standard (30 mg/L is well below the 100 mg/L limit).** The industry may safely discharge to inland surface water.

**Marking Scheme (7 marks):** Each sub-part (i), (ii), (iii) carries ~2.3 marks — show full calculation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch is the #1 reason students lose marks.** Always show unit conversion explicitly (e.g., mg → kg, m³ → L).
> 2. **Confusing BOD with COD.** BOD measures *biodegradable* organics only; COD measures *all* oxidizable organics (chemical method). For the same wastewater, **COD > BOD always**.
> 3. **Using wrong Q in stack formula.** The CPCB formula uses **Q in kg/hr**, not kg/s or tonnes/day. Convert before applying.
> 4. **Forgetting the 5-day, 20°C standard condition for BOD.** Always specify in the answer.
> 5. **Neglecting the direction of migration in ESP.** Particles move to the *oppositely* charged electrode (negatively charged particles → positive plate). Many students get this wrong.
> 6. **In Deutsch-Anderson equation, students forget to convert $wA/Q$ to a dimensionless quantity.** All three terms must be in consistent units (e.g., $w$ in m/s, $A$ in m², $Q$ in m³/s).
> 7. **Skipping diagrams in 14-mark questions.** KTU examiners deduct 1.5–2 marks if a flow diagram is missing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 4 – Pollution Control in Industries: Last-Minute Revision Points**

### Quick-Reference Bulleted Summary

- **Three pollution streams:** Air (gaseous + PM), Water (effluent), Solid (slag/ash/sludge).
- **CPCB stack height formula:** $H = 14\sqrt{Q}$, where Q = SO₂ emission rate in kg/hr.
- **Deutsch-Anderson ESP efficiency:** $\eta = 1 - e^{-wA/Q}$.
- **Universal removal efficiency:** $\eta = \left(\dfrac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}}\right) \times 100\%$.
- **BOD standard test:** 5 days at 20°C; result in mg/L.
- **BOD regulatory limit (India):** <30 mg/L (CPCB, inland discharge).
- **COD > BOD** always — COD includes non-biodegradable organics.
- **Mass Emission Rate (MER):** MER = C × Q × 10⁻⁶ (kg/hr).
- **HRT (Hydraulic Retention Time):** HRT = V/Q.
- **Pollution Control Hierarchy:** Source Reduction → Recycle → Treatment → Disposal.
- **ESP migration direction:** Negatively charged dust → moves to positively charged plates.
- **FGD removes SO₂**; **SCR removes NOₓ**; **ESP/Baghouse removes PM**; **Thermal Oxidizer removes VOCs**.
- **CEMS** = Continuous Emission Monitoring System — mandated for 17 categories of highly polluting industries in India.
- **NAAQS for SO₂:** 80 µg/m³ (24-hour average, industrial area); for PM2.5: 60 µg/m³.
- **ETP stages:** Primary (physical/chemical) → Secondary (biological) → Tertiary (advanced polishing).
- **Key ETP instrumentation:** pH sensor, DO sensor, MLSS sensor, BOD/COD analyzer, conductivity meter, flow meter.
- **Cleaner Production** is always preferred over End-of-Pipe treatment (cheaper, more sustainable).
- **ZLD (Zero Liquid Discharge):** Achieved via RO + MEE (Multi-Effect Evaporator) + ATFD (Agitated Thin Film Dryer); mandatory for textile, pharma, and tannery industries.
- **Green Belt Development** around industries is a statutory requirement to attenuate air pollution and noise.

### Mnemonics for Quick Recall

- **For ESP components:** **"CEHR"** → Corona, Electrodes, Hoppers, Rappers.
- **For ETP stages:** **"P-S-T"** → Primary, Secondary, Tertiary.
- **For air control hierarchy:** **"P-G-S"** → Particulate (ESP/Bag), Gaseous (FGD/SCR), Stack dispersion.
- **For BOD/COD relationship:** **"COD Boss"** → COD is always the **B**igger value (Oxygen demand).

### Key Instrumentation Suffixes (KTU Frequently Tests)

- **DO Sensor** → Aeration tank control.
- **pH Sensor** → Neutralization tank control.
- **TDS / Conductivity** → RO performance and water reuse.
- **Opacity Monitor** → Stack PM compliance.
- **NDIR Analyzer** → SO₂, CO measurement.
- **Chemiluminescence** → NOₓ measurement.
- **FTIR** → Multi-gas (SO₂, NOₓ, CO, HF, HCl, VOCs).
- **TOC Analyzer** → Organic load measurement in water.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pollution Control in Petroleum Refineries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT832 - ENVIRONMENTAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pollution Control in Petroleum Refineries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Petroleum Refinery Pollution Control** refers to the systematic implementation of engineering processes, monitoring instrumentation, and regulatory compliance measures designed to identify, quantify, capture, treat, and mitigate the emission of harmful contaminants (solid, liquid, and gaseous) generated during crude oil refining operations. As per the KTU 2024 OEICT832 syllabus, it encompasses the deployment of continuous emission monitoring systems (CEMS), wastewater treatment plants (ETP/STP), and advanced separation technologies to ensure refinery discharges conform to the standards prescribed by the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** and the **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC), Government of India**.

The three principal pollution vectors in a refinery are:
- **Atmospheric emissions** — SO₂, NOₓ, CO, CO₂, H₂S, VOCs, PM₂.₅, PM₁₀
- **Liquid effluents** — Oily wastewater, spent caustic, phenol-containing streams
- **Solid/Hazardous waste** — Spent catalysts, oily sludge, biological sludge

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Petroleum refining is a continuous-process industry classified as a **17-category Red Category** highly polluting industry under the CPCB's Red/Orange/Green/White categorization. It is therefore a mandatory subject for instrumentation engineers since every control loop and every pollution abatement unit depends on accurate sensor data.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Refinery as a Human Body"

Imagine a petroleum refinery as a giant living organism:
- **Crude oil** is its "food" (input).
- **Furnace heaters, distillation columns, catalytic crackers** are its "digestive organs" that break down the food.
- **Just like digestion produces waste (CO₂, urea, sweat), refining produces waste** — flue gases, sour water, sludge.
- The **pollution control units** are the kidneys, lungs, and liver of the refinery — they filter, scrub, and neutralize the waste before it is released.
- The **instrumentation system** is the **nervous system** — sensors (the nerves) detect the toxicity of the waste, and control valves (the muscles) regulate the treatment chemicals and flow rates.

If even one nerve (sensor) is damaged, the organism cannot tell whether it is releasing poison or clean output — hence pollution control instrumentation is mission-critical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Context:** A typical 10 MMTPA (Million Metric Tonnes Per Annum) Indian refinery such as BPCL Kochi or IOCL Panipat generates approximately **40,000–50,000 m³/day of wastewater** and **300–500 tonnes/day of SO₂**, which must be continuously monitored and treated before discharge.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Refinery Pollution Streams

Pollution in a petroleum refinery can be classified into four primary streams, each demanding distinct instrumentation:

#### A. Air Pollution Sources
1. **Sulfur Dioxide (SO₂)** — From combustion of sulfur-rich fuel in process heaters, boilers, and Fluidized Catalytic Cracking Units (FCCU).
2. **Nitrogen Oxides (NOₓ)** — Thermal NOₓ from high-temperature furnace operations (>1300 °C).
3. **Particulate Matter (PM)** — From catalyst dust in FCCU regenerators, coke handling.
4. **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)** — Fugitive emissions from storage tank floating roofs, valves, flanges.
5. **Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S)** — From hydrodesulfurization units and sour water strippers.
6. **Carbon Monoxide (CO) and CO₂** — Incomplete/complete combustion products.
7. **Ammonia (NH₃)** — From amine treatment units.

#### B. Water Pollution Sources
1. **Desalter effluent** — Contains suspended solids, oil, and dissolved salts.
2. **Sour water** — H₂S, NH₃, phenols from hydrocracker overhead receivers.
3. **Spent caustic** — From Merox and caustic wash units (pH > 12).
4. **Cooling tower blowdown** — Contains biocides, chromates, phosphates.
5. **Storm water runoff** — Oily contamination from tank farm areas.
6. **Ballast water from tankers**.

#### C. Solid/Hazardous Waste
1. **Spent catalysts** (e.g., spent Ni-Mo, Co-Mo from HDS units).
2. **Oily sludge** from crude oil storage tank bottoms.
3. **Biological sludge** from wastewater treatment plants.
4. **Spent clay** from lube oil refining.

#### D. Noise Pollution
- From compressors, turbine exhausts, high-pressure steam let-down stations.

### 2.2 Pollution Control Technologies — The "Why" and "How"

#### 2.2.1 Air Pollution Control

| Technology | Pollutant Targeted | Operational Principle | Key Instrument |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wet Scrubber (FGD)** | SO₂, H₂S, HCl | Gas-liquid contact using lime/NaOH slurry; SO₂ + Ca(OH)₂ → CaSO₃ + H₂O | pH meter, flow transmitter, DP transmitter |
| **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** | NOₓ | Injection of NH₃ over catalyst bed at 300–400 °C: 4NO + 4NH₃ + O₂ → 4N₂ + 6H₂O | NH₃ analyzer, NOₓ CEMS |
| **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)** | Particulate Matter (PM) | Corona discharge charges dust particles; collected on oppositely charged plates | Voltage controller, opacity monitor |
| **Baghouse Filter** | PM (esp. catalyst dust) | Fabric filtration; periodic reverse-jet cleaning | Differential Pressure (DP) sensor |
| **Thermal Oxidizer / Incinerator** | VOCs, CO, HCs | High-temp oxidation (≥760 °C) of hydrocarbons to CO₂ + H₂O | LEL meter, thermocouple |
| **Flare System** | Emergency HC relief | Combustion of excess hydrocarbons at the tip of an elevated flare stack | Flame detector, flow meter, gas chromatograph |

#### 2.2.2 Water Pollution Control — The Refinery Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP)

A refinery ETP follows a **four-stage treatment hierarchy**:

**Stage 1 — Pre-treatment (Primary):**
- **API Oil-Water Separator** — Gravity separation based on density difference.
- **Corrugated Plate Interceptor (CPI)** — Enhanced gravity separation using inclined plates.
- **Equalization Basin** — Buffers flow and concentration fluctuations.

**Stage 2 — Physico-chemical (Secondary):**
- **Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF)** — Air bubbles attach to oil/emulsified particles and float them.
- **Coagulation-Flocculation** — Alum, polyelectrolyte dosing to agglomerate colloids.
- **Biological Oxidation** — Activated Sludge Process (ASP) or Trickling Filter where bacteria metabolize hydrocarbons, phenols, cyanides.

**Stage 3 — Tertiary / Polishing:**
- **Activated Carbon Filtration (ACF)** — Adsorption of residual organics.
- **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** — Final dissolved solids removal.
- **Tertiary chemical oxidation** (O₃/UV/H₂O₂) for non-biodegradables.

**Stage 4 — Sludge Handling:**
- **Centrifuge / Belt Press** dewatering → Incineration or secured landfill.

#### 2.2.3 Solid Waste Management

- **Spent catalyst recovery** — Metal reclamation (Ni, V, Mo) through pyrometallurgical routes.
- **Oily sludge** — Centrifugation + thermal desorption.
- **Co-processing in cement kilns** for non-hazardous fractions.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for an Instrumentation Engineer:**
> Every pollution control unit is essentially a **closed-loop control system** governed by the classical feedback equation: **e(t) = SP − PV**, where SP is the regulatory emission setpoint (e.g., SO₂ < 100 mg/Nm³) and PV is the live sensor measurement. A scrubber loop controlling pH between 6.5–7.5, or an SCR loop dosing NH₃ to maintain NOₓ < 50 mg/Nm³, are both classic PID applications in environmental instrumentation.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Refinery Material Balance (overall) | $\sum m_{in} = \sum m_{out} + m_{accumulation}$ | kg/h | Mass conservation check on ETP |
| 2 | API Separator Oil Removal Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Performance of primary oil-water separator |
| 3 | Reynolds Number (pipe flow) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Wastewater pipe flow regime |
| 4 | Scrubber Gas-Liquid Mass Transfer | $N_A = k_L \cdot a \cdot (C^* - C)$ | kg/m³·s | SO₂ absorption in FGD |
| 5 | Stoichiometry of FGD (Limestone) | $SO_2 + CaCO_3 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CaSO_4 + CO_2$ | — | Lime/limestone requirement calc |
| 6 | SCR Reaction | $4NO + 4NH_3 + O_2 \xrightarrow{catalyst} 4N_2 + 6H_2O$ | — | NH₃ dosing for NOₓ control |
| 7 | Henry's Law (gas-liquid) | $C^* = k_H \cdot P_{gas}$ | mol/m³·Pa | VOCs stripping from water |
| 8 | Opacity (Ringelmann Scale) | $\text{Opacity} = 1 - \dfrac{I}{I_0}$ | 0–1 | Stack PM measurement |
| 9 | BOD₅ Substrate Removal | $S = S_0 \, e^{-kt}$ (first order) | mg/L | Activated sludge design |
| 10 | COD Stoichiometric O₂ Demand | $C_nH_mO_p + (n + \tfrac{m}{4} - \tfrac{p}{2})O_2 \rightarrow nCO_2 + \tfrac{m}{2}H_2O$ | mg/L | Theoretical O₂ for oxidation |
| 11 | ESP Migration Velocity (Deutsch) | $w = \dfrac{Q}{A} \cdot \dfrac{-\ln(1-\eta)}{1}$ | m/s | ESP collection efficiency |
| 12 | NDIR Beer-Lambert Law | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\varepsilon c l}$ | — | SO₂/CO analyzer optical path |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The Ringelmann Scale (opacity) and the Deutsch-Anderson equation for ESP efficiency are the most frequently tested numerical concepts in board exams. Memorize the **SO₂ + CaCO₃** and **4NO + 4NH₃ + O₂** reactions verbatim.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: API Oil-Water Separator Sizing

The API separator is sized using **Stokes' Law** for terminal settling velocity of an oil droplet in water. This derivation is essential for KTU numerical problems.

**Given:**
- Oil droplet diameter $d$ = 150 μm = $150 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- Density of oil $\rho_o$ = 850 kg/m³
- Density of water $\rho_w$ = 1000 kg/m³
- Dynamic viscosity of water $\mu_w$ = $1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- g = 9.81 m/s²

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant force balance:**

For a small spherical oil droplet rising in still water, the terminal (Stokes) velocity is reached when buoyant force equals drag force:

$$\tfrac{\pi}{6} d^3 (\rho_w - \rho_o) g = 3 \pi \mu_w d \, v_t$$

**Step 2 — Solve for terminal velocity $v_t$:**

$$v_t = \dfrac{(\rho_w - \rho_o) \, g \, d^2}{18 \mu_w}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$v_t = \dfrac{(1000 - 850) \times 9.81 \times (150 \times 10^{-6})^2}{18 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the numerator:**

$$(150) \times 10^{-6} \, m = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \, m$$

$$(1.5 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 2.25 \times 10^{-8} \, m^2$$

$$150 \times 9.81 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-8} = 3.31 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the denominator:**

$$18 \times 10^{-3} = 1.8 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 6 — Final result:**

$$v_t = \dfrac{3.31 \times 10^{-5}}{1.8 \times 10^{-2}} = 1.84 \times 10^{-3} \, m/s$$

$$\boxed{v_t \approx 1.84 \times 10^{-3} \, m/s \;\;\text{or}\;\; 6.62 \, m/h}$$

**Step 7 — Engineering implication:**

For a 5 m wide × 4 m deep × 30 m long API separator, residence time is $t = L/v_t = 30/(1.84 \times 10^{-3}) = 16{,}304$ s ≈ 4.53 h — well within typical 1–6 h design residence times. The droplets larger than 150 μm will float to the surface and be skimmed.

### 3.2 Derivation: ESP Collection Efficiency (Deutsch-Anderson Equation)

**Given:**
- Migration velocity $w$ = 0.10 m/s
- Gas flow rate $Q$ = 50 m³/s
- Total collection plate area $A$ = 500 m²

**Step 1 — Deutsch-Anderson Equation:**

$$\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{w \cdot A}{Q}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\dfrac{w \cdot A}{Q} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 500}{50} = 1.0$$

**Step 3 — Compute efficiency:**

$$\eta = 1 - e^{-1.0} = 1 - 0.3679 = 0.6321$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 63.21\%}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

This ESP removes ~63% of the particulate. To meet CPCB norms of <50 mg/Nm³ from a feed of 200 mg/Nm³, the efficiency needed is 75%. Either $A$ must be increased to ≈693 m², or $w$ improved to ≈0.144 m/s (e.g., by reducing plate spacing).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection for Refinery Pollution Monitoring

| Pollutant | Sensor Principle | Range | Response Time | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SO₂ | UV Fluorescence / NDIR | 0–500 ppm | < 30 s | Stack CEMS |
| NOₓ | Chemiluminescence | 0–1000 ppm | < 20 s | Stack CEMS, SCR outlet |
| CO | NDIR (Infrared) | 0–1000 ppm | < 10 s | Combustion tuning |
| H₂S | Electrochemical | 0–50 ppm | < 60 s | Sour water stripper overhead |
| VOC | PID (Photo-Ionization Detector) | 0–1000 ppm | < 5 s | Fugitive emission monitoring |
| PM | Beta attenuation / Optical | 0–10 g/m³ | 1 min | Stack opacity |
| pH (ETP) | Glass electrode | 0–14 | < 5 s | Scrubber and neutralization |
| DO (ETP) | Galvanic membrane | 0–20 mg/L | < 30 s | Aeration tank |
| Conductivity | 4-electrode | 0–50 mS/cm | < 2 s | RO reject monitoring |
| TOC | UV-Persulfate | 0–1000 mg/L | 5 min | Final ETP outlet |
| Flow (effluent) | Magnetic / Ultrasonic | 0–10 m/s | continuous | ETP influent |
| Noise | Sound Level Meter (IEC 61672) | 30–140 dB | 1 s | Boundary noise survey |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** The **PID (Photo-Ionization Detector)** for VOC and the **NDIR (Non-Dispersive Infrared)** for SO₂/CO are the two most frequently asked instruments. Always mention the Beer-Lambert law when explaining NDIR: $I = I_0 \, e^{-\varepsilon c l}$, where $\varepsilon$ is the molar absorptivity, $c$ is the gas concentration, and $l$ is the optical path length.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Refinery Pollution Monitoring and Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crude Oil Feed] --> B[Desalter]
    B --> C[Atmospheric Distillation]
    C --> D[FCCU]
    C --> E[Hydrocracker]
    C --> F[Reformer]
    D --> G[Air Pollution Source Stack]
    E --> H[Sour Water]
    F --> I[Flue Gas]
    G --> J[ESP]
    G --> K[Wet Scrubber FGD]
    H --> L[API Separator]
    H --> M[Sour Water Stripper]
    I --> N[SCR NO Reduction]
    J --> O[Stack Emission Monitor CEMS]
    K --> O
    N --> O
    L --> P[Activated Sludge ETP]
    M --> P
    P --> Q[DAF Clarifier]
    Q --> R[Sand Filter and ACF]
    R --> S[Treated Effluent Discharge]
    O --> T[PLC and DCS Controller]
    P --> T
    T --> U[Control Valve and Dosing Pump]
    U --> K
    U --> N
    U --> P
    O --> V[Regulatory Reporting CPCB Server]
    T --> V
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: ETP Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Refinery Process Wastewater] --> B[Equalization Tank]
    B --> C[API Oil Water Separator]
    C --> D[Coagulation Flocculation]
    D --> E[DAF Dissolved Air Flotation]
    E --> F[Aeration Tank ASP]
    F --> G[Secondary Clarifier]
    G --> H[Sand Filter]
    H --> I[Activated Carbon Filter]
    I --> J[Tertiary RO and UV]
    J --> K[Discharge to Environment]
    F --> L[Sludge Thickener]
    L --> M[Sludge Dewatering Centrifuge]
    M --> N[Secured Landfill or Incineration]

```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: CEMS Continuous Emission Monitoring System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Refinery Stack] --> B[Sample Probe and Heated Line]
    B --> C[Sample Conditioning Unit]
    C --> D[Gas Analyzers SO2 NOX CO O2]
    C --> E[Opacity PM Monitor]
    C --> F[Flow and Temperature Sensors]
    D --> G[Data Acquisition System DAS]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[PLC and DCS]
    H --> I[Operator HMI Display]
    H --> J[Alarm and Trip Logic]
    H --> K[Regulatory Data Push to CPCB]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Air Pollution Control Selection Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Pollutant] --> B{Is it Particulate?}
    B -- Yes --> C[ESP or Baghouse]
    B -- No --> D{Is it SO2 or H2S?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Wet Scrubber FGD]
    D -- No --> F{Is it NOX?}
    F -- Yes --> G[SCR or SNCR]
    F -- No --> H{Is it VOC or CO?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Thermal Oxidizer or Flare]
    H -- No --> J[Carbon Adsorption or Biofilter]
    C --> K[Continuous Opacity CEMS]
    E --> L[SO2 CEMS]
    G --> M[NOX CEMS]
    I --> N[VOC FID PID]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for students:** These Mermaid schematics intentionally abstract the physical process into functional blocks, which is the engineering instrumentation perspective — i.e., *what is being sensed, what is being controlled, and what is the data flow to the DCS/CPCB* — rather than mechanical P&ID line drawings.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** List any three major air pollutants emitted from a petroleum refinery and state one control technology used for each.

**Model Answer:**
- **Sulfur Dioxide (SO₂):** Controlled by **Wet Limestone Scrubber (FGD)** where SO₂ reacts with CaCO₃ slurry to form CaSO₃, which is oxidized to gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O).
- **Nitrogen Oxides (NOₓ):** Controlled by **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)**, where NH₃ is injected over a catalyst at 300–400 °C to reduce NO to N₂.
- **Particulate Matter:** Controlled by **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)** which charges the particles using corona discharge and collects them on grounded plates.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is meant by a "sour water stripper" in a refinery ETP, and which pollutants does it handle?

**Model Answer:**
A sour water stripper is a distillation column in the refinery wastewater treatment system that removes dissolved **H₂S** and **NH₃** from process water streams (such as overhead receiver water from hydrocracker and catalytic cracker units). The acidic H₂S is recovered as sulfur in a downstream **Claus plant**, and NH₃ is either recovered as anhydrous ammonia or sent to a sour water treatment unit. The treated water is then routed to the main ETP for biological treatment.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** Describe with a neat block diagram, the functioning of a **Continuous Emission Monitoring System (CEMS)** used for refinery stack monitoring. List the analyzers typically deployed. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A CEMS is an integrated, real-time monitoring system mandated by CPCB for stacks in highly polluting industries such as petroleum refineries. The block diagram consists of:

1. **Sample extraction probe** — Located in the stack, with a heated filter to prevent condensation of acidic gases.
2. **Heated sample transport line** — Maintains gas above 120 °C to avoid moisture loss and acid corrosion.
3. **Sample conditioning unit (SCU)** — Removes moisture via a chiller/condenser and filters particulates.
4. **Gas analyzers** (the heart of CEMS):
    - **SO₂** → UV fluorescence or NDIR analyzer
    - **NO/NO₂/NOₓ** → Chemiluminescence analyzer
    - **CO** → NDIR analyzer
    - **O₂** → Paramagnetic or electrochemical cell (for dilution correction)
    - **HCl/HF** (if applicable) → NDIR
5. **Particulate / Opacity monitor** — Transmissometer or beta-attenuation device.
6. **Stack flow & temperature sensors** — Pitot tube + thermocouple (for mass emission calculation: $\dot{m}_{pollutant} = C \cdot Q_{stack}$).
7. **Data Acquisition System (DAS)** — Logs all parameters, performs emission calculations, and generates hourly/daily averages.
8. **Communication module** — Pushes data to the refinery DCS and the CPCB online server every 15 minutes.

**KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:** CO3 — *Design pollution monitoring systems using appropriate sensors.*

**(b)** A refinery boiler stack emits **SO₂ at 800 mg/Nm³**. A wet limestone scrubber is to be installed to bring it down to **150 mg/Nm³** (the CPCB limit for SO₂). Calculate the **required removal efficiency** and the **theoretical mass of limestone (CaCO₃) needed per hour** if the flue gas flow is 25,000 Nm³/h. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Required removal efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100 = \dfrac{800 - 150}{800} \times 100 = \dfrac{650}{800} \times 100$$

$$\eta = 81.25\%$$

**Step 2 — Mass of SO₂ to be removed per hour:**

$$\dot{m}_{SO_2,removed} = (C_{in} - C_{out}) \times Q = 650 \, mg/Nm^3 \times 25{,}000 \, Nm^3/h$$

$$\dot{m}_{SO_2,removed} = 16{,}250{,}000 \, mg/h = 16.25 \, kg/h$$

Converting to moles (M = 64 g/mol):

$$n_{SO_2} = \dfrac{16.25 \times 10^3}{64} = 253.91 \, mol/h$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric CaCO₃ required:**

The reaction: $SO_2 + CaCO_3 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CaSO_4 + CO_2$ (1:1 molar ratio with SO₂).

$$n_{CaCO_3} = 253.91 \, mol/h$$

Mass (M = 100 g/mol):

$$m_{CaCO_3} = 253.91 \times 100 = 25{,}391 \, g/h \approx 25.39 \, kg/h$$

**Step 4 — Engineering design (with 20% excess and 90% purity of limestone):**

$$m_{limestone,design} = \dfrac{25.39 \times 1.20}{0.90} \approx 33.86 \, kg/h$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 81.25\% \quad ; \quad m_{CaCO_3,design} \approx 33.86 \, kg/h}$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the working of an **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)** in a refinery Effluent Treatment Plant. List the key instrumentation parameters monitored in the aeration tank. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)** is a suspended-growth biological treatment where a mixed microbial consortium ("activated sludge") metabolizes dissolved and suspended organic pollutants in refinery wastewater. The process flow:

1. **Equalized wastewater** enters the aeration tank along with **return activated sludge (RAS)** from the secondary clarifier.
2. **Compressed air is diffused** through fine-bubble diffusers at the bottom of the tank, providing oxygen and keeping the biomass in suspension.
3. Microorganisms (mostly bacteria, protozoa) **oxidize hydrocarbons, phenols, cyanides, sulfides, and ammonia** into CO₂, H₂O, sulfates, and nitrates.
4. The mixed liquor flows to a **secondary clarifier** where biomass settles; a portion is recycled (RAS) and the rest is wasted (WAS).
5. The clarified effluent is sent to tertiary treatment.

**Key instrumentation in the aeration tank:**

| Parameter | Sensor | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Dissolved Oxygen (DO)** | Galvanic membrane probe | Maintain 1.5–2.5 mg/L for aerobic bacteria |
| **pH** | Glass electrode | Keep 6.5–7.5; outside range inhibits microbes |
| **Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids (MLSS)** | Optical/nephelometric | Maintain 2,000–4,000 mg/L biomass |
| **Temperature** | RTD/PT100 | Optimum 20–35 °C |
| **Flow** | Magnetic flow meter | F/M ratio control |
| **F/M Ratio (Food:Microorganism)** | Calculated | BOD load / MLSS; control air supply |

**KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:** CO2 — *Comprehend wastewater treatment processes.*

**(b)** The **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD₅)** of refinery wastewater entering an aeration tank is **450 mg/L**, and the **effluent BOD₅** is **30 mg/L**. The tank volume is 1500 m³, and the wastewater flow rate is 200 m³/h. Assuming first-order kinetics, calculate the **reaction rate constant k** of the activated sludge system. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the first-order model:**

$$C = C_0 \, e^{-kt}$$

**Step 2 — Determine hydraulic residence time (HRT):**

$$t = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{1500 \, m^3}{200 \, m^3/h} = 7.5 \, h$$

**Step 3 — Substitute C₀, C, and t:**

$$30 = 450 \times e^{-k \times 7.5}$$

$$e^{-7.5k} = \dfrac{30}{450} = 0.0667$$

**Step 4 — Take natural logarithm:**

$$-7.5k = \ln(0.0667) = -2.708$$

$$k = \dfrac{2.708}{7.5} = 0.361 \, h^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to per day (typical design unit):**

$$k_{day} = 0.361 \times 24 = 8.66 \, day^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{k \approx 0.361 \, h^{-1} \;\;\text{or}\;\; 8.66 \, day^{-1}}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
A high $k$ value indicates a highly active biomass — typical for refinery wastewater with acclimatized microbial culture. A standard municipal ASP has $k$ in the range 2–6 day⁻¹, so 8.66 day⁻¹ confirms strong biological activity due to the presence of readily degradable hydrocarbons in refinery influent.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in CEMS problems** — Concentrations are in **mg/Nm³** (normal cubic meters, at 0 °C, 1 atm), not mg/m³. Failure to use standard conditions costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Scrubber stoichiometry** — Always state the reaction explicitly; do not skip the $1:1$ molar ratio between SO₂ and CaCO₃. Add the ½ O₂ term and the CaSO₄ product — students often write incomplete reactions.
> 3. **HRT vs SRT confusion** — Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT = V/Q) is for plug flow hydraulics; Solids Retention Time (SRT) is a biomass retention parameter. Do not interchange them.
> 4. **Opposite sign in first-order BOD equation** — The decay form is $C = C_0 e^{-kt}$ (negative exponent). Students frequently write $+kt$ and end up with $C > C_0$ — guaranteed zero.
> 5. **Missing oxygen correction** in NOₓ CEMS — NOₓ is always reported on a **dry, O₂-corrected basis** (typically 3% O₂ for refinery heaters). Skip this and lose 2 marks.
> 6. **Catalyst name** — In SCR, the catalyst is typically V₂O₅/TiO₂ (vanadia on titania) — not just "a catalyst."

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pollution Control in Petroleum Refineries**

- **Air pollutants from refineries:** SO₂, NOₓ, CO, CO₂, H₂S, NH₃, VOCs, PM, dioxins (in older units).
- **Primary control technologies:** FGD (for SO₂), SCR/SNCR (for NOₓ), ESP/Baghouse (for PM), Thermal Oxidizer (for VOCs/CO), Flare (for emergency HC).
- **CEMS analyzers:** UV Fluorescence (SO₂), Chemiluminescence (NOₓ), NDIR (CO), Paramagnetic (O₂), Opacity (PM).
- **Beer-Lambert Law** $I = I_0 e^{-\varepsilon c l}$ is the operating principle of all NDIR analyzers.
- **ETP four-stage hierarchy:** Pre-treatment (API separator) → Physico-chemical (DAF, Coagulation) → Biological (ASP) → Tertiary (ACF, RO).
- **Stokes' Law settling velocity:** $v_t = \dfrac{(\rho_w - \rho_o) g d^2}{18 \mu_w}$ — used to design API separators.
- **Deutsch-Anderson ESP efficiency:** $\eta = 1 - \exp(-wA/Q)$ — the *must-memorize* equation for board exams.
- **FGD reaction:** $SO_2 + CaCO_3 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CaSO_4 + CO_2$ — both for stoichiometry and gypsum by-product.
- **SCR reaction:** $4NO + 4NH_3 + O_2 \rightarrow 4N_2 + 6H_2O$ at 300–400 °C.
- **ASP operational parameters:** DO = 1.5–2.5 mg/L; pH = 6.5–7.5; MLSS = 2,000–4,000 mg/L; F/M = 0.2–0.5 day⁻¹.
- **CPCB standards to memorize:** SO₂ < 100 mg/Nm³ (refinery boiler), NOₓ < 50 mg/Nm³ (heater), PM < 50 mg/Nm³, BOD < 30 mg/L (ETP outlet).
- **Online monitoring mandates:** Refineries must report 15-minute averaged CEMS data to the CPCB server (Continuous Stack Monitoring Portal).
- **BOD vs COD:** BOD measures biodegradable organics (5-day test); COD measures total oxidizable organics (2–3 hour test). BOD/COD ratio for refinery wastewater is typically 0.4–0.6.
- **Instrumentation hierarchy:** Field Sensor (transmitter) → Junction Box → Marshalling Cabinet → DCS/PLC → SCADA → Regulatory Server.
- **Key standards:** IS 11255 (Indian Standard for stack monitoring), EPA Method 6 (SO₂), EPA Method 7 (NOₓ), ISO 17075 (VOC), 40 CFR Part 60 (US EPA benchmark).

> **Mnemonic for Air Pollution Abatement — "SPONCH-V":**
> **S** — SO₂ → **S**crubber
> **P** — Particulate → **P**recipitator (ESP)
> **O** — Odour/H₂S → Scrubber & Biofilter
> **N** — NOₓ → SCR
> **C** — CO → Thermal Oxidizer
> **H** — Hydrocarbons (VOCs) → Thermal Oxidizer/Flare
> **V** — VOCs → Carbon Adsorption/Fugitive LDAR program

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fertilizer Industries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT832 - ENVIRONMENTAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fertilizer Industries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fertilizer Industry** refers to the integrated chemical manufacturing sector that converts atmospheric and mined raw materials (natural gas, naphtha, phosphate rock, potash, sulfur, and air) into plant nutrients — primarily **Nitrogen (N)**, **Phosphorus (P)**, and **Potassium (K)** — in concentrated, water-soluble, and biologically assimilable forms such as ammonia, urea, ammonium nitrate, ammonium phosphates, and NPK complexes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (OEICT832 – Module 4):**
> *Fertilizer Industries* is studied under Environmental Instrumentation for the purpose of identifying **(a)** process flow and unit operations, **(b)** source-specific pollutants generated at each stage, and **(c)** the appropriate online/offline analytical instrumentation required to monitor and mitigate environmental emissions.

The industry is classified by **NPK grade** — the percentage by weight of elemental Nitrogen (N), Phosphorus pentoxide (P₂O₅), and Potassium oxide (K₂O) in the finished product.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Nomenclature Standard (FAI, India):**
> - **N** is reported as elemental Nitrogen.
> - **P** is reported as P₂O₅ equivalent.
> - **K** is reported as K₂O equivalent.
> This convention allows direct numerical comparison of nutrient strength across all fertilizer products worldwide.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Plant Vitamin Factory"

Imagine a **hospital pharmacy that manufactures vitamins for plants**. Just as a human body cannot absorb a whole iron nail, a plant cannot directly consume atmospheric nitrogen gas (N₂) or rocky phosphate ore. The fertilizer industry acts like a **molecular chef**:

- It **cracks** the inert triple bond of N₂ (analogous to chewing a nut to release its protein).
- It **grinds and acidulates** insoluble phosphate rock to release the locked-up phosphorus (analogous to cooking vegetables to free their iron content).
- It **crystallises and prills** the final salts into uniform granules that can be spread mechanically on a field (analogous to packaging tablets in a pharma plant).

The **by-products** of this "cooking" — analogous to kitchen exhaust and food waste — are gaseous oxides of nitrogen, ammonia slip, fluoride dust, and phosphogypsum slurry. These are precisely the streams the **environmental instrumentation engineer** must sense, quantify, and treat.

## 1.3 Industry Classification (KTU Reference Table)

| Class | Primary Nutrient | Typical Products | Key Raw Material |
|-------|------------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Nitrogenous** | N | Urea, Ammonium Nitrate (AN), Ammonium Sulphate (AS), Calcium Ammonium Nitrate (CAN), Anhydrous Ammonia | Natural gas, Naphtha, Coal |
| **Phosphatic** | P | Single Super Phosphate (SSP), Triple Super Phosphate (TSP), Di-Ammonium Phosphate (DAP) | Phosphate rock, Sulfuric acid |
| **Potassic** | K | Muriate of Potash (MOP), Sulphate of Potash (SOP) | Sylvite, Carnallite ores |
| **Complex / Mixed** | N + P + K | NPK 17-17-17, 10-26-26, 12-32-16, 15-15-15 | All three nutrients combined |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Statistic (Global, Reference Year 2023):**
> Global fertilizer consumption is approximately **190 million tonnes** of N + P₂O₅ + K₂O. India is the **2nd largest consumer** and **3rd largest producer** of fertilizers globally. **Urea alone accounts for ~55% of total N consumption** in India — making urea plants the most environmentally sensitive installations.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Environmental Instrumentation

A modern fertilizer complex typically integrates **ammonia, urea, nitric acid, and NPK plants** under one fence line. Each sub-plant carries its own signature pollution profile — and hence its own sensor suite. The instrumentation engineer must therefore understand the **process first**, and then map the right transducer to the right emission point. This module equips the student to do exactly that.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Architecture of an Integrated Fertilizer Complex

A modern integrated fertilizer complex typically comprises the following **interlinked sub-units**:

1. **Ammonia Plant** — Haber-Bosch synthesis of NH₃ from N₂ + H₂.
2. **Urea Plant** — Bosch-Meiser process: 2 NH₃ + CO₂ → NH₂CONH₂ + H₂O.
3. **Nitric Acid Plant** — Ostwald process: NH₃ → NO → NO₂ → HNO₃.
4. **Ammonium Nitrate Plant** — Neutraliser: NH₃ + HNO₃ → NH₄NO₃.
5. **Sulfuric Acid Plant** — Contact process: S → SO₂ → SO₃ → H₂SO₄.
6. **Phosphoric Acid Plant** — Wet process: Ca₃(PO₄)₂ + 3H₂SO₄ → 2H₃PO₄ + 3CaSO₄.
7. **NPK Granulation Plant** — Slurry / steam granulation of N+P+K melt.

## 2.2 Core Process Reactions (Step-by-Step Logic)

### Step A — Primary Reforming (Ammonia Plant Front-End)
Natural gas (CH₄) is steam-reformed in nickel-catalysed primary and secondary reformers.

$$
CH_4 + H_2O \xrightarrow{750-820\,°C,\, Ni} CO + 3H_2 \quad \Delta H = +206\,kJ/mol
$$

$$
CH_4 + 2H_2O \xrightarrow{1100-1150\,°C,\, Ni} CO_2 + 4H_2 \quad \Delta H = +165\,kJ/mol
$$

> **Logic:** Highly endothermic — requires fired-heater duty input. **Why?** Breaking the C–H bond of CH₄ and forming H–H bonds of H₂ requires sustained high energy flux.

### Step B — Shift Conversion
CO is reacted with steam over an iron-chrome catalyst to produce additional H₂ and CO₂.

$$
CO + H_2O \xrightarrow{400-450\,°C,\, Fe_2O_3/Cr_2O_3} CO_2 + H_2 \quad \Delta H = -41\,kJ/mol
$$

### Step C — CO₂ Removal (Acid Gas Scrubbing)
CO₂ is absorbed in **Benfield (hot K₂CO₃)** or **MDEA** solution; this CO₂ is the feedstock for the **downstream urea plant** — making ammonia and urea plants inherently coupled.

### Step D — Methanation (Final Gas Purification)
Trace CO and CO₂ are converted back to CH₄ to avoid catalyst poisoning downstream.

$$
CO + 3H_2 \xrightarrow{Ni,\, 300-400\,°C} CH_4 + H_2O
$$

### Step E — Ammonia Synthesis Loop (Haber-Bosch)
The purified synthesis gas is compressed to **150–300 bar** and passed over a **magnetite (Fe₃O₄) catalyst** with K₂O and Al₂O₃ promoters at **400–500 °C**.

$$
N_2 + 3H_2 \xrightleftharpoons[Fe\,cat.]{450\,°C,\, 200\,bar} 2NH_3 \quad \Delta H = -92.4\,kJ/mol
$$

> **Logic:** Reversible, exothermic. Lower temperature favours NH₃ by Le Chatelier, but kinetics demand ≥400 °C. The compromise temperature (~450 °C) and very high pressure are essential engineering trade-offs.

### Step F — Urea Synthesis (Total Recycle / Stamicarbon Process)
Liquid NH₃ and gaseous CO₂ react at **140–200 bar** and **180–200 °C** in a **synthesis tower (reactor)**.

$$
2NH_3 + CO_2 \rightarrow NH_2COONH_4 \text{ (ammonium carbamate)}
$$

$$
NH_2COONH_4 \rightarrow NH_2CONH_2 + H_2O \text{ (carbamate dehydration)}
$$

**Overall:**
$$
2NH_3 + CO_2 \rightarrow NH_2CONH_2 + H_2O
$$

> **Key Engineering Insight:** Only **60–70%** of the carbamate converts to urea in a single pass. The unconverted carbamate is decomposed, separated, and **recycled** back — hence the name *Total Recycle* process.

### Step G — Nitric Acid Synthesis (Ostwald)
Ammonia is catalytically oxidised over **Pt–Rh gauze** at **900 °C** and **1–10 bar**.

$$
4NH_3 + 5O_2 \xrightarrow{Pt-Rh,\, 900\,°C} 4NO + 6H_2O \quad \Delta H = -905\,kJ/mol
$$

$$
2NO + O_2 \rightarrow 2NO_2 \quad \Delta H = -114\,kJ/mol
$$

$$
3NO_2 + H_2O \rightarrow 2HNO_3 + NO
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Environmental Hotspot:** The **NOx tail gas** (typically 200–500 ppm NO + NO₂) from the acid absorber is the single largest point source of acid-rain precursors in the fertilizer industry. Modern plants install **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** with NH₃ injection to bring this below **50 ppm**.

## 2.3 Source-Specific Pollutant Matrix

| Sub-Plant | Primary Air Pollutants | Primary Water Pollutants | Solid/Hazardous Waste |
|-----------|----------------------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| Ammonia | CO₂ slip, NH₃ slip, CH₄ vent | Process condensate (NH₃, urea) | Spent catalyst, molecular sieves |
| Urea | NH₃, urea dust | Process condensate | Carbamate decomposition vents |
| Nitric Acid | NOx (NO, NO₂), N₂O | Acidic blowdown | Pt-Rh gauze fines |
| Sulfuric Acid | SO₂, SO₃ mist, acid mist | Acidic effluent | Spent V₂O₅ catalyst |
| Phosphoric Acid | Fluorides (HF, SiF₄), dust | Gypsum slurry, F⁻, P₂O₅ | **Phosphogypsum (Pg)** – 5 t / t P₂O₅ |
| NPK Granulation | NH₃, HNO₃ mist, dust | Process wash water | Off-spec granules |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Code)

| # | Formula / Relationship | Engineering Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|-----------------------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $N_2 + 3H_2 \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3$ | Haber-Bosch stoichiometry | kmol |
| 2 | $X_{NH_3} = \dfrac{P_{NH_3}}{P_{tot}} = \dfrac{2y}{1+y}$ | Equilibrium NH₃ mole fraction in synthesis loop (y = extent) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $K_p = \dfrac{P^2_{NH_3}}{P_{N_2} \cdot P^3_{H_2}}$ | Equilibrium constant of ammonia synthesis | bar⁻² |
| 4 | $K_p = 0.0066 \cdot \exp\!\left[\dfrac{4740}{T}\right]$ | Empirical KTU/standard textbook Kp (T in K) | bar⁻² |
| 5 | $\eta_{conv} = \dfrac{\text{Actual NH}_3 \text{ produced}}{\text{Stoichiometric NH}_3 \text{ possible}} \times 100$ | Per-pass conversion efficiency | % |
| 6 | $S = \dfrac{H_2O}{CO_2} \text{ (molar, shift reactor feed)}$ | Steam-to-carbon ratio (typical 3.0–3.5) | mol/mol |
| 7 | $\text{NPK grade} = \%N + \%P_2O_5 + \%K_2O$ | Nutrient declaration (FAI India) | % (w/w) |
| 8 | $\text{F}_{ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Actual F emitted}}{\text{Theoretical F released}}$ | Fluoride emission index (kg F / t P₂O₅) | kg/t |
| 9 | $\eta_{DeNOx} = \dfrac{[NOx]_{in} - [NOx]_{out}}{[NOx]_{in}} \times 100$ | SCR/SNCR removal efficiency | % |
| 10 | $CF = \dfrac{C_{measured}}{C_{limit}}$ | Compliance factor for stack monitoring | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\text{CEP} = \dfrac{\text{kg pollutant}}{\text{tonne product}}$ | Compound Emission Parameter (kg/t product) | kg/t |
| 12 | $Q_s = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2g \cdot h}$ | Stack volumetric flow (orifice-type) | Nm³/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Table Syntax Rule:** All vertical bars in mathematical expressions (e.g., $\vert \Delta H \vert$, $\vert K_p \vert$) MUST be rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` — never as a raw `|` pipe — to prevent the markdown table parser from breaking the column structure. Examiners will deduct marks for poorly formatted numerical tables.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Utility of These Equations |
|--------------------|----------------------------|
| **Plant Design** | Sizing the ammonia converter, urea reactor, prilling tower |
| **Environmental Audit** | Calculating CEP (Compound Emission Parameter) for CPCB/SPCB returns |
| **Online Monitoring** | Validating CEMS (Continuous Emission Monitoring System) data using Kp vs temperature |
| **Energy Optimisation** | Steam-to-carbon ratio (S) tuning to minimise primary reformer fuel |
| **Process Control** | Trim control of H₂:N₂ ratio at 3:1 for maximum Haber yield |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equilibrium Composition of Haber-Bosch Synthesis

### Problem Setup
A stoichiometric mixture of N₂ and H₂ (1:3 molar) is fed to a Haber-Bosch reactor operating at $T = 450\,°C = 723\,K$ and $P = 200\,bar$. Let $\xi$ be the extent of reaction (in kmol) per kmol of N₂ fed. Find the equilibrium mole fraction of NH₃.

### Step 1 — Define Initial Moles

| Species | Initial | Change | Equilibrium |
|---------|---------|--------|-------------|
| N₂ | 1 | $-\xi$ | $1 - \xi$ |
| H₂ | 3 | $-3\xi$ | $3 - 3\xi$ |
| NH₃ | 0 | $+2\xi$ | $2\xi$ |
| **Total** | 4 | $-2\xi$ | $4 - 2\xi$ |

### Step 2 — Express Mole Fractions

$$
y_{N_2} = \frac{1-\xi}{4-2\xi}
$$

$$
y_{H_2} = \frac{3-3\xi}{4-2\xi}
$$

$$
y_{NH_3} = \frac{2\xi}{4-2\xi}
$$

### Step 3 — Express Partial Pressures

Using Dalton's law: $P_i = y_i \cdot P_{tot}$

$$
P_{N_2} = \frac{1-\xi}{4-2\xi} \cdot P
$$

$$
P_{H_2} = \frac{3(1-\xi)}{4-2\xi} \cdot P
$$

$$
P_{NH_3} = \frac{2\xi}{4-2\xi} \cdot P
$$

### Step 4 — Write the Equilibrium Constant Expression

$$
K_p = \frac{P^2_{NH_3}}{P_{N_2} \cdot P^3_{H_2}}
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute the Partial Pressures

$$
K_p = \frac{\left(\dfrac{2\xi}{4-2\xi}\,P\right)^{2}}{\left(\dfrac{1-\xi}{4-2\xi}\,P\right)\cdot \left(\dfrac{3(1-\xi)}{4-2\xi}\,P\right)^{3}}
$$

### Step 6 — Algebraic Simplification

$$
K_p = \frac{4\xi^2 \cdot P^2 \cdot (4-2\xi)^4}{(1-\xi) \cdot 27(1-\xi)^3 \cdot P^4 \cdot (4-2\xi)^2}
$$

$$
K_p = \frac{4\xi^2 \cdot (4-2\xi)^2}{27(1-\xi)^4 \cdot P^2}
$$

### Step 7 — Final Compact Form

$$
K_p \cdot P^2 = \frac{4\xi^2(4-2\xi)^2}{27(1-\xi)^4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\xi_{eq} = f(T,\, P,\, K_p(T))\,}
$$

### Step 8 — Numerical Evaluation at 723 K, 200 bar

At 723 K, the equilibrium constant from the empirical correlation:

$$
K_p = 0.0066 \cdot \exp\!\left[\frac{4740}{723}\right] = 0.0066 \cdot e^{6.555} = 0.0066 \times 701.0
$$

$$
K_p \approx 4.626 \;\; \text{bar}^{-2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
K_p \cdot P^2 = 4.626 \times (200)^2 = 4.626 \times 40000 = 1.85 \times 10^{5}
$$

For the simplified low-conversion case $\xi \ll 1$, the denominator $(1-\xi)^4 \approx 1$ and $(4-2\xi) \approx 4$:

$$
1.85 \times 10^{5} \approx \frac{4\xi^2 \cdot 16}{27}
$$

$$
\xi^2 \approx \frac{1.85 \times 10^{5} \times 27}{64} \approx 7.81 \times 10^{4}
$$

$$
\xi \approx 279 \;\; \text{(unphysical — too high!)}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This unphysical value proves the **approximation** $\xi \ll 1$ is invalid. The correct numerical solution requires iteration. The **exact per-pass conversion** of an industrial Haber-Bosch reactor at 200 bar and 450 °C is typically **15–22%** per pass — the rest is recycled.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Per-Pass Conversion Efficiency of a Urea Reactor

### Step 1 — Stoichiometric Baseline
Maximum possible urea from 2 kmol NH₃ + 1 kmol CO₂ is **1 kmol urea**.

### Step 2 — Actual Production
An industrial total-recycle reactor yields $n_{urea}$ kmol of urea per cycle.

### Step 3 — Define Efficiency

$$
\eta_{urea} = \frac{n_{urea,actual}}{n_{urea,stoich}} \times 100\,\%
$$

### Step 4 — Typical Plant Values

| Parameter | Stochiometric | Total-Recycle Plant |
|-----------|---------------|---------------------|
| NH₃ fed | 2.0 kmol | 2.0 kmol |
| CO₂ fed | 1.0 kmol | 1.0 kmol |
| Urea produced | 1.0 kmol | 0.65 kmol |
| **η** | 100% | **65%** |

### Step 5 — Mass Balance Check (Why < 100%?)

$$
\eta_{carbamate} = \frac{\text{carbamate that dehydrates to urea}}{\text{total carbamate formed}} \times 100\,\%
$$

> **Engineering Reality:** Only **60–70%** of the carbamate dehydrates per pass; the rest is recycled. Modern **CO₂-stripping** designs (Stamicarbon) push this to **75%** per pass.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Compound Emission Parameter (CEP) for a Urea Prill Tower

### Step 1 — Definition

$$
CEP = \frac{\text{kg of pollutant emitted}}{\text{tonne of product}}
$$

### Step 2 — Urea Dust Emission

A prilling tower emits 0.5 kg urea dust per tonne of product. For a 1500 TPD plant:

$$
\text{Daily emission} = 0.5 \times 1500 = 750 \;\text{kg/day} = 0.75 \;\text{t/day}
$$

### Step 3 — Compliance Factor (CF)

If CPCB limit is 1.0 kg/t product:

$$
CF = \frac{0.5}{1.0} = 0.5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Compliant (CF < 1)}
$$

> **Engineering Take-away:** A CF > 1 means **non-compliance** and triggers Section 25 of the Water Act / Section 21 of the Air Act enforcement.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Instrumentation Mapping

| Process Variable | Sensor Type | Typical Range | Installation Point | Purpose |
|------------------|-------------|---------------|--------------------|---------|
| NH₃ slip in absorber | **NDIR (Non-Dispersive IR)** | 0–500 ppm | Absorber tail gas | Process + emission |
| NOx in stack | **Chemiluminescence** | 0–1000 ppm | Acid plant stack | CEMS (CPCB) |
| SO₂ in stack | **UV absorption** | 0–2000 ppm | Sulfuric acid stack | CEMS (CPCB) |
| F⁻ in effluent | **Ion-Selective Electrode (ISE)** | 0.1–10 ppm | Phosphoric acid blowdown | Pollution control |
| pH in cooling water | **Glass electrode** | 0–14 pH | Cooling tower basin | Corrosion control |
| Conductivity in DM water | **4-electrode conductivity** | 0.01–100 µS/cm | Boiler feed | Quality control |
| Stack dust (PM) | **TEOM / Beta attenuation** | 0–100 mg/Nm³ | Calciner stack | Particulate CEMS |
| Level in prill bucket | **Radar / Capacitance** | 0–100% | Prilling tower | Inventory |
| Flow of slurry | **Coriolis / Magnetic** | 0–200 m³/h | NPK slurry line | Mass balance |
| HF in vent | **Electrochemical sensor** | 0–50 ppm | HF scrubber outlet | Worker safety |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Integrated Fertilizer Complex with Instrumentation Hooks

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Natural Gas Feed] --> B1[Primary Reformer]
    B1 --> B2[Secondary Reformer]
    B2 --> C1[Shift Converter]
    C1 --> D1[CO2 Removal Absorber]
    D1 --> E1[Methanator]
    E1 --> F1[NH3 Synthesis Loop]
    F1 --> G1[NH3 Storage]
    F1 --> F2[Urea Plant Reactor]
    F2 --> F3[Prilling Tower]
    G1 --> H1[Nitric Acid Plant]
    H1 --> I1[AN CAN Plant]
    J1[Sulfur Furnace] --> J2[Sulfuric Acid Plant]
    K1[Phosphate Rock] --> K2[Phosphoric Acid Plant]
    F2 --> L1[NPK Granulation]
    I1 --> L1
    J2 --> K2
    K2 --> L1
    L1 --> M1[Finished Fertilizer Storage]

    %% Instrument tags
    B1 -.TIC 101.- B1
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define NPK grade of a fertilizer. A complex fertilizer is labelled 17-17-17. What is the total plant nutrient percentage and identify the form in which P and K are reported.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** NPK grade is the weight-percent declaration of the three primary macronutrients — Nitrogen (as elemental N), Phosphorus (as P₂O₅ equivalent), and Potassium (as K₂O equivalent) — in a fertilizer product, as per Fertiliser Association of India (FAI) standards.
- **Total Nutrient % (1 mark):** Total = 17% N + 17% P₂O₅ + 17% K₂O = **51%** plant nutrients; the rest (49%) is filler/granulation aid.
- **Reporting Convention (1 mark):** P is reported as **P₂O₅ equivalent** (not as elemental P); K is reported as **K₂O equivalent** (not as elemental K). This convention is mandatory under FCO 1985.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any three major air pollutants emitted from a nitric acid plant and the corresponding continuous emission monitoring instrument used to measure each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **NO and NO₂ (collectively NOx)** (1 mark) → Measured by **Chemiluminescence Analyser** (1 mark).
- **N₂O (nitrous oxide)** (1/2 mark) → Measured by **NDIR or FTIR** (1/2 mark).
- **NH₃ slip (acid mist)** (1/2 mark) → Measured by **NDIR** (1/2 mark).

> Optional extras for completeness: **Acid mist (HNO₃ droplet)** measured by **differential optical absorption (DOAS)**; **CO** in reformer section by **NDIR**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam – July 2024]
**(a)** With a neat process flow diagram, describe the **manufacture of ammonia by the Haber-Bosch process**. State the operating conditions of the synthesis converter. (7 marks)
**(b)** Discuss the **major sources of air and water pollution** in an integrated fertilizer complex. What is the role of **CEMS** in regulatory compliance? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks
**Process Flow Description (4 marks):**
1. **Feed Desulfurisation (0.5 mark):** Natural gas is passed over a Co-Mo or ZnO bed to remove H₂S to <0.1 ppm; otherwise it poisons the Ni reformer catalyst downstream.
2. **Primary Reforming (0.5 mark):** CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂ at 750–820 °C, 30–35 bar, over Ni/Al₂O₃ catalyst in fired-heater tubes.
3. **Secondary Reforming (0.5 mark):** Air is injected at 1100–1150 °C to supply the stoichiometric N₂ for downstream NH₃ synthesis. CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O and partial oxidation of residual CH₄.
4. **Shift Conversion (0.5 mark):** Two-stage (HTS at 400 °C + LTS at 200 °C) converts CO to CO₂ + H₂ over Fe-Cr and Cu-Zn-Al oxide catalysts.
5. **CO₂ Removal (0.5 mark):** Hot K₂CO₃ (Benfield) or MDEA absorption; recovered CO₂ is sent to the urea plant.
6. **Methanation (0.5 mark):** Final cleanup of trace CO/CO₂ over Ni catalyst to <10 ppm — essential to prevent ammonia synthesis catalyst poisoning.
7. **Compression and Synthesis Loop (0.5 mark):** Centrifugal compressor raises syngas to 150–300 bar; it is mixed with recycle gas (H₂:N₂ ≈ 3:1) and fed to the converter. Ammonia is condensed by refrigeration; unreacted gas is recycled.

**Operating Conditions of the Synthesis Converter (2 marks):**

$$
\boxed{\; T = 400-500\,°C \quad | \quad P = 150-300\,bar \quad | \quad \text{Catalyst: magnetite } Fe_3O_4 + K_2O + Al_2O_3 \quad | \quad \text{Per-pass conversion: 15-22%} \;}
$$

**Why these conditions (1 mark):**
- **High pressure** shifts equilibrium to the right (Le Chatelier — fewer gas moles on the product side).
- **Moderate temperature** is a kinetic-vs-thermodynamic compromise.
- **Iron catalyst** with K₂O electronic promoter and Al₂O₃ structural promoter is the industry standard since Mittasch (1909).

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks
**Air Pollution Sources (3 marks):**

| Sub-Plant | Pollutant | Quantity | Standard |
|-----------|-----------|----------|----------|
| Nitric Acid | NOx | 1–3 kg/t HNO₃ | <200 ppm after SCR |
| Sulfuric Acid | SO₂ | <4 kg/t H₂SO₄ | <2 kg/t (CPCB) |
| Urea prilling | NH₃, urea dust | 0.5 kg NH₃/t | <50 mg/Nm³ |
| Phosphoric Acid | HF, SiF₄ | 0.05–0.2 kg F/t P₂O₅ | <5 mg/Nm³ HF |
| Ammonia | CO₂ vent, NH₃ slip | — | Stack monitoring |

**Water Pollution Sources (2 marks):**
- Process condensate (NH₃, urea, methanol) from ammonia/urea plants.
- Gypsum slurry from phosphoric acid (5 t gypsum / t P₂O₅).
- Acidic blowdown from sulfuric/nitric acid plants.
- Fluoride-bearing cooling tower blowdown.

**Role of CEMS (2 marks):**
- **Continuous Emission Monitoring System (CEMS)** provides **real-time, online** measurement of stack parameters (NOx, SO₂, PM, HF, NH₃, flow, O₂).
- Data is **continuously telemetered to CPCB/SPCB servers** (one data point every 15 minutes, 96% uptime mandated).
- Enables **automatic regulatory compliance verification**, **plant process optimisation**, and **public transparency** (CPCB dashboard).
- Non-functioning CEMS attracts **Rs 1 lakh/day environmental damage compensation** under Section 31-A of the Air Act.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**(a)** Describe with a flow diagram the **process of urea manufacture by the total recycle method**. What are the major environmental concerns of a urea plant? (7 marks)
**(b)** Explain the **principle, working, and application of the Continuous Stack Monitoring System** used in fertilizer industries. List the typical sensors used. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks
**Process Description (4 marks):**
1. **Synthesis Section (1.5 marks):** Liquid NH₃ and gaseous CO₂ are fed in 2:1 (molar) excess NH₃ ratio into the **urea reactor** at 140–200 bar and 180–200 °C. Reactions:
   - $2NH_3 + CO_2 \rightarrow NH_2COONH_4$ (fast, exothermic)
   - $NH_2COONH_4 \rightarrow NH_2CONH_2 + H_2O$ (slow, endothermic, rate-determining)
2. **Carbamate Decomposition / Recycling (1 mark):** The reactor effluent (~65% conversion) passes to a **stripper** where CO₂ is recovered (CO₂-stripping) or to a **decomposer** where heat reduces pressure; the released NH₃ and CO₂ are absorbed in the **high-pressure absorber** using carbamate condensate as absorbent, and recycled to the reactor.
3. **Urea Concentration and Prilling (1 mark):** The ~70–75% urea solution is concentrated in **two-stage vacuum evaporators** to 99.7% melt, then sprayed from a **prilling bucket** at 30 m height; droplets solidify in the air into spherical prills (1–2.8 mm). Modern plants use **fluid-bed granulation** (less dust, larger granules).
4. **Storage and Bagging (0.5 mark):** Prills are cooled, screened, and bagged in 45-kg HDPE bags or 1-tonne bulk bags.

**Environmental Concerns (3 marks):**
- **NH₃ emissions** from prill tower, granulator, and vacuum evaporator vents (B.L. <50 mg/Nm³).
- **Urea dust** (respirable, causes irritation; B.L. <30 mg/Nm³).
- **Process condensate** containing NH₃, urea, methanol → biotreated or hydrolyzed.
- **CO₂ emissions** (urea plant is actually a net CO₂ consumer; positive aspect).
- **Biuret formation** (>1% reduces agronomic quality; product spec ≤1.0%).

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks
**Principle (1.5 marks):** A CEMS continuously samples and analyses the flue gas stream in-situ or extractively, computes pollutant concentrations, and transmits the data to a regulatory server. **Working (3.5 marks):**
1. **Sampling Probe** — stainless-steel heated probe (180 °C) extracts representative gas from the stack.
2. **Sample Conditioning** — removes moisture, particulates; maintains gas at constant T to avoid condensation losses of soluble gases (SO₃, HCl).
3. **Analyser Module** — measures specific pollutants (NDIR for SO₂/CO/CO₂; UV for NOx; chemiluminescence for low-range NOx; FID for THC; FTIR for HF).
4. **Particulate CEMS** — measures dust via **beta attenuation** (absorption of β-rays) or **TEOM** (Tapered Element Oscillating Microbalance).
5. **Flow and O₂ Measurement** — **Pitot tube** for velocity and **paramagnetic or zirconia** cell for O₂ (used for O₂ correction to standard conditions).
6. **Data Acquisition & Telemetry** — DAS computes 15-min averages, calculates **mass emission rate** in kg/h, and pushes to **CPCB server via GPRS/ISP** every 15 minutes.

**Sensors Used (2 marks):**

| Pollutant | Sensor | Range |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| NOx | Chemiluminescence | 0–500 ppm |
| SO₂ | NDIR / UV | 0–1000 ppm |
| CO | NDIR | 0–500 ppm |
| CO₂ | NDIR | 0–20% |
| HF | Electrochemical / FTIR | 0–50 ppm |
| NH₃ | NDIR / TDLAS | 0–100 ppm |
| PM | Beta attenuation / TEOM | 0–200 mg/Nm³ |
| Flow | S-type Pitot + dP | 0–30 m/s |
| O₂ | Zirconia / Paramagnetic | 0–25% |

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks (Common Pitfalls)**
> 1. **Confusing "P" with "P₂O₅" and "K" with "K₂O"** — A 17-17-17 fertilizer does **not** mean 17% elemental P; it means 17% **P₂O₅ equivalent**. Converting: %P = %P₂O₅ × 0.4364. (Loss: 1–2 marks per question).
> 2. **Reporting temperatures as °F or without units** — Always state °C or K; the Kp formula requires **T in Kelvin** only. (Loss: 0.5–1 mark).
> 3. **Missing the "Why" behind operating conditions** — A 14-mark question on ammonia synthesis **demands** a sentence on Le Chatelier / kinetic compromise; just listing 200 bar and 450 °C is incomplete. (Loss: 2–3 marks).
> 4. **Skipping the mass balance closure** — In any derivation, never end without checking that inputs = outputs. (Loss: 1 mark).
> 5. **CEMS block diagram without telemetry** — A "CEMS" answer that omits the **CPCB server push** is considered incomplete for Environmental Instrumentation. (Loss: 1–2 marks).
> 6. **Writing "ammonia plant" when meaning "urea plant"** — Be specific; the two have very different pollution profiles. (Loss: 0.5 mark per occurrence).
> 7. **In diagrams, not labelling streams** — A flow diagram without inlet/outlet labels and conditions loses 1–2 marks. (Loss: 1–2 marks).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

### **A. Core Concepts to Remember (1-liners)**
- **NPK grade** = %N + %P₂O₅ + %K₂O (FAI convention).
- **Haber-Bosch:** N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ at **200 bar, 450 °C, Fe catalyst**; **15–22% per pass**.
- **Ostwald:** 4NH₃ + 5O₂ → 4NO + 6H₂O on **Pt-Rh gauze at 900 °C**.
- **Urea total recycle:** 2NH₃ + CO₂ → NH₂CONH₂ + H₂O at **140–200 bar, 180–200 °C**.
- **Wet-process phosphoric acid:** Ca₃(PO₄)₂ + 3H₂SO₄ → 2H₃PO₄ + 3CaSO₄↓ (gypsum).
- **Per-pass conversion** of industrial Haber reactor: 15–22%; rest recycled.
- **Per-pass conversion** of industrial urea reactor: 60–70% (Stamicarbon CO₂-stripping pushes to ~75%).
- **Phosphogypsum** generation: **5 tonnes per tonne of P₂O₅** produced.
- **CEMS uptime** requirement: ≥ **96%** annually (CPCB).
- **Stack monitoring** frequency: data point every **15 minutes**, 96 data points/day.

### **B. Hot-Spot Pollutants (Memorise for 1-Mark Side-Headings)**
- **Nitric acid plant → NOx, N₂O.**
- **Sulfuric acid plant → SO₂, acid mist.**
- **Urea plant → NH₃ slip, urea dust.**
- **Phosphoric acid plant → HF, SiF₄, F⁻ in effluent, phosphogypsum.**
- **Ammonia plant → CO₂ vent, NH₃ slip, CH₄ vent, process condensate.**

### **C. Sensor–Pollutant Pairing (Quick-Recall Table)**

| Pollutant | Sensor | Method |
|-----------|--------|--------|
| NH₃ | NDIR / TDLAS | IR absorption at 10.4 µm |
| NOx | Chemiluminescence | NO + O₃ → NO₂* + hν |
| SO₂ | UV absorption | 280–320 nm |
| CO₂ | NDIR | 4.26 µm |
| HF | Electrochemical / FTIR | Ion-selective |
| PM | Beta attenuation | β-ray absorption |
| F⁻ (in water) | ISE | LaF₃ crystal |
| pH | Glass electrode | Nernst equation |

### **D. Quick Numerical Conversions (Memorise)**
- **%P (elemental) = %P₂O₅ × 0.4364**
- **%K (elemental) = %K₂O × 0.8301**
- **1 tonne of NPK 17-17-17 = 510 kg of active nutrients**
- **Haber-Bosch theoretical energy = 21 GJ/tonne NH₃** (actual modern plant: ~28–32 GJ/t)
- **N₂O global warming potential ≈ 273 × CO₂** (IPCC AR6)

### **E. Regulatory Limits to Remember (CPCB, 2024)**
- **NOx in nitric acid stack:** ≤ **200 ppm** (with SCR); ≤ 400 ppm (without).
- **SO₂ in sulfuric acid stack:** ≤ **2 kg/t H₂SO₄**.
- **HF in phosphoric acid stack:** ≤ **5 mg/Nm³**.
- **NH₃ in urea prill tower:** ≤ **50 mg/Nm³** (new plants).
- **CEMS data availability:** ≥ **96%** per quarter.
